Following Bristol's declaration of a climate emergency in November 2018, The Mayor's Climate Emergency Action Plan set out how Bristol could follow through on the commitments that it made in declaring the emergency and the target of making Bristol carbon neutral by 2030. In this plan the Mayor lays out initial policy and budgetary measures to achieve Bristol's climate goals, including funding for the development of Bristol's One City Climate Strategy, our climate change adaptation and mitigation plan and funding for an enhanced climate change team.  The plan also contained actions such as the training of council staff on climate change and the integration of climate change into decision making.The 2019 plan is now being updated to reflect the strategy and deliver the city council actions under that strategy.

This comprehensive action plan sets out all the actions which Bristol City Council is taking to reduce both its own carbon emissions and those of the city. It follows the same themes and enabling conditions as the One City Climate Strategy.

While the current cost of the Climate Action Plan is only estimated, the City has dedicated $188 million in bond financing to support a down payment toward equitable climate action. plan_comments: In 2020, Essex County Council (ECC) established the Essex Climate Action Commission (ECAC) as an independent advisory body to support Essex on its journey to tackle climate change, reduce greenhouse gas emissions and to build a climate resilient county.  The ECAC has over 30 members. They include local councillors, academics, business people and two members of the Young Essex Assembly. In July 2021, the ECAC published its report, ‘Net Zero: Making Essex Carbon Neutral’. This report is considered an integrated climate plan, addressing mitigation, adaptation and energy-related issues, regarding six core themes: 1) Land Use and Green Infrastructure, 2) Energy; 3) Built Environment; 4) Transport, 5) Waste; and 6) Community Engagement. The recommendations were endorsed by Essex County Council in full in November 2021.  The purpose of the ECAC to May 2025 is to monitor and drive implementation of the recommendations on the race to net zero across greater Essex by: • Advising on priorities for direction in the focus areas of the Special Interest Groups; • Reviewing and evaluating progress towards Net Zero; • Supporting the adoption of climate positive policies and practices at all levels of local authorities in the region; • Facilitating collective action across the county; • Encouraging public engagement with residents, businesses, schools, communities, third sector organisations and other institutions of Essex. Work plans for the ECAC will be set out and agreed by the ECAC for each year of activity. (https://www.essex.gov.uk/sites/default/files/migration_data/files/assets.ctfassets.net/knkzaf64jx5x/19xSQKa5iZ2JYS6qFlZOU2/7d4e0ce73061398733d375f3cf150b6a/ECAC-terms-of-reference.pdf)

plan_comments: The Essex Climate Action Commission (ECAC)’s report ‘Net Zero: Making Essex Carbon Neutral’ sets out recommendations to 2050 and recognises that Essex County Council (ECC) cannot tackle climate and environmental challenges alone. ECC’s Climate Action Plan outlines the immediate actions ECC is taking directly and in concert with partners to drive effective progress against ECAC recommendations. It is structured following ECAC’s report core themes with the addition of a new theme focused on local business-oriented projects. In its first version in 2021 the themes were: 1) Land Use and Green Infrastructure, 2) Energy; 3) Built Environment; 4) Transport, 5) Waste; and 6) Community Engagement; 7) Supporting Our Businesses. However, since original publication, there have been significant changes at regional, national, and international levels which have impacted Essex residents. Therefore, the plan has been reassessed and a second edition of the plan was launched in July 2023. This current version widened up the theme seven ‘Supporting Our Businesses’ to add just transition and green economy, renaming the theme as ‘A Just Transition to Green Economy’.

plan_name: Leeds Climate Adaptation and Resilience Plan (plus 3 appendices)
plan_comments: This document sets out plans to build the city’s resilience and adapt to the impacts of climate change, in line with the government’s National Adaptation Programme and latest UK Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA). This report summarises some of the activity that has taken place to date, along with plans to undertake a council-wide climate risk assessment and further develop the city’s resilience to climate change over the year ahead and beyond.Whilst there is significant work taking place across the council to address the impacts of climate change and robust arrangements in place for dealing with severe weather and planning for potential economic shocks, there is a need to undertake a comprehensive climate risk assessment of all council services to identify any gaps and opportunities to ‘future proof’ and enhance existing policy. Following an initial audit of service plans, the Climate, Energy and Green Spaces team will deliver a series of workshops with council teams, with support from academic partners, to identify climate related risks to individual services, what activity is already taking place to address such risks, and how teams can build further resilience within their own policies and frameworks. This will include a full review of service level business continuity plans. An update will be provided in September 2023

This Climate Action Plan sets out our vision for reducing borough-wide emissions, and how we will work with our staff, suppliers, residents, businesses, schools, statutory partners and government to become a carbon neutral organisation by 2030 and create a carbon neutral borough by 2040. To achieve this, our plan sets out the action that we will take in the following areas:  1. The Council’s operations2. Travel3. Buildings4. Waste5. Energy6. Natural environment7. Influencing others

The 2022 Power Strategic Long-Term Resource Plan (SLTRP) serves as a comprehensive roadmap through 2045 that guides the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power’s (LADWP) Power System in its efforts to supply reliable electricity in an environmentally responsible and cost-effective manner. Since decisions about which resources to procure and deploy can have significant economic and environmental consequences, it is essential for the planning process to be conducted with transparency, active participation, and collaborative dialogue with affected stakeholders and LADWP’s customers.

Developed by: Manchester Climate Change Agency (lead), Manchester City Council, Greater Manchester Combined Authority, Consultants The Manchester Climate Change Framework 2020-25 is the city's high-level strategy for tackling climate change. It sets out how Manchester will 'play its full part in limiting the impacts of climate change', a commitment in the Our Manchester Strategy 2016-25. It has been produced by the Manchester Climate Change Partnership and Agency and sets out: Our aim: Manchester will play its full part in limiting the impacts of climate change and create a healthy, green, socially just city where everyone can thrive.Our objectives and targets:Staying within our carbon budgets Climate adaptation and resilienceHealth and wellbeingInclusive, zero carbon and climate resilient economy Seven areas for action to meet our objectives and targets:Buildings Renewable energyTransport and flyingFoodThe things we buy and throw awayGreen infrastructure and nature-based solutionsSupporting and enabling residents and organisations to act  Why a framework?Manchester has adopted a different approach to most other cities. We don’t have a single plan setting out how we will meet our climate change targets. Our approach is based on every resident and every organisation in the city making and delivering their own commitments and action plans. The Framework provides our overarching structure for everyone in the city to 'plug in' their plans. To help people and organisations we've developed a list of 15 actions we need everyone to take.The Framework is currently undergoing a refresh, expected to be published in September 2022. The refresh is currently in its second round of consultation with Manchester's residents and organisations.

Appendix 1 sets out all of the individual actions resulting from this Action Plan. These are split into actions that will reduce our own direct emissions and those that will enable others to play a full role in decarbonising the city. Appendix 1 can be found here: https://www.manchester.gov.uk/info/500002/council_policies_and_strategies/8225/climate_change_action_plan_2020-25_appendix_1_actions

Manchester has not been reducing its direct emissions by the targeted 13% per year and so we are not currently on track to stay within our carbon budget and are at risk of missing our first milestone: to reduce the city’s direct emissions by 50% by 2025.The purpose of this 2022 Update to the Framework is to:Present the findings of detailed modelling done to identify granular targets for reducing the city’s direct emissions by 50%, to help us stay within our carbon budget.Provide an overview of ongoing work carried out in support of the Framework’s other headline objectives, notably on adaptation to climate change.Recommend specific actions for delivery at local, regional, and national level by government and the wider public sector, private companies and third sector organisations, and communities and individuals, that will support our transition to a zero carbon and climate resilient city.The Update highlights the co-benefits of climate action including potential to improve health and wellbeing of communities, increase inclusivity, productivity, and sustainability of economy.Emphasis on a just transition that ensures all of Manchester’s residents are protected from climate change, that actions do not negatively impact on the most vulnerable, and that the costs of change do not fall unevenly on those that are least able to afford them.It identifies the following key messages:Urgent action is needed to reduce direct emissions from our buildings and ground transport, and to increase renewable energy generation, if Manchester is to stay within its carbon budget.Decisive action is needed to assess the city’s vulnerability to climate change and to ensure we are adapting our infrastructure, buildings, economy, and residents to be resilient to a changing climate.Everyone has a role to play – individuals, organisations, local and national government – and there is a great deal we have the power to achieve locally, if we work collaboratively.The cost of transitioning to a zero carbon, climate resilient city cannot be borne solely by the public purse, so we need to find innovative ways to unlock private finance investment.Tackling the climate crisis brings opportunities to deliver wider strategic ambitions, including improving people’s health, reducing fuel and food poverty, creating new jobs and economic growth, and delivering a greener city for everyone.
Total cost of the plan was not estimated.

In 2022/23, Nottingham's Carbon Neutral Action Plan, contained 248 actions. At the end of June 2022, 54 actions had been completed, with the remaining 194 in progress. The Action Plan is broken down into four main sections: Carbon Reduction Measures; Carbon Removal; Resilience and Adaption; and Ecology and Biodiversity.The action plan is reviewed on an annual basis and each action is assessed quarterly to ensure its delivery is on track. For any actions considered as not on track, these are raised with the operational delivery board for prioritisation and recommended actions to rectify if possible are then passed to the steering strategy board for approval.This Action Plan builds on the Nottingham 2028 Carbon Neutral Charter by setting out high-level objectives and subsequent actions in order to achieve a resilient and carbon neutral Nottingham by 2028. The Carbon Neutral Charter sets out the city's vision for a sustainable Nottingham, considering the co-benefits, trade-offs and synergies of actions.A stakeholder engagement & communication plan was published in 2022 which seeks to engage with the key stakeholder groups in the city. These include businesses and residents, particularly those from minor ethnic backgrounds. This available at the following web address: https://www.nottinghamcity.gov.uk/media/3376918/nottingham-cn28-engagement-strategy-2022.pdfThe full action plan and Carbon Neutral Charter are available at https://www.nottinghamcity.gov.uk/your-council/about-the-council/carbon-neutral-nottingham-2028/why-do-we-need-to-act/  , and the Action Plan is undergoing its third year annual review to ensure it continues to remain valid for each year of delivery towards 2028.
Nottingham's Fuel Poverty action plan is currently being updated to reflect changes in circumstance since it was written.

Authorship of the Reading Climate Emergency Strategy was a collective effort co-ordinated by the Board of the Reading Climate Change Partnership (an independent, multi-agency, cross-sectoral partnership which is hosted by Reading Borough Council). Drafting was undertaken by a combination of volunteer theme leads, a dedicated team of officers within the local authority and paid staff of other partner organisations. This approach has helped secure 'buy-in' from sectors beyond the local authority. Progress in delivering the Strategy is monitored quarterly by the Partnership Board and is formally reported on annually - a link to the most recent annual report (2021/22)  is at: https://readingcan.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/Reading-Climate-Emergency-Strategy-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf

The Reading Climate Change Adaptation Plan is a comprehensive assessment of potential impacts on all key sectors though some are addressed in more detail than others. There is a particular focus on six areas which align with the action plans in the Reading Climate Emergency Strategy, namely: water resources and flood risk; the built environment and energy; health and wellbeing; transport; natural environment and greenspaces; and production, consumption and waste. The Adaptation Plan was commissioned by the Reading Climate Change Partnership and developed by the consultancy Mott Macdonald in consultation with a wide range of stakeholders.

Description: The Climate Emergency Action Plan is mitigation-focused, and contains 31 action recommendations (organized into six “Big Moves”) in line with efforts to limit global warming to 1.5ºC. CEAP sets a target of 50% reduction in community-wide greenhouse gas emissions reduction by 2030. CEAP adopts an existing goal of deriving 100% of energy used in Vancouver from renewable sources before 2050. It expands the 2050 target, by committing to achieving carbon neutrality in our community-wide greenhouse gas emissions before 2050. CEAP supersedes the previous Renewable City Action Plan (2017), and the greenhouse gas related goals in the Greenest City Action Plan (2011).Priority Action Areas(“Big Moves”):1. Complete, Walkable Communities2. Active Transportation and Transit3. Zero Emission Vehicles4. Zero Emission Space and Hot Water Heating5. Low-Carbon Construction Materials6. Carbon SequestrationGovernance: The Climate Emergency Directors Forum provides oversight at a working level, while the Climate Emergency Steering Committee provides oversight at a program level. The Sustainability Group tracks progress and reports to Council annually. Executive reports are also prepared as required. Staff will update CEAP for approval by Council at the halfway mark of implementation (2025).The primary purpose of this plan is to reduce emissions in areas of jurisdiction the City has control or significant influence over. CEAP estimated a cost of approximately $500,000,000 to reach 2030 targets between 2021-2025. Updated modelling have helped the City to measure how a changing policy context provides opportunities to deepen climate action and meet the targets laid out in the plan, including opportunities to ensure that we are meeting our emissions reductions targets.

Description: Among the first of its kind in Canada, the Climate Change Adaptation Strategy is the City’s strategy in addressing climate-related risks and hazards in the community. Its priorities include addressing urban drainage flooding and coastal flooding and inundation, and heat and air quality-related impacts to community and City services and infrastructure. It contains 84 core actions in five core areas, and 17 enabling actions to help integrate an adaptation lens as standard practice into all City work.Core Action Areas:Climate Robust InfrastructureClimate Resilient BuildingsConnected and Prepared CommunitiesCoastal PreparednessHealthy and Vigorous Natural AssetsGovernance: The Adaptation Steering Committee provides oversight to implementation. This committee comprises senior managers, many of whom are also in the Corporate Leadership Team. The Sustainability Group tracks progress and reports to Council annually. Executive reports are also prepared as required. Staff will update the Adaptation Strategy every five years, which includes updating climate information, reviewing the status of adaptation actions, and identifying new or revised actions. The next update will be in 2023.

Description: The Resilient Vancouver Strategy builds City capacity to understand and proactively address current and future shocks and stresses, including those related to climate change. It leverages global and local knowledge to build the resilience of organizational, community, and infrastructure systems, and works in unison with the Climate Change Adaptation Strategy to build resilience to priority shocks and stresses in Vancouver. Objectives aligned with the “Proactive and Collaborative City” priority include strengthening organizational capacity to manage risk and recover from shocks and stresses and advancing collaborative disaster risk reduction and recovery planning. An associated action taking shape currently is to bring various City governance structures that deal with risk together into one disaster-risk reduction group. The group would oversee risk posed by various shocks and stresses, whether climate-related or not.Priority Areas:1. Thriving and Prepared Neighbourhoods2. Proactive and Collaborative City3. Safe and Adaptive Buildings and InfrastructureGovernance: Staff in the Vancouver Emergency Management Agency (VEMA), and staff working on seismic policy in other departments, manage RVS implementation.

Clean Energy DC is the District of Columbia’s energy and climate action plan. It explains how the District will use forward-looking energy policies, while also encouraging innovation, efficiency, and resiliency. Clean Energy DC re-imagines what a 21st century energy system could be but is also pragmatic and focused on achieving tangible goals.Clean Energy DC identifies what actions need to be taken between now and 2032 in our buildings, our energy infrastructure, and our transportation system to meet the District’s ambitious GHG reduction targets. It lists 57 actions that we can do today, next steps for each, and what we will need to do in the future.DOEE has begun the update process for its Clean Energy DC Plan.  Clean Energy DC 2.0 (CEDC 2.0) is the latest effort to develop a cutting-edge climate and energy action plan for the District of Columbia. CEDC 2.0 will serve as an update to Mayor Bowser's first Clean Energy DC Plan, released in 2018.

The Sustainable DC plan was first adopted in 2013 and revised (SDC 2.0) in 2019. The plan outlines goals for 2032 across 13 topic areas, including climate, energy, the built environment, transportation, and waste. The climate section contains goals and actions related to both adaptation and mitigation.  Note:  The SDC 2.0 plan is too large to attach but the main landing page is here: https://sustainable.dc.gov/node/1447351

The District's Climate Ready DC plan is the District’s plan to adapt to a changing climate. Its 77 actions focus on strategies across four sectors: Transportation & Utilities, Buildings & Development, Neighborhoods and Communities, and Governance and Implementation. In July 2023, DOEE launched an effort to update the District's climate projections, a first step in a planned 10-year update to the original plan.

plan_comments: 都は、2016年３月に策定した環境基本計画の下、「世界一の環境先進都市・東京」の実現を目指し、幅広く環境施策に取り組んできた。　計画策定から6年、持続可能な社会の実現に対する関心が世界的に高まる中で、気候危機の一層の深刻化、生物多様性の損失、水・大気環境の変化など、環境を取り巻く状況は世界規模で大きな課題となっている。また、国連の推計によると、2030年までに世界の人口は85億人、2050年には97億人に増加し、世界人口のほとんどの増加は都市地域で発生すると予測されている。さらに、新型コロナウイルス感染症の感染拡大に加え、ウクライナ・ロシア情勢による資源やエネルギーへの影響等、未曽有の危機の最中にある。　欧州を中心に、気候危機への対処を図りながら、コロナ禍からの「より良い復興」を目指す「グリーン・リカバリー」の流れが生まれている。都は、持続可能な生活を実現する観点にまで広げた「サステナブル・リカバリー（持続可能な回復）」を進めている。グリーンかつレジリエントな「ゼロエミッション東京」を実現し、50年、100年先も、自然との共生や質の高い大気環境など、豊かさにあふれる持続可能な都市をつくるため、まさに今、行動を起こさなければならない。　都は、世界の主要都市の一員として、世界の、そして東京の未来を切り拓くため、都の環境施策を更に大胆に加速する、新たな環境基本計画を策定することとした。（P2）In accordance with the Environmental Master Plan formulated in March 2016, the Tokyo Metropolitan Government (TMG) has been working on a wide range of environmental initiatives with the aim of realizing Tokyo as a world-leading, environmentconscious city.While interest in the realization of a sustainable society has increased worldwide in the six years since the formulation of the plan, the environmental situation is posing ever more serious issues on a global scale, such as further aggravated climate crisis, biodiversity loss, and changes in the water environment and air quality. The United Nations estimates that the world population will grow to 8.5 billion by 2030 and 9.7 billion by 2050, with almost all of the growth occurring in urban areas. In addition, we are in the midst of unprecedented crises, including the COVID-19 pandemic and the impact of the conflict between Ukraine and Russia on resources and energy.In European and other countries, there is a movement aiming for a green recovery to “build back better” from the COVID-19 crisis while coping with the climate crisis. TMG is promoting sustainable recovery that expands the perspective to include the realization of sustainable lifestyles. Now is the time to act for TMG to realize a green and sustainable Zero Emission Tokyo and create a sustainable city full of abundance, which will provide environmental symbiosis and an excellent air quality 50 or 100 years from now.As one of the major cities in the world, TMG determined to formulate a new Environmental Master Plan that will more boldly accelerate our environmental initiatives to open up the future of the world and Tokyo. (P2)

plan_name: Tokyo Climate Change Adaptation Plan 東京都気候変動適応計画
plan_url: https://www.kankyo.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/policy_others/zeroemission_tokyo/strategy.files/zero_emission_tokyo_strategy.pdf ゼロエミッション東京戦略2020 Update & Report https://www.kankyo.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/dbook/202210/master_plan/2022-10_tokyo_kankyo/book.pdf  Tokyo Environmental Masterplan https://www.kankyo.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/en/climate/100200a20220607180322567.files/TokyoClimateChangeAdaptationPlan.pdfhttps://www.kankyo.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/climate/hydrogen/tokyo_hydrogen_vision.files/tokyo_hydrogen_vision.pdf  Tokyo Hydrogen Vision
plan_comments: 近年の猛暑や豪雨など、気候変動による深刻な影響は、既に私たちの身近な生活にも及んでいます。こうした状況も踏まえ、都では、2050年の世界のCO2排出量実質ゼロに貢献する、ゼロエミッション東京の実現に向け、令和元年12月に策定した「ゼロエミッション東京戦略」に基づき、具体的な取組を進めています。これらの取組と併せ、都内における気候変動の影響を踏まえた、自然災害、健康、農林水産業など幅広い分野で、都⺠⽣活や⾃然環境への影響被害を可能な限り回避、軽減するため、東京都気候変動適応方針で示した考え方に加え、デジタルトランスフォーメーション（DX）の推進などの視点も取り入れながら、持続可能な回復を目指す「サステナブル・リカバリー」の考え方に立って、「東京都気候変動適応計画」を策定しました。ゼロエミッション東京の実現に向けた取組に加え、本計画に基づく様々な施策を展開することで、気候変動の緩和と適応の両面から総合的に施策を展開し、極端な気象変化から都⺠の⽣命と財産を守る強靭な都市を築いていきます。（P2）Severe impacts of climate change, such as extreme heat and heavy rains in recent years, already affect our daily lives.In light of this situation, TMG is promoting concrete efforts to realize a Zero Emission Tokyo that will contribute to the goal of global net zero CO2emissions by 2050, based on the Zero Emission Tokyo Strategy formulated in December 2019.In conjunction with these efforts, and taking into account the impacts of climate change in Tokyo, we have formulated the Tokyo Climate Change Adaptation Plan in line with the concept of a sustainable recovery, incorporating perspectives, such as the promotion of digital transformation (DX) in addition to the ideas indicated in the Tokyo Climate Change Adaptation Policy. The purpose of this plan is to avoid or reduce as much as possible the impacts on or damage to the lives of Tokyo residents and the natural environment in a broad range of fields, including natural disasters, human health, and agriculture, forestry, and fisheries.By implementing various initiatives based on this plan in addition to efforts to realize a Zero Emission Tokyo, we will comprehensively develop both mitigation and adaptation measures for climate change to build a robust city that protects the lives and property of Tokyo residents from extreme changes in climate.(P2)

plan_name: Second Phase of Greenhouse Gas Regulatory Implementation Plan
plan_comments: Kaohsiung city’s "Second Phase of Greenhouse Gas Regulatory Implementation Plan" consists of energy, industry, residential and commercial, transport, agriculture and environment, planning for total reduction in 2025. The carbon deduction is expected to be approximately 2.17 million tons of CO2e, with a total of 58 indicators(measures).

In a city geared to international standards, Kaohsiung City Government published the first Voluntary Local Review (VLR) in 2021. To respond to the spirit of “leaving no one behind” of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), a comprehensive review with 17 indicators was conducted. The compilation of Kaohsiung City VLR  has officially entered its second year. In response to the international trend, in 2022's report, we were taking sustainable net zero as the theme, and incorporate the four transformation strategies: energy, industry, life, and society of the “General Statement of Taiwan’s 2050 Net Zero Emission Path and Strategies” to echo the net zero strategy of the City. In addition, SDGs policies and indicators are reviewed as guidelines for the City to move towards net zero and becoming an international sustainable city.
Synergies and trade-offs were considered by the project team in the development of this Strategy, however these have not been explicitly addressed in the public document.

plan_comments: The new plan will include the following 7 fields of action: Mobility, buildings (heating etc.), construction activities including Scope 2, economy, energy supply, disposal and negative emissions as well as agriculture and forestry.

With the continuation of the BEK 2030 for the implementation period 2022 to 2026, far-reaching measures were adopted in the areas of climate protection and climate adaptation, which the governemt is to implement in the coming years. In the area of climate protection, the switch to fossil-free energy sources in the electricity and heat supply plays a central role in the field of energy. All available renewable energy potentials in the areas of solar, wind, waste heat, geothermal and bioenergy must be tapped in the best possible way and appropriate infrastructures for storage solutions must be developed. In the buildings sector, the increase of the energetic refurbishment rate in existing buildings, the climate-neutral construction of new buildings and the phase-out of fossil fuels for the supply of buildings are named as central key factors. Important measures here are the development of spatial heat planning and the expansion of advisory services and state funding programmes for building owners. The City of Berlin will also advocate the socially acceptable implementation of renovation obligations in existing buildings at the federal level. In the transport sector, a transofrmation of mobility within the city is key. In the field of passenger mobility, this means the expansion of cycling and walking infrastructures or the qualitative improvement and quantitative expansion of public transport services. The conversion of the municipal vehicle fleet to climate-friendly drive systems is to be exemplary. Climate adaptation was strengthened in this Climate Action Plan's update. Fields of action such as biodiversity and tourism, sport and culture have been newly included in the BEK 2030.

El Plan de Acción Climática contempló la participación de diversos sectores para su implementación, por lo cual, también se tuvieron en cuenta en la Política Pública de Acción Climática próxima a expedirse. En este último instrumento, se identificaron resultados y productos asociados a las temáticas de los sectores, a fin de mejorar su desempeño con un enfoque hacia la acción climática, teniendo en cuenta, además, que en los últimos años el sistema productivo de la ciudad ha cambiado, de acuerdo con la dinámica económica y muchos de los sectores se han visto afectados.Lo anterior también es derivado de la inminencia de la afectación que los riesgos asociados con el cambio climático tendrían sobre actividades de servicios, comercio e industria, así como el impacto que un inadecuado desarrollo de actividades de algunos sectores, como por ejemplo la construcción, podrían tener , con lo cual se distanciaría la adaptación y resiliencia de la ciudad.

La Ciudad de Buenos Aires cuenta con tres Planes de Acción Climática, que de acuerdo a la Ley N° 3871/2011 de Adaptación y Mitigación al Cambio Climático, deben ser actualizados con una periodicidad de cinco años. El Plan de Acción Climática 2050 de la Ciudad de Buenos Aires fue lanzado oficialmente en Abril de 2021, luego de un proceso de validación y aprobación por parte de C40.

Em maio de 2018, foi assinada a Carta de Compromisso da Prefeitura de Curitiba com o relatório Deadline 2020 - Goal 2020 do Grupo C40 de Grandes Cidades para Liderança Climática, referente ao Planejamento de Ação Climática (PAC), para apoiar a elaboração do PlanClima.Ao assumir esse compromisso, a cidade ganhou acesso ao Marco de Planejamento de Ação Climática, que contém os componentes essenciais para o desenvolvimento de um plano de ação climática consistente com as metas do Acordo de Paris, bem como um conjunto de recursos, incluindo orientação técnica, treinamento, ferramentas e compartilhamento de conhecimento por meio de um extenso Programa de Assistência Técnica.A partir do compromisso da cidade com a Meta 2020, iniciou-se o processo de elaboração do PlanClima, que incluiu as seguintes medidas:desenvolvimento da base técnica e documentos de referência; definição de estratégias; priorização de ações com a participação de diversos atores; e elaboração de anteprojeto.

The Istanbul Climate Change Action Plan was prepared/revised in 2021, in line with the commitment to become a "Carbon Neutral and Resistant City by 2050", included in the Deadline 2020 document signed by our Mayor, Mr. Ekrem İmamoğlu, at the C40 Presidents Summit in 2019. There are 21 mitigation and adaptation actions prioritized in line with certain criteria for the expanded ambitious targets included in the action plan. With the vision of fair, green, creative city and happy Istanbul, we continue our climate change strategies that serve sustainable development goals.In addition, as the IMM Climate Change Directorate, our Sustainable Energy and Climate Action Plan (SECAP) will be published by the end of 2023.In this context, based on 2019, the "Institutional Greenhouse Gas Emission Inventory" of Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality was calculated for the first time, and the total greenhouse gas emission amount was determined as "1.543,303 tCO2e".Istanbul SECAP has 3 main objectives:1- Reduction of CO2 emissions by at least 40% by 2030 through reduction (acceleration of the transition to low carbon in the region), renewable energy and energy efficiency measures.2- Adaptation (increasing the capacity to adapt to the inevitable effects of climate change), being prepared for the change that global warming will bring and creating a resilient structure.3-To develop cooperation with other governments in the region for sustainable energy (energy efficiency and renewable energy), access to reliable and affordable energy.From this point of view, a "40% reduction target" has been set for "2030", based on 2019, based on the corporate greenhouse gas emission inventory.IMM has also started Istanbul's Green City Action Plan (GCAP) in May, 2023. In addition, the Waste Management Directorate of our Municipality has the goal of gradually increasing the facility capacities until 2050 within the scope of sustainable waste management.

plan_comments: Rencana Umum Energi Daerah (RUED) Provinsi DKI Jakarta disusun sebagai pelaksanaan dari Undang Undang Republik Indonesia Nomor 30 Tahun 2007 tentang Energi Pasal 18. RUED Provinsi DKI Jakarta disusun untuk merencanakan pemenuhan kebutuhan energi sebagai pendukung pembangunan di Provinsi DKI Jakarta. Visi RUED Provinsi DKI Jakarta adalah: “TERPENUHINYA KEBUTUHAN ENERGI YANG BERWAWASAN LINGKUNGAN DAN TERDEPAN DALAM PEMANFAATAN TEKNOLOGI ENERGI BERSIH DENGAN MENDORONG PERAN SERTA MASYARAKAT” Misi RUED Provinsi DKI Jakarta adalah sebagai berikut: (1) Menjamin ketersediaan energi secara merata dan berkesinambungan. (2) Mengoptimalkan pemanfaatan teknologi energi baru dan terbarukan. (3) Meningkatkan kegiatan konservasi dan diversifikasi energi. (4) Meminimalisasi dampak lingkungan dari sektor energi. (5) Mendorong peran serta masyarakat dalam pemanfaatan energi baru dan terbarukan dan konservasi energi. Tujuan RUED Provinsi DKI Jakarta adalah sebagai berikut: (1) Tersedianya energi secara merata dan berkesinambungan. (2) Pemanfaatan teknologi energi baru dan terbarukan secara optimal. (3) Pemakaian energi yang efisien melalui konservasi energi. (4) Berkurangnya penggunaan BBM melalui diversifikasi energi. (5) Terkendalinya pencemaran lingkungan dari sektor energi. (6) Terwujudnya peran serta masyarakat dalam pemanfaatan energi baru dan terbarukan dan konservasi energi. Kebijakan pengelolaan energi Provinsi DKI Jakarta adalah sebagai berikut: (1) Penyediaan energi yang cukup dan andal. (2) Pemanfaatan energi baru dan terbarukan. (3) Peningkatan konservasi dan diversifikasi energi. (4) Pengurangan dampak lingkungan dari sektor energi. (5) Penyediaan energi untuk Kepulauan Seribu. (6) Peningkatan kemampuan pengelolaan energi.

As a ballpark estimation, in the next 15 to 20 years, the Government will devote HK$240 billion to take forward mitigation and adaptation measures to combat climate change, including energy saving, renewable energy, green transport, waste-to-energy facilities, coastal enhancement and upgrading of drainage works, etc.

El PLCC tiene por objetivo describir cómo la ciudad se adaptará a las amenazas climáticas y reducirá sus emisiones de GEI, teniendo como eje central a las poblaciones más vulnerables bajo un enfoque de inclusión y equidad. Ha sido elaborado por la Municipalidad Metropolitana de Lima, con apoyo valioso del Grupo de Liderazgo Climático C40 Cities a través del Programa de Asistencia Técnica de Planificación Climática ejecutado entre 2019 y 2020. El alcance del plan comprende a nivel geográfico los 43 distritos de Lima Metropolitana, incluyendo sus áreas naturales y sus áreas rurales, lo cual permitirá tener una gestión climática integral de la ciudad, contando con ejes transversales en sectores vitales. El alcance temporal comprende el periodo de tiempo 2021-2030 con una visión al 2050. Para su diseño se ha identificado el estado actual de la ciudad y se han elaborado proyecciones de escenarios de cambio climático al 2030, y dos proyecciones de emisiones de GEI para dos periodos de tiempo adicional, 2040 y 2050. Para su formulación, se realizó un intercambio de conocimientos con expertos de entidades públicas y un proceso participativo con actores sociales claves de la ciudad como niñas y niños, las juventudes, municipalidades distritales, organizaciones sociales y la academia; lo cual enriqueció el proceso de definición de objetivos y acciones. Se ha identificado a las autoridades y actores sociales relevantes que deben de comprometerse para alcanzar las metas de mitigación y los objetivos de resiliencia de la ciudad, de forma sostenible.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: La Estrategia de Acción Climática  considera no solo a la Ciudad de México,  también considera a la Zona Metropolitana del Valle de México
plan_comments: En cumplimiento con la Ley de Mitigación y Adaptación al Cambio Climático y Desarrollo Sustentable para la Ciudad de México (Ley de Cambio Climático), se diseñaron los dos instrumentos que dirigen la política climática en la Ciudad de México, la Estrategia Local de Acción Climática 2021-2050 (ELAC) y el Programa de Acción Climática 2021-2030 (PACCM), en ambos se busca integrar, coordinar e impulsar políticas públicas para disminuir los riesgos ambientales, sociales y económicos derivados del cambio climático, y así encaminar a la ciudad hacia un desarrollo bajo en carbono y resiliente, enmarcado en los enfoques y principios de la economía circular, la inclusión social, los derechos humanos y la equidad de género. La Estrategia, con horizonte temporal 2021 - 2050 tiene como objetivo específico , presentar la visión, objetivos, los ejes y las líneas de acción de la Ciudad de México en materia de cambio climático durante el periodo 2021-2050. Visualiza el futuro de la ciudad en 8 ejes estratégicos y 23 líneas de acción, que se articulan para responder a la emergencia climática actual y construir un futuro cero emisiones y resiliente a los impactos del cambio climático a mediados del siglo .El cumplimiento de los objetivos planteados a largo plazo dependerá de las medidas transformadoras implementadas en el corto plazo mediante el Programa  de Acción Climática de la Ciudad de México, así como de condiciones habilitadoras de tipo jurídico, institucional, técnico y financiero que faciliten la progresividad y viabilidad de las metas a 2050 en cada uno de los ejes que integran la Estrategia.La revisión y actualización de los escenarios, objetivos y líneas de acción de la Estrategia se sustentarán en los resultados de los reportes de seguimiento de las medidas de acción climática que permitan conocer el avance del cumplimiento de las metas, y se llevarán a cabo en los términos y periodicidad señalados en la Ley del Sistema de Planeación del Desarrollo de la Ciudad de México, la Ley de Cambio Climático y su Reglamento.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: El Programa de Acción Climática  considera no solo a la Ciudad de México,  también  a la Zona Metropolitana del Valle de México
plan_comments: En cumplimiento con la Ley de Mitigación y Adaptación al Cambio Climático y Desarrollo Sustentable para la Ciudad de México (Ley de Cambio Climático), se diseñaron los dos instrumentos que dirigen la política climática en la Ciudad de México, la Estrategia Local de Acción Climática 2021-2050 (ELAC) y el Programa de Acción Climática 2021-2030 (PACCM), en ambos se busca integrar, coordinar e impulsar políticas públicas para disminuir los riesgos ambientales, sociales y económicos derivados del cambio climático, y así encaminar a la ciudad hacia un desarrollo bajo en carbono y resiliente, enmarcado en los enfoques y principios de la economía circular, la inclusión social, los derechos humanos y la equidad de género. El objetivo del Programa de Acción Climática es presentar las metas y medidas de la ciudad en materia de cambio climático para el periodo 2021-2030, con metas intermedias que plantean la reducción del 10% de las emisiones de la Ciudad de México y el incremento de la capacidad adaptativa de los ecosistemas, la infraestructura estratégica, las comunidades y sus medios de vida al 2024. Su desarrollo sienta las bases para transitar hacia las metas planteadas en la Estrategia y se acompaña de la puesta en marcha de un Sistema de Seguimiento que permita monitorear sus avances y sus impactos.

El Gobierno de la Ciudad de México ha establecido el “Programa Ambiental y de Cambio Climático de la Ciudad de México 2019-2024”, el cual fue anunciado por la Dra. Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo, Jefa de Gobierno de la Ciudad de México el pasado 5 de junio de 2019, y constituye el mayor esfuerzo que se implementa desde la administración pública con el objetivo claro de mejorar el ambiente y hacer frente al problema del Cambio Climático, a través de metas específicas en torno a siete ejes fundamentales: 1. Revegetación del campo y la ciudad; 2. Rescate de ríos y cuerpos de agua; 3. Manejo Sustentable de Agua; 4. Cero basura; 5. Movilidad Integrada y sustentable; 6. Calidad del Aire; y 7. Ciudad Solar. En este séptimo eje se inscribe el presente Programa. El séptimo eje “Ciudad Solar” tiene el objetivo de promover, en un universo de más de 164 mil hogares, comercios y edificios públicos, la instalación de tecnologías de aprovechamiento de las fuentes renovables de energía y la implementación de medidas de aumento en la eficiencia de consumo de energía, a través de una inversión pública y privada de 13,656 MDP, permitiendo la generación de 10,700 empleos y la mitigación de 2 millones 26 mil 600 toneladas de CO2 durante el período 2019 – 2024.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: Préciser le périmètre hors de Paris (aérien, fret, etc.)
plan_comments: The City of Paris is taking significant steps to combat climate change by reducing its carbon footprint and preparing for adaptation. Their strategy comprises two approaches: "mitigation," involving actions to cut greenhouse gas emissions and atmospheric pollutants, and "adaptation," encompassing measures to enhance resilience to climate hazards and protect residents. The City is committed to fostering an ecologically just transition, with a focus on social inclusion at the core of its Climate Plan and Resilience Strategy.The latest Climate Plan resulted from extensive public consultation between November 2016 and April 2017, and it underwent a climate referendum engaging over 73,000 Parisians, with more than 15,000 becoming "climate volunteers."This third edition of the Climate Plan aligns with the Paris Agreement's core objective, aiming for carbon neutrality by 2050, with intermediate goals set for 2030. Significant reductions in energy needs, 35% by 2030 and 50% by 2050, are crucial for transitioning to a 100% renewable and recovery-based energy supply. The plan also targets increasing the share of renewable energy consumed in Paris from 17% in 2014 to 25% in 2020, 45% in 2030, and 100% in 2050, while phasing out fossil fuels by 2030.In 2018, the Climate Plan focused on reducing resource wastage and energy precarity. Paris considers this plan a societal transition that will generate jobs, drive innovation, and facilitate professional conversions.The Climate Plan has been supported by the "Bleu Climat," an annual activity report tracking progress since 2007. A mid-term assessment for 2018-2020 was affected by the 2020 context, including lockdowns and delayed municipal elections. Some initiatives were slowed down, but others were accelerated, considering residents' heightened awareness of climate emergencies and the need for better living conditions and solidarity. The Climate Plan has achieved almost all major objectives for 2020.Paris conducted an initial study on climate change vulnerabilities and strengths in 2012, leading to the first adaptation strategy in 2015. After a decade, the study was updated to align with IPCC knowledge and tools, exploring three scenarios for Paris' future by 2100 (proactive/intermediate/worst case).https://www.paris.fr/pages/paris-s-adapte-au-changement-climatique-18541

Since its first Climate Action Plan in 2004, San Francisco has been leading the way on local climate action, environmental justice, and launching innovative community programs and outreach campaigns for residents and businesses. Tackling the interwoven climate, equity, and racial justice challenges we face has been the driving force for the development of this Climate Action Plan (CAP). It provides a summary of progress through existing programs, and a detailed list of priority actions that San Francisco can take that will have the greatest potential to reduce emissions, while also having the greatest potential to provide an equitable distribution of benefits. The process of creating the CAP brought City departments, residents, community-based organizations, and businesses together to craft a plan focused on science and equity and grounded in compassion and lived experience. This data-driven, community-based plan outlines a detailed list of strategies and actions to achieve net-zero emissions by 2040, while creating solutions that serve intersectional challenges of racial and social equity, public health, economic recovery, and resilient communities. To advance climate justice, the CAP makes four core commitments:1) Build greater racial and social equity; 2) Protect public health; 3) Increase community resilience; and 4) Foster a more just economy. By integrating these four climate justice commitments, the CAP proposes two ambitious and achievable climate emission reduction targets: 1) An interim target of cutting sector-based emissions 61% below 1990 levels by 2030; and 2) Net-zero sector-based emissions by 2040, a 90% reduction from 1990 levels.

O PlanClimaSP é um plano abrangente e tem como objetivo a implementação de medidas que resultem na neutralidade de emissões até 2050, adaptação aos impactos da mudança do clima e a equidade das consequências da mudança do clima. Dessa forma, os setores acima selecionados estão distribuídos ao longo das 43 ações que compõem o plano.  Alguns exemplos são as ações 43 (fomentar estratégias de agricultura urbana orgânica), para o setor de agricultura; 34 (expandir o Programa Ambientes Verdes e Saudáveis (PAVS) para todas as unidades básicas de saúde (UBS), ampliando a incorporação das questões da mudança do clima), relacionado à saúde humana e serviço social; 41 (Promover e aprofundar a temática da mudança do clima nas ações da Política Municipal de Educação Ambiental, fortalecendo sua implantação e ampliando os públicos-alvo), que contribui para a educação, a informação e a comunicação; 2 (elaborar estudos sobre padrões de consumo energético no Município de São Paulo, para a adoção de medidas gerais de eficiência energética), que se enquadra em atividades profissionais, científicas e técnicas; e 21 (Incrementar o provimento habitacional para população de baixa renda), relacionada à construções, atividades imobiliárias e atividades administrativas de serviços e apoio.Referente à mineração e extração, a temática está relacionada ao processo de eletrificação de veículos, presente na ação 9 (promover a substituição gradativa das frotas de ônibus municipais para veículos zero emissões). As atividades de saneamento, gestão de águas residuais e recuperação, bem como a preservação e o abastecimento de água, são setores presentes na ação 39 (proteger e requalificar nascentes e cursos d’água). Enquanto isso, o setor fornecimento de eletricidade, gás, vapor e ar condicionado está presente nas ações 1, 2 e 3, relacionadas à eficiência energética.As atividades de hospedagem e alimentação podem ser exemplificadas na ação 35 (combater o desperdício de alimentos e aumentar a segurança alimentar em todo o Município), enquanto a administração pública de defesa e segurança está exemplificada na ação 29 (fortalecer a governança do Sistema Municipal de Defesa Civil para uma gestão intersetorial e transversal da redução de risco e de desastres.). Por fim, o manejo de resíduos estão relacionados à ação 16 (maximizar os processos de compostagem).

plan_name: Taipei City Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan (Phase 1)
plan_comments: In order to build a livable and sustainable city and to address the climate warming hazards caused by the greenhouse effect, Taipei City conducted a city-level greenhouse gas inventory on its own. The "Taipei City Energy Saving and Carbon Reduction Program" has been promoted in 2008, and in 2016, the "Taipei City Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan" was formulated to strengthen the division of work among various competent authorities to jointly assume responsibility for greenhouse gas reduction in the City. In accordance with international climate agreements and the Greenhouse Gas Reduction and Management Act, the City formulated the "Taipei City Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan" in 2019.The City's Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan (Phase 1) was promoted from 2018 to 2020, and the "Greenhouse Gas Reduction Steering Committee" was established, with the mayor as the convener, to supervise the implementation of energy saving and carbon reduction action plans of four major city government sectors (residential and commercial, transportation, waste, and agriculture and forestry) every quarter, and invite experts and scholars to jointly discuss. In addition, the target of 8% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions in 2020 compared to the base year (2005) was set, and the total emissions in 2020 were 11,388,000 tons, showing a 12.9% reduction compared to the base year, achieving and exceeding the original target of 8% reduction. 1. Target to have a 20MW photovoltaic power generation equipment installation capacity in 2020: 33.63MW of cumulative installation capacity.2. Target to complete the replacement of 140,440 public streetlights with LED and other energy-saving lamps by the end of 2020: Completed the replacement of 150,930 LED streetlights in the whole City.3. Target to reach 84.51% of the population with access to wastewater treatment by the end of 2020: 85.58% of the population with access to wastewater treatment.4. Target to increase the area of green resources in the City by 250,000m2 by the end of 2020: Increased the area of green resources by about 265,000m2.5. Target to reach 66.8% of the green transportation market share by 2020: Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the percentage of public transportation ridership was affected, and the percentage of people using and taking green transportation reached 60.1%

plan_name: Taipei City Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan (Phase 2)
plan_comments: Taipei City has conducted an independent greenhouse gas inventory operation at the city level, showing a gradual decrease in emissions from 13.074 million metric tons of CO2e in 2005, in 2021, greenhouse gas emissions were 11.1426 million metric tons of CO2e, representing a reduction of approximately 1.9311 million metric tons compared to 2005, a decrease of 14.77%.The main sources of greenhouse gas emissions in the city are the residential, commercial, and transportation sectors, accounting for approximately 95% of the total emissions. To achieve energy-saving and carbon reduction goals, the city initially focused on the public sector and formulated an energy-saving plan for the Taipei City Government and affiliated agencies. The city government implemented measures to promote energy-saving and carbon reduction in the industrial and commercial sectors. These measures include energy-saving counseling, law enforcement and advocacy, incentive subsidies, and the promotion of energy-saving products. On-site inspections of energy-consuming equipment are conducted, and compliance with regulations is ensured. The promotion of energy-saving products is carried out through cooperation with various stores. In 2021, the service sector's electricity consumption decreased by 349 million kWh compared to 2020, resulting in an electricity saving rate of 3.77%.Solar photovoltaic systems have been installed on government agencies and schools, resulting in significant power generation and carbon reduction. Citizen power plants managed by civic groups have been established on city-owned buildings. Landfill sites are being transformed into energy parks, and small hydropower generation systems are being introduced. The city is also actively promoting the electrification of vehicles. An electric vehicle subsidy program has been announced, and charging stations are being constructed. The proportion of electric scooters in the city has significantly increased, and efforts are being made to establish a public bicycle system.To achieve net-zero emissions by 2050, the city government has revised environmental impact assessment regulations and promotes green building certifications. It has allocated funds for circular economy buildings and is actively promoting the concepts of an "agricultural city" and a "sponge city."

plan_name: Taipei City 2050 Net Zero Emissions Action White Paper 2
plan_comments: Taipei City has been actively implementing reduction measures, resulting in a decrease in emissions of11.388 million metric tons of CO2e in 2020, representing a 12.9% reduction compared to the baseline year of 2005. The residential, commercial, and transportation sectors are the main sources of emissions, accounting for 74.6% and 20.6% of the city's total emissions, respectively. Emissions from the residential and commercial sectors decreased by 13.1% in 2020 compared to the baseline year, while emissions from the transportation sector decreased by 8.2%.The residential, commercial, and transportation sectors constitute the city's major carbon sources, accounting for 74.60%, 20.58%, and 2.69% of the total emissions, respectively. Together, these three sectors contribute to nearly 98% of the city's carbon emissions.Based on projections of future population and economic changes, it is estimated that without any relevant reduction measures, emissions in 2050 would reach 12.496 million metric tons of CO2e.The promotion of net-zero emissions in Taipei City is pursued through the implementation of smart zero-carbon buildings, green and low-carbon transportation, a circular economy with zero waste, and the enhancement of carbon sinks. A collaboration between the central government and public-private sectors is crucial to achieve the net-zero emissions goal. The main strategies for the three major sectors include energy efficiency inspections, disclosure, and regulations in the residential and commercial sectors, along with the integration of renewable energy and hydrogen use to achieve zero-carbon buildings. The transportation sector focuses on promoting green transportation and the adoption of electric (hydrogen) vehicles. The waste sector aims for a circular economy with zero waste and ultimately zero emissions from waste treatment facilities. The agriculture and forestry sector emphasizes afforestation to increase carbon sinks and strengthens wetland conservation to maintain carbon sequestration.By implementing these strategies, Taipei City aims to achieve its net-zero emissions goal and actively contribute to mitigating climate change.

plan_name: Taipei City Climate Change Adaptation Implementation plan
plan_comments: Through cross-disciplinary communication and consultation, the City's overall climate change adaptation capacity is strengthened. In addition, the City will also gather the key contents and discussion results of the implementation situation of experts and various agencies, supplemented by the concept of dynamic adaptation pathways, and finally propose the City's climate change adaptation implementation plan. The initial planning of the implementation plan will refer to the framework of the City's 2021 adaptation action plan and incorporate the results of the City's past research and analysis, as well as the City's policy development and urban vision in recent years, in order to complete the contents of the City's adaptation implementation plan, which will focus on seven major areas, including disasters, water resources, life support infrastructure, industry and energy, agriculture and biodiversity, and land use and health.The planning chapters of the City's climate change adaptation implementation plan include: (1) Current Situation Analysis, (2) Program Objectives, (3) Promotion Process, (4) Implementation Plan, (5) Expected Benefits, and (6) Management and Evaluation Mechanism.

plan_name: Taipei City Net-Zero Emissions Management Autonomy Regulations
plan_comments: In order to promote the City's net zero transformation, the City proposed the "Taipei City Net Zero Emission Management Autonomy Act" , which was revised after public hearings and consultation with various sectors. Incorporating the 2050 net zero emissions target into this Act, specifying the authorities and responsibilities of various city government agencies, introducing a "carbon budget" system, and setting up a climate transformation fund. In response to the United Nations IPCC AR6 ,the Act will be strengthened with a special chapter on adaptation. The Act is divided into 7 chapters and 55 articles.1. In order to increase the carbon reduction to 40% by 2030, with a net zero emissions target by 2050. Also, promote a "carbon budget", set carbon emission caps for various sectors.2. In order to promote industrial transformation, City will set up a climate transformation fund, which will be dedicated to climate change adaptation, greenhouse gas reduction, and taking care of the underprivileged in climate transformation to ensure fair transformation.3.  It specifies that public buildings and buildings of a certain size in Taipei City should publicly label information on building energy consumption and strengthen controls from 2030 onward to improve building energy efficiency.4.  From the public sector to private sector, the City government has set 2023 as the target year for electrification of public motorcycles and 2030 as the target date for electrification of urban buses and public cars as the key policy priority.  Low-carbon transportation zones will be promoted gradually toreduce the amount of carbon emissions from transportation.5. The Act  stipulates the reduction and banning of the use of disposable items, that the use of tableware should be paid for and completely banned, and requires public and private venues to set up environmentally friendly container recycling systems. In addition, it stipulates that existing old incinerators should be upgraded to high-efficiency green energy power plants and environmentally friendly green energy recycling parks, and carbon capture and utilization equipment should be introduced.6. The Act  stipulates that the City Government should conduct studies and surveys on the City's land use,vulnerability and regularly review urban plans. https://www.taiwannews.com.tw/en/news/4579962

This new action plan is a multi-sector approach of actions in both climate mitigation and adaptation. Actions included in these documents vary from yet to start to currently under development, to in progress. The new Climate Strategy: Pathways to 2050 was approved by Council in July 2022 and replaces the previously updated 2018 Resilience Strategy.

plan_url: https://resource.capetown.gov.za/documentcentre/Documents/City%20strategies,%20plans%20and%20frameworks/CCT_Climate_Change_Action_Plan.pdf  AND  City of Cape Town Climate Change Strategy:
plan_comments: The City has a long history of progressively and technically responding to climate change and aligned its climate change response to global ambition through its participation in the C40 Deadline 2020 programme from 2017 onwards. This commitment required the development of a climate action plan by mid-2021, aligned with the Paris Agreement The City’s latest Climate Change Strategy was subsequently approved by Council in May 2021 and the Climate Change Action Plan - a detailed programme-level implementation plan of the Strategy was approved by the City Manager in August 2021. A monitoring and evaluation (M&E) framework was developed in 2022, with the aim of tracking both progress against the actions contained with the Action Plan, as well as monitoring trends in indicators that have been developed to assess whether medium-term outcomes related to the goals of the Strategy and Action Plan are being achieved.The Action Plan includes 101 actions across 10 Strategic Focus Areas: Urban cooling and heat responsiveness, Water security and drought readiness; Water sensitivity, flood readiness and storm management; Coastal management and resilience; Managing fire risk and responsiveness; Spatial and resource inclusivity; Carbon-neutral energy for work creation and economic development; Zero-emissions buildings and precincts; Mobility for quality of life and livelihoods; Circular Waste Economy and 5 Cross-cutting Work Areas: Mainstreaming, governance, research and knowledge management; Economic impacts and green economy opportunities; Business models, and financing climate change response; Communication, collaboration and skills development and Human and ecosystem health.. Cost estimates are allocated to the actions according to range categories (low/no cost, medium cost or high cost) but a detailed quantitative assessment has not taken place yet, although work is underway in partnership with the World Bank/IFC group to use a GHG emissions modelling and investment analysis tool (called APEX)  to do further analysis of key actions and better understand funding required and financing options.M&E takes the form in annual action status level updates, and detailed/in-depth assessments every 3 years. These also inform the full update of the action plan which takes place every 5 years. The first implementation review report (2021/22 reporting year) is attached here.

Em 2015 foi lançado o Plano de Ações e Metas para a Redução dos Gases de Efeito Estufa - Plano de Baixo Carbono de Fortaleza. Este foi o primeiro documento estratégico em que a cidade alinhou suas ações com os compromissos do Acordo de Paris.Durante o ano de 2016, a PMF publicou o seu 2º Inventário de Emissões de GEE, em parceria com o Banco de Desenvolvimento da América Latina (CAF), sob o nome Pegadas de Carbono e Hídrica da Cidade de Fortaleza, integrante do projeto Pegadas das Cidades realizado pela consultoria contratada Servicios Ambientales S.A. Em conjunto ao Projeto, em 2017, foi desenvolvido o Plano de Ações para Redução das Pegadas de Carbono e Pegadas Hídricas da Cidade de Fortaleza.Posteriormente estes documentos passaram por uma atualização que reflete uma evolução tanto à nível de ambição proposta para as reduções, como à nível de estratégias de governança. As ações e propostas do Plano de Baixo Carbono foram objeto do Sustainable Dialogue Lab (SD Lab - Laboratório de Diálogo Sustentável), sendo revistas por meio de uma ação conjunta com as universidades Northeastern University, da cidade de Boston, nos Estados Unidos e a Universidade de Fortaleza - UNIFOR, integrando assim academia, a juventude e o poder público.As atualizações deste planejamento se concretizam em um novo Plano, denominado Plano Local de Ação Climática (PLAC) de Fortaleza, produzido como parte do Projeto UrbanLEDS II. Sua elaboração partiu do alinhamento entre metas, ações planejadas e legislação existentes, utilizando a instância de participação social do FORCLIMA para debate das pautas e realizando diálogo com gestores de diferentes instituições e secretarias para validar e verificar possibilidades. Para fundamentação das metas são utilizadas informações dos estudos produzidos por Fortaleza, tendo como ponto de partida os Inventários de Emissão de Gases do Efeito Estufa (GEE), documentos nos quais se realiza o mapeamento, relato e monitoramento das fontes de emissão, e que são essenciais para o início do planejamento para a ação climática.OBSERVAÇÃO: NÃO FOI ESTIMADO O CUSTO TOTAL DE IMPLEMENTAÇÃO DO PLANO.
Essa ação irá ajudar a reduzir a pobreza energética da cidade FORTALEZA

Miami Forever Carbon Neutral is the City’s Greenhouse Gas Reduction (GHG) Plan and is a roadmap to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050 in the community, strengthen the local economy, and enhance climate justice. This Plan is focused on rapidly decreasing greenhouse gas emissions from sources within City of Miami’s jurisdiction, aiming to achieve a minimum 60% reduction in emissions from 2018 levels by 2035 and setting the City up to get as close to zero emissions as possible. The plan is based on five overarching goals that support a GREEN Miami and achievement of the City’s GHG reduction targets: G - GETTING AROUND MIAMI Objective: 15% shift away from private vehicle trips compared to 2018 levels by 2035 R - RENEWABLE ENERGY Objective: 100% carbon-free electricity by 2035 Objective: 35% reduction in natural gas emissions compared to 2018 levels by 2035 E - ELECTRIC VEHICLES Objective: 40% of registered passenger vehicles are electric by 2035 E - ENERGY EFFICIENCY Objective: Improve energy efficiency in buildings to decrease overall energy consumption and support achievement of Goal 2: Renewable Energy N - NEW ECONOMY Objective: Grow the Green Economy Ecosystem Objective: Recruit and Retain Green Workforce Objective: Open Occupational Pathways Objective: Welcome and Support Green Industry GREEN actions will not only reduce GHG emissions but also build a new green economy.Total cost of implementing plan is the Office of Resilience and Sustainability annual budget, including salaries and program funding. Additional partial staff salaries and program funding from other Departments are not included.

Additional partial staff salaries and program funding from other Departments are not included.

The Stormwater Master Plan provides a strategy for remediating the impacts of flooding and storm surge. These climate impacts have the capacity to disrupt surface road transportation, sewer and septic system operation, and public health.  The Stormwater Master Plan developed a capital infrastructure plan for the City to inform expenditures of the City’s general obligation bond allocation for flood mitigation. Overall, the City’s intent for the Miami Forever General Obligation Bond is to build a stronger, more resilient future for Miami. This is achieved by alleviating existing and future risks to the residents, economy, tourism, and by protecting the City’s legacy. The Bond funds a series of immediate, near-term, and long-term projects with the goal of transforming the future of Miami in key categories which align with the City’s most pressing needs, including addressing sea level rise and flood prevention. The objectives of the stormwater-related bond projects are to minimize flooding frequency, severity, duration, and impact, and to protect critical infrastructure, and high-use areas. This, in turn, reduces financial and economic vulnerability. The plan incorporates green infrastructure such as bioswales, natural drainage, and shoreline improvements, and recommends changes to stormwater infrastructure to filter and capture sediment, debris, and plastics before they can pollute the bay, thus achieving additional environmental and conservation benefits.

plan_name: Plano Local de Ação Climática da Cidade do Recife (PLAC) -
plan_comments: Coordenado pelo ICLEI, o Plano Plano Local de Ação Climática do Recife (PLAC) foi concluído no final de 2020. Em 2021 a Secretaria de Meio Ambiente e Sustentabilidade (SMAS) disponibilizou à cidade do Recife sua metodologia de Monitoramento das Ações e Estratégias Climáticas (MOCLIMA). Esse instrumento técnico/metodológico permite acompanhar o cumprimento das metas de redução do carbono atmosférico e os objetivos do combate à crise climática por meio de indicadores tangíveis com os status das ações de mitigação e adaptação da cidade. Todas as informações desenvolvidas e monitoradas estão armazenadas nos servidores e bancos de dados da própria Prefeitura do Recife, e disponibilizadas no site da SMAS por meio de web services, aplicativos e dashboards, facilitando o acompanhamento por parte dos cidadãos e tomadores de decisão. Esse instrumento é o mecanismo operacional e pragmático do Plano Local de Ação Climática da Cidade do Recife.

The Climate Action Plan 3.0 was a multi-year process that was focused on thoughtful civic engagement. The deliberative civic engagement process included over 400 residents representing 90 organizations from the Pittsburgh business community, non-profit sector, and local, state, and federal government partners. This process allowed the City to adopt pragmatic strategies that account for and expand upon many actions and initiatives already underway.While the Climate Action Plan is designed first and foremost as a carbon mitigation strategy, there is a broad scope of ‘cobenefits’ that can be achieved through the proposed actions.When putting forth strategies and pathways for deep carbon reductions, these co-benefits were analyzed to assist with action prioritization. Mitigation actions can help address Pittsburgh’s Resilience Challenges and achieve key co-benefits such as improved equity, increased economic development and decreased negative health impacts

The strategy was developed to integrate the short- and long-term actions in the Climate Action Plan into the City’s development review processes to help reach the goal of reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 50 percent by 2030. Over the next four years, the City Energy Strategy will integrate energy plans into planning and development review to evaluate energy systems decisions early in the planning process and improve long-term coordination between utilities, permit holders and developers. Planning for clean and renewable energy in developments leads to fewer emissions, more efficient buildings, healthier spaces for building occupants and annual cost-savings for building owners.

O Plano Local de Ação Climática - PLAC se configurará como um documento estratégico focado em entregar à cidade uma visão integrada e inclusiva -  alinhada com suas prioridades sociais, ambientais e econômicas - bem como as condições facilitadoras e marcos de implementação necessários para ações voltadas à mitigação de emissões de gases de efeito estufa e aumento da resiliência da cidade frente aos impactos da mudança do clima. Assim, o Plano, que envolverá diversos atores, tanto instituições públicas, privadas, quanto sociedade civil e academia, responderá aos compromissos e metas assumidos pelo município enquanto fortalece a gestão pública intersetorial ao integrar a ação climática aos processos estratégicos de planejamento, gestão, serviços urbanos e ecossistêmicos. O plano será elaborado de forma integrada e participativa com a elaboração de 3 (três) Workshop e participação social que irão estabelecer as prioridades e ações para a Ação Climática Integrada ampla (ACI). Quatro produtos técnicos serão usados como base neste processo: 1) a análise do estado atual da ação climática integrada em Campinas, 2) a atualização do inventário de Emissões de Gases de Efeito Estufa - GEE, 3) a elaboração de cenários de reduções de emissões para que sejam zeradas até 2050 e 4) uma análise de riscos climáticos e sua distribuição sócio-geográfica na cidade. O produto final será um relatório, previsto para ser finalizado no primeiro trimestre de 2024.

plan_comments: Looking at 2050, every city, large, medium-sized or small, can make a difference and every single person can contribute to achieving a future  that leaves no one behind. If we consider, for example, the scenarios envisaged in the reports of the competent international agencies, they disclose that the average temperature of the atmosphere will continue to increase; precipitation regimes will be significantly transformed; the European population will decrease and will last longer while digital systems and infrastructures will determine the rise and fall of the cities’ positioning in the ability to provide services, to ensure prosperity at work, to acquire skills in studying, caring, building solid relationships, etc. Genoa position between now and 2050 traced a participatory route focused on THREE clear work assets, THREE systemic goals and an implementation program based on 12 priority action areas described in the Genoa 2050 Action Plan. the operational strategy is developed in a serious pathway made of anticipation and strengthening efforts on thematic focuses such as: innovating the planned land use, optimizing and renewing infrastructures, regenerating the urban fabric, creating multifunctional connections, investing in knowledge, innovative training, soft skills and improving the governance of the city system for the future generations. Furthermore, the scenarios of the preliminary study phases properly consider the new and unexpected element of transformation, the one that in more technical terms it is called “gray rhino” or “black swan” according to our ability to forecast and consider it both as an implementation risk and as a new planning element. Finally, to measure effectiveness of the actions reported in the plan, a particular attention was paid to the monitoring and evaluation aspects of the interventions.  Thanks to a research project, it has been developed an innovative and flexible framework capable of expressing a quantitative assessment of infrastructures, environment and governance. Latter aspects brought back to the final goal of improving six qualities (liveability, inclusiveness, development capacities, attractiveness, sustainability and well-being) which, as a whole, contribute to become a lighthouse city for present and future communities

plan_comments: Despite several obstacles (UNESCO site, wide cultural heritage, >14 million tourists per year, many commuters, large administrative structure, competence on services shared with other municipalities…), Florence has tackled seriously its commitment to sustainability in a sort of never-ending pathway (from the Aalborg Charter to the new Covenant of Mayors for Climate and Energy to Green City Accord…).Based on human-centric soul, in 2010 Florence adopted an interdisciplinary, co-productive and integrated approach to strategic planning, convinced that only exploiting synergies among sectors, empowering stakeholders and involving citizens, the city could have been able to face  challenges.The first two examples of participated and integrated plans have been the SEAP and the Structural plan “Zero Volume” when the city set up a specific “task force” involving all departments and implemented a wide participatory process in “100 squares”.The team has been supported (definition and monitoring) by external consultants in the framework of the European Energy Award process and several H2020 projects (Come2CoM, STEEP, Replicate, CoME Easy).The SEAP, following the CoM requirement, targeted mitigation actions in each sector of municipal competence: public, tertiary and residential buildings and facilities, municipal fleet and public transport, private mobility... Several adaptation issues have already been included like green spaces and water management. The monitoring of the plan shown higher impacts than expected, thanks to the strong administration commitment in daily operations and to citizens' engagement.

plan_comments: Following the SEAP and urban plan experience and thanks to an FP7 project, the city developed an innovative and comprehensive "Smart City Plan" exploiting system thinking methodologies and co-production approaches.The plan, adopted in 2015, was meant as a "master plan" for any future urban development, setting the guiding strategy and principles for the evolution of the local regulatory framework. The targets set at 2030 and 2050, very ambitious, were already in line with the recent EU indications (carbon neutrality at 2050) and adaptation and energy poverty issues already addressed.This one-of-a-kind master plan has become the reference for the following policies and governance tools of the city; the Smart City Plan, as the SEAP/SECAP, is meant to be a cross-cutting tool integrating different sectors: it shows the ambition to influence every sector of competence, exploiting technology and innovation only with the aim of “prosperity” and well-being (reported also as best practice in the Smart Cities Guidance Package).Florence's target vision is not merely related to IT, perceived as “tools”, but to a wider scope, in line with UN SDGs, for sustainability and wellbeing; this have been reflected in the "Smart prioritization" concept developed in H2020-SCC1 REPLICATE project based on the assessment of all different direct impacts and indirect externalities (health, inclusion, poverty, justice…).The city has decided to focus on vulnerable people, energy poverty and social inclusion also in its innovation testbeds with intervention in low-income districts and social housing, advanced mobility services for vulnerable people, affordable public transport, dedicated trainings on digital competences for elderly people, …. Florence is also aware of its weaknesses and boundary conditions: playing an exemplary role, the public sector began to involve more and more stakeholders in sharing data to take together the best decision. The Smart City Control Room best practice is the materialisation of this cooperation between different stakeholders for the common good.Aimed at optimising the daily management, the SCCR demonstrated during COVID periods its flexibility, supporting emergency analysis and in providing useful services/info to citizens.

plan_comments: The Urban Plan for Sustainable Mobility of the Metropolitan City has been adopted in the second half of 2019 after a long process where the municipality together with the metropolitan city and the Region have contributed and agreed contents with local stakeholders.The first phase of the work, started in 2018,  was aimed at the reconstruction of the cognitive framework in relation also to the local and supra-local planning tools and at starting the participatory path that allowed the objectives to be declined; the second work phase concerned the design of the scenarios which have taken advantage from the Replicate pilot action and all the previous experiences of the municipality of Florence in terms of electric mobility, ICT platform and traffic supervision, mobility infrastructures, bike and pedestrian areas, parking management, multimodality, etc. The final draft of the plan has been subjected to a strategic environmental assessment as required, pointing out all the environmental benefits/costs and the monitoring methodologies foreseen.The plan reports in detail all the actions agreed at 2030, which has been taken as reference scenario, with an implementation program, the related costs and impacts as well as the monitoring KIPs. The measures included have been divided into two different groups depending on the years of implementation (2021-2025 and 2026-2030).This document is the main tool for the extension of the measures to the whole metropolitan area in short/mid-term period. The full documentation is available at https://www.cittametropolitana.fi.it/pums/and the final plan adopted can be downloaded here:  https://www.cittametropolitana.fi.it/pums/pums-approvato/
