plan_url: https://ambiente.comune.fi.it/pagina/sostenibilita-ambientale/patto-dei-sindacihttps://ambiente.comune.fi.it/pagina/smart-cityhttps://www.cittametropolitana.fi.it/pums/pums-approvato/httpNAhttps://pianostrutturale.comune.fi.it/documenti_del_piano/ Together with the environmental impacts evaluation.httpNAhttpNA
plan_comments: The distinguishing feature of Florence’s particular nature derives from both its history and from the inclusion of the city’s historical centre among the UNESCO heritage sites. The centre represents a one-of-a-kind social and urban accomplishment, resulting from centuries of continuous creation and includes priceless museums, churches, palaces, and properties. Hence, the challenge is maintaining a balance between tradition and development within the stratified urban context.The municipal Structural Plan, approved in 2010 and continuously updated (2014, 2019), defined the strategy of "governance" of the territory aimed at the concept of "zero volume" to contain the anthropic pressure on a single environment. The planning document also contains precise indications on sustainability as a transversal element that integrates with policies and actions.Much of the choices of the Structural Plan, for the environment, transport and widespread quality, reflect this fundamental perspective, which must find specific operational translation in the Urban Planning RegulationsThe combined urban tools, Structural Plan and Urban planning Rules, represent a complete innovation in terms of approach to planning, since they do not foresee the exclusive reuse of abandoned buildings/surfaces, but only their transformation for the city’s development. The principle of planning is to rehabilitate, limiting as far as possible the consumption of new ground (so-called “zero volume”) and improving the amount of the existing ecological network and social spaces.For the first time, the Structural Plan granted equal importance to the system of ecological allocations as to that of mobility and collective facility systems, for the planning and management of the territory. Hence the definition and identification of ecological allocations, consisting of nodes and corridors making up the main network, formed by green elements of the urban fabric and connected to the metropolitan one.Therefore, the city is designed to contain and mitigate its pressure on the environment, also thanks to the network of green corridors (green infrastructure) identified through the compatibility of requalification and enhancement interventions, privileging the public and the private. Hence, the urban planning vision, the guidelines until 2030, are included in the Structural Plan.

plan_comments: The actions included in the City Action Plan (PAC) for the rehabilitation and maintenance of air quality are compliant with the guidelines of the Regional Air Quality Plan (PRQA) whose objective is protecting public health, and the measures established in the sectorial plans are consistent and concurrent to reduce the emissions from the main sources.In order to protect public health, in compliance with PRQA the PAC pursues the objective of protecting air quality through the following general objectives:•reduce to zero the % of the population exposed to exceedances beyond the limit values of NO2 and PM10.•reduce the proportion of the population exposed to pollution levels above the target value for ozone.•maintain good air quality where pollution levels are permanently below the limit values•update and improve the dissemination of information.The different measures are:a)rehabilitation:  actions whose effect is mainly in the areas of exceedance of air quality;b)maintenance: actions having  effect on the entire regional territory;c)prescriptions: binding provisions that directly affect the planning tools of municipalitiesFlorence foresees:•to introduce a traffic ban in air quality plans for commercial vehicles (N1,N2, N3) and diesel vehicles up to Euro-4 from 8,30 to 18,30 on weekdays. This limitation will be extended as well to the Euro-5 by 2025.•to include in the air quality plans the obligation to use certified pellet in accordance with class A1 of UNI EN ISO 17225-2•incentives for the replacement of one or more types of vehicles registered in the municipality of Florence, subject to the limitation referred to in letter a), with vehicles with low environmental impact such as vehicles powered exclusively by electricity, plug-in electricity, hybrid-electric or exclusively LPG or methane gas.•contributions for small farms and citizens who grow trees in areas of exceedance for the purchase of bio-shredders and for the management of cuttings and pruning compatible with the protection of air quality.•planting of tree species particularly performing for the reduction of air pollution, implementation of green roof and walls.•encourage the use of the TPL- Local Public Transport (incentives for subscriptions, single train-bus ticket, day ticket, etc.)•strengthen the infrastructure network for charging electric vehicles.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: We view resilience as a regional issue, so the project area for the plan includes all of Shelby County and additional surrounding counties, conforming to the Memphis Metropolitan Planning Organization (MPO) boundaries.
plan_comments: Our climate adaptation plan is regional in scope and scale. This means that the recommendations take a regional lens in terms of research, analysis, and design. They do not, for example, recommend specific dimensions for resizing local culverts or designate the detailed boundaries of a proposed water detention area. Instead, they seek to identify resilience opportunities that can only be achieved when planning at a regional scale, such as watershed or aquifer management. Even with this caveat about spatial scale, the recommendations do attempt to be site specific wherever practical and pair recommendations with specific jurisdictions, agencies, and collaborators whenever possible. More specific recommendations for the City of Memphis are listed in the Memphis 3.0 Climate Action Plan and these recommendations align with the regional recommendations in the Mid-South Regional Resilience Master Plan.

Riga municipal government has developed a Plan, using the  methodology of the Pact. The Plan consists of 14 sections, including a brief overview of European,  national, and regional guidelines for energy and climate policy, and the linking of the Plan with the  development planning documents of Riga. A separate section of the plan is devoted to strategy,  including vision, commitments, and goals, as well as the coordinating and organisational structures  and financial aspects. The Plan identifies 7 sectors (e.g., municipal infrastructure, energy production,  transport, etc.), providing a description of the current situation, the main challenges, and the  measures to be implemented to achieve the goals set in the Plan. In general, the energy goals are for reducing CO2 emissions, adapting to climate change, and reducing  air pollution. In order to achieve the goals, the Plan has identified 112 measures that will generate a  total of 1,289 GWh in energy savings, provide 1,350 GWh of renewable energy and reduce CO2 emissions by 509 thousand tonnes. The overall goal of the plan is to reduce CO2 emissions in Riga by  30% compared to 2019, in order to ensure progress towards climate neutrality by no later than 2050.

The Sustainable Energy and Climate Action Plan is a document of a strategic nature who needs multidisciplinary skills and the interaction of several sectors of the Municipality. The Plan affects many local policies, through regulatory measures, information and direct asset management. The Municipaloty is willing to create a dedicated city team, with a coordination between the offices that can support the administration in the implementation of the activities identified in the SECAP. The management of the Plan will be entrusted to an intersectoral working group, coordinated by the Environment and Territory Department, through the Informambiente office and the Energy Manager office. The working group will have to involve the following Departments: Environment and Territory; Mobility; Public Works; Green, Parks and Agriculture urban; Private Construction; Urban planning and Cadastral Services; IT and telematic services; Local Police and Civil Protection. For each sector, a contact person will be identified, who will take part in the meetings and will act as a point of contact for the activities concerning its competence.The working group is called to manage over time the correct implementation of the individual actions envisaged from the Plan, directly implementing those of its own responsibility and soliciting commitment and collaboration of personnel from all sectors indirectly involved.The group also has the task of collaborating in the biennial monitoring of the Plan.

plan_name: Pilot Climate Equity Fund, Just Transition Pilot Program, Equity Guardrails, ACEEE Renter Protections Toolkit
plan_boundary_desc: Smaller boundary: Pilot programs will target a subset of low-income residents in Berkeley
plan_comments: Energy burden is a huge challenge faced by our low-income communities. Although the DOE LEAD tool shows Berkeley has a 1% household energy burden rate, we know there are subpopulations that are struggling to pay energy bills and our energy rates (which are already high) continue to increase. As a local jurisdiction we cannot control rates, but we provide outreach and communication to educate and support our communities about programs to help support. We are also conducting a Pilot Climate Equity Fund and Just Transition Pilot Program, which serve to integrate efficiency and electrification to low- and moderate-income Berkeley community members along with increasing high road jobs in the clean energy transition. We also participated in the ACEEE Renter Protection program to try and learn how we can protect renters from displacement and increased energy burden as rental homes are transitioned away from gas. We have much more to do and this will require action from our local utilities, Public Utilities Commission, and community choice aggregators, along with other partners.

O Plano de Mitigação e Adaptação às Mudanças Climáticas de Salvador representa um importante passo para a promoção da justiça climática e igualdade social ao garantir que o ônus das mudanças do clima não recaia de maneira mais intensa na população que já se encontra em situação de maior vulnerabilidade e que notoriamente menos contribui com o desequilíbrio climático. O termo ‘justiça climática’ significa tratar da questão climática, e ao mesmo tempo, avançar em questões de equidade e proteção dos direitos humanos. Além disso, baseia-se na elaboração de alternativas que diminuam de forma equitativa os riscos das mudanças do clima e distribua os custos e benefícios de cada ação à toda a população e ecossistemas da cidade, tendo um olhar especial para as populações mais vulneráveis. O PMAMC, no eixo Salvador Inclusiva, além de inserir a justiça climática no cerne de seus objetivos, reforça ações de outros planos e políticas sobre a ampla participação da população, em especial os grupos mais vulneráveis, na formulação, implementação e monitoramento de propostas para ação climática. Considerando que a melhor forma de qualificar a participação, engajamento e o monitoramento das ações da Prefeitura por parte da população seja o acesso à informação, educação e capacitação, a cidade pretende formar cidadãos conscientes dos efeitos das mudanças do clima em suas vidas e no planetaCom isso, passamos a compreender que as ações climáticas são uma oportunidade de dar mais dignidade e igualdade a todos, oferecendo para a população um futuro com mais oportunidades de inclusão social e desenvolvimento econômico, dando mais acesso à saúde, energia, saneamento básico e oportunidades de trabalho dignas, ajudando a reduzir a desigualdade social existente.

Co-benefits and synergies were carefully considered in Thrive. The convergence of sustainability and resilience within our plan inevitably creates co-benefits and synergies within action items. Some examples of action items that create co-benefits/synergies are:  1. BE:2A - Systematically integrate climate change projections into all future capital projects by 2020, ensuring new infrastructure can withstand current and projected impacts  -  this action item aims to make the built environment more resilient to climate change occurrence (ex. Extreme weather). It will create co-benefits to the community so that the built environment is safer for community members that will encounter extreme weather events 2. Many of the Economy section creates co-benefits/synergies:  EC:1B - Collaborate with workforce development partners to pilot a green jobs training program by 2022. EC:1D - Pilot a green job incubator or co-working space by 2025. EC:2B - Develop and pilot a green business / energy efficiency training program for the re-entry population with local workforce development and re-entry partners by 2022. In all of these, building the local green job workforce will create a faster transition within vital levels of infrastructure that will help lead to carbon neutrality (ex. building electrification, vehicle electrification, solar integration, ecological land stewardship, circular economy). These economic endeavors also create reliable job opportunities for people entering the workforce or people transitioning from aging industries.  3. EN:2C - Identify and eliminate barriers to engagement in IPL’s Income-Qualified Weatherization Program and other efficiency programs focused on low-income households.  -   This action item works to connect low-income households with energy efficiency programs through the local electrical utility to decrease utility usage (smaller household carbon footprint) while also saving low-income households money.  At times within action item implementation, balancing Thrive’s two overarching goals has led to trade-offs. Thrive’s overarching goals are:  1. Increase community resilience by prioritizing equity in policy, planning and project implementation. 2. Achieve net zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 2050 Not every action item can center equity, while moving the needle toward carbon neutrality.

plan_name: City of Milwaukee Climate and Equity Plan:
plan_comments: The Climate and Equity Plan is the result of a  4-year community-driven process, originating out of the City-County Task Force on Climate and Economic Equity.  The Task Force began convening in 2019, released a preliminary report in 2020, met throughout 2021 in 9 substantive work groups to develop recommendations (including energy efficiency experts, community members, agency staff and non-profit organizations), finalized and sought additional community feedback on the Plan in 2022 and the Plan was approved by the City of Milwaukee Planning Commission, Milwaukee Common Council and was signed by Milwaukee Mayor Johnson in June 2023.  It is comprised of 10 Big Ideas to mitigate and reduce GHG emissions, as well as adapt to existing  and future climate change impacts.  The complete Climate and Equity Plan is available here: https://city.milwaukee.gov/climate/Climate-Plan.
The Office of Sustainability and Resilience was the primary author of the plan.

plan_comments: While the total cost of implementation was not quantified for Climate Resilient Edmonton: Adaptation Strategy and Action Plan the cost of climate impacts was quantified. This showed that  climate impacts on the city could increase by $8.0 billion by the 2050s and $18.2 by the 2080s (compared to today). These ‘social’ costs include health costs, environmental costs and financial losses. Looking solely at the gross domestic product (GDP) component, Edmonton’s GDP would be $3.2 billion and $7.4 billion lower by the 2050s and 2080s, respectively (compared to today). Social and GDP costs for Edmonton increase with each degree of additional warming.

This document comprises a Hazard Mitigation Plan for Mecklenburg County, North Carolina and its incorporated municipalities. A well-prepared hazard mitigation plan will ensure that all possible activities are reviewed and implemented so that the problem is addressed by the most appropriate and efficient solutions. It can also ensure that activities are coordinated with each other and with other goals and activities, preventing conflicts and reducing the costs of implementing each individual activity. This plan provides a framework for all interested parties to work together toward mitigation. It establishes the vision and guiding principles for reducing hazard risk and proposes specific mitigation actions to eliminate or reduce identified vulnerabilities.This plan was prepared in coordination with FEMA Region IV and the North Carolina Division of Emergency Management (NCEM) to ensure that it meets all applicable federal and state planning requirements. A Local Mitigation Plan Review Tool, found in Appendix A, provides a summary of FEMA’s current minimum standards of acceptability and notes the location within this plan where each planning requirement is met.

This is the City of Charlotte’s first Strategic Energy Action Plan (SEAP), as required by its commitment to the Global Covenant of Mayors (GCoM). The SEAP provides a framework for delivering the ‘Sustainable and Resilient Charlotte by 2050’ Resolution. The Resolution was championed by City Council’s Environment Committee as an example of their leadership and commitment to Charlotte’s low carbon future and was unanimously passed in June of 2018.The SEAP provides a roadmap for the City’s transition to a low carbon future, delivering the City Council’s resolution to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to less than 2 tons of CO2e per person across the City by 2050.To be a competitive city in a globally engaged era, Charlotte recognizes that the City needs to become more sustainable and resilient. This needs to be achieved by employing an integrated, inclusive, and systematic approach to urban development. The SEAP will form one part of the City’s broader resiliency strategy and will be used to build stronger and more inclusive neighborhoods that can make informed decisions around the use of energy. The SEAP provides the City with the framework it needs to deliver the transition to a low carbon economy that attracts and retains talent, and grows local jobs and a green economy.Recommendations provided by the SEAP will encourage innovative administration, organization, and mechanisms that ensure strategic alignment and efficiencies within implementation efforts. These recommendations enable the SEAP to align with other current and future City strategies, so that they are better integrated and mutually reinforcing. This includes key issues such as funding models, total costs of ownership, and policy revisions.The City of Charlotte acknowledges that the SEAP is ambitious in documenting what the City wants to achieve, as the City seeks innovative approaches to the challenges and opportunities an energy regulated state provides. The effort to successfully implement the SEAP and achieve a low carbon future lies within a citywide collaborative effort.

plan_comments: The ACT Climate Change Strategy 2019-2025 includes actions to reduce emissions and build resilience to climate change impacts. It replaces the previous ACT Climate Change Adaptation Strategy and brings together mitigation and adaptation in a holistic climate change response. Synergies and co-benefits include improving air quality while reducing emissions from transport by encouraging active travel and zero emissions vehicles, and improving public health, resilience to extreme heat and energy efficiency through building upgrades. Trade-offs such as prioritising resource use across programs and the economic impacts of regulatory and other measures were considered in developing the Strategy.

plan_comments: Canberra’s Living Infrastructure Plan: Cooling the City (the Plan) provides strategic direction to help our expanding and densifying metropolitan areas become better prepared for and more resilient to climate change. It identifies options and opportunities provided by living infrastructure measures to enable us to continue to enjoy the benefits of:- climate resilience- the amenities of nature- economic prosperity and- health and wellbeing.The Plan is used to inform policy and forward planning studies for centres, urban renewal projects and urban intensification precincts. Through the plan, the Government collects and reports data to monitor progress against resilience indicators, including continuation of the longitudinal survey and climate-related health impacts and costs.

plan_name: Parliamentary & Governing Agreement: 10th Legislative Assembly Australian Capital Territory
plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: It covers the area of Government responsibility, the (Australian Capital Territory).
plan_comments: This plan is the Parliamentary and Governing Agreement for the 10th Legislative Assembly in the ACT and was signed by all ACT Labor and ACT Greens MLAs. The parties agreed that the world is facing a climate change emergency, and committed to undertaking rapid, science-based action to mitigate and adapt to climate change and transition the ACT to net zero emissions. The parties also committed to working closely with the community, ensuring a just transition for those impacted by the shift to net zero emissions, and growing sustainable industries and jobs.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: It covers the area of Government responsibility, the (Australian Capital Territory).
plan_comments: Working alongside renewed strategies for climate change, transport and housing, the ACT Planning Strategy 2018 will take Canberra to 2045. It will guide the development of a city that is locally strong, regionally integrated and globally connected. Careful urban planning will improve our sustainability and resilience to climate change through protecting and expanding living infrastructure, managing our waterways, reducing emissions, protecting our parks and reserves for both for our community and our biodiversity, and reducing our ecological footprint. This can make our city a more sustainable and liveable place for current and future Canberrans.If private space gets smaller with urban intensification, then the importance of public space increases. The plan acknowledges the need to plan for climate change impacts and grow the urban forest to manage urban heat in Canberra. The city is already experiencing the effects of climate change and is predicted to experience more heatwaves, more intense storms and increasing severity of droughts. Adapting to the impacts of climate change in our region will mean improving our resilience to natural hazard events such as bushfire, storms, flooding, drought and heatwaves. The ACT Government is preparing for these impacts by planning to keep our city cool and liveable in a changing climate. Improving our ‘living infrastructure’ (plants, soils and waterways) will help keep our city cool and reduce urban heat impacts. This could include increasing tree canopy cover, designing parks and open spaces for natural cooling, restoring urban waterways and encouraging green roofs and walls.

In 2020, Climate action was identified as a priority area for investment through the 10-year Budget 2021-2031. Council for the first time included a dedicated climate action investment package of $152 million of funding allocated over 10 years.A Climate Action Targeted Rate (CATR) over the next 10-years was adopted as part of the Annual Budget 2022/23. The purpose of this targeted rate is to increase funding for climate action to reduce emissions, lay the foundation to enable further reductions in the future, and prepare for the impacts of climate change, with an immediate focus on enhancing low carbon transport options and greening our neighbourhoods. The CATR builds on the $152m climate action investment in the 10-year Budget 2021-2031. The CATR package was developed based on four principles, it needed to be high impact, address inequity, be started fast and have wide regional benefits. Public consultation on the CATR received two-thirds majority support from individuals, organisations and Māori entities and there was also a clear margin of support from the independent Kantar Public Survey. The $574million raised through the targeted rate will be used to unlock government cofunding of $354 million. Together with the expected fare revenue of $127 million, we expect to be able to invest an additional $1.056 billion investment in buses, ferries, walking, cycling and our urban ngahere (forest) over the next 10 years.Therefore, the overall investment for the next 10 years could be $1,208 million ($152 million from  the 10-year Budget 2021-2031 plus  $1.056 billion from the Climate Action Targeted Rate (CATR) and other revenue over the next 10-years from 2022/2023).

plan_name: Ahmedabad Climate Resilient City Action Plan (Towards A Net Zero Future 2070)
plan_comments: AMC used the ‘Net Zero ClimateResilientCITIES’ methodology to develop its climate action plan to achieve net zero emissions by 2070. This methodology includes a multi-stakeholder approach that involves assessing climate vulnerabilities, estimating GHG emissions, proposing climate resilient interventions that address adaptation and mitigation, and developing frameworks for financing, implementing, monitoring and reporting climate actions. By incorporating expertise and experience from initiatives led by ICLEI – Local Governments for Sustainability, the plan benefits from a wealth of knowledge and best practices in climate planning and action. This methodology provided a step-wise approach to address climate change adaptation and mitigation. Its nine steps are organised into three phases: Analyse, Act, and Accelerate. Ahmedabad embarked on a climate-resilient journey through this ninestep process. AMC committed to climate action by forming a City Climate Core Team to lead the charge. A Stakeholder Committee was constituted to seek inputs from and involve the community, including the private sector, in the implementation of the action plan. The city’s socio-environmental landscape and key challenges were identified, climate risks and vulnerability assessed, and emissions baselines established. Goals, strategies, and actions that address identified climate vulnerabilities and thematic actions to reduce GHG emissions across sectors, were defined. To guide emissions reduction strategies across three different planning horizons, i.e., 2030, 2050 and 2070, the CRCAP scales the defined goals, strategies, and actions across two emissions reduction scenarios.

plan_name: Plan de Clima y Energía Sostenible de Vitoria-Gasteiz (PACES 2030)

Taking the findings from the powers, policies and performance analyses as a whole, key findings and recommendations for GMM are summarised below: •The city is struggling with rapid urban development, increasing water and energy use and growing demand for waste disposal because of the unusually high levels of population growth partly due to the Syrian migrant crisis.•Across all sectors and topics, there is limited or no evidence of a completed policy cycle whereby plans, policies and decisions are implemented through comprehensive, consistent enforcement and monitoring to ensure that they drive positive change and to improve future policies and decision-making. A system of enforcement and monitoring is needed that will not only provide more detail on policy implementation and lead to future policy improvement but can also help tackle, through appropriate survey and information gathering, the problems resulting from the refugee situation.•There is limited control over energy efficiency in buildings not owned or operated by GMM, in particular existing rather than new buildings (given that construction incentives are available for the latter); similarly, there is only limited control over the energy sector, which tends to be centralised in ownership and operation for large-scale infrastructure (albeit GMM has the power to own and operate smaller-scale generation, as noted in Table 0.1 above). GMM also has only partial influence over energy policy-setting, budget and revenue.•The municipality could build on the excellent and creative policies developed so far on energy efficiency in GMM-owned buildings and on green design for new buildings – for example, by introducing incentives for retrofitting of buildings and extending policies to buildings in private ownership. •Investments made in Organised Industrial Zones and/or industrial buildings are primarily private sector led, on the basis of central government industrial and economic policies, thus constraining industrial oversight, visioning and monitoring by GMM.•GMM and/or relevant partners should lobby central government for further decentralisation of key powers to municipalities where there may be sustainability benefits in doing so, including for example the ability to own, operate and invest in medium to large-scale energy infrastructure and regulating and investing in industrial zones.

plan_comments: El proceso de planificación estratégica, implementación y seguimiento participativo a través de la búsqueda de consensos intersectoriales y participación ciudadana han sido base para la construcción democrática. El desarrollo del PLAC, su implementación ha sido llevado adelante por la Secretaría de Ambiente y Espacio Público bajo la responsabilidad de la Dirección General de Acción Climática y Calidad Ambiental de la Municipalidad de Rosario. Para la actualización de los ejes, priorización de las acciones y la conformación de una agenda climática se convocaron diversos espacios: Gabinete Local de Cambio Climático y Transición Ecológica Justa, conformado por dos espacios de trabajo, Gabinete político y Gabinete técnico; y el Comité Asesor frente al Cambio Climático (mencionados anteriormente).El Plan Local de Acción Climática de Rosario ha sido el resultado de un proceso participativo de reflexión y consenso, con involucramiento de la ciudadanía, con enfoque interdisciplinario en la planificación y en la búsqueda de soluciones, y con una base técnica y científica, para el diseño de políticas y actuaciones que apunten a construir territorios más sustentables, equilibrados y cohesionados socialmente. Funciona como instrumento guía de la política climática frente al calentamiento global, incluyendo la política de mitigación -reducción de emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero y mejora de los sumideros- y de adaptación -ajuste al clima real o proyectado moderando los efectos-.  Para la elaboración del PLAC se tomó como base una serie de postulados y acuerdos internacionales, tales como: el Acuerdo de París de la Convención Marco de Naciones Unidas sobre Cambio Climático (CMNUCC), los informes más recientes del Panel Intergubernamental de Expertos sobre Cambio Climático (IPCC, por sus siglas en inglés), la Agenda 2030 y los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible (ODS), la Nueva Agenda Urbana y diferentes acuerdos internacionales a los que ha adherido o suscrito la ciudad de Rosario.

plan_name: POLÍTICA AMBIENTAL DE CAMBIO CLIMÁTICO Y TRANSICIÓN ENERGÉTICA DE BUCARAMANGA
plan_comments: El plan estratégico  de la Política Pública Ambiental de Cambio climático y transición energética fue construido intersectorialmente, de manera que se garantice el desarrollo bajo en carbono y positivo con los recursos naturales para el bienestar de los habitantes de Bucaramanga, articulando los esfuerzos y las acciones en un diálogo continuo entre los agentes, sectores, las instituciones y los diferentes actores sociales, que tengan competencia en el tema.De acuerdo con lo anterior, con miras al logro del objetivo general de la presente política, se proponen 6 objetivos enmarcados dentro de cada eje temático, bajo las cuales se definen las líneas estratégicas concretas de implementación. Este documento viene acompañado por el Anexo 1, en el cual se detallan las líneas estratégicas, así como las metas y acciones propuestas bajo el Plan estratégico, junto con los indicadores y respectivos actores vinculados.

Es importante mencionar que nos encontramos en la actualización de nuestro Plan de Acción Climática Municipal, los nuevos Objetivos, medidas y estrategias, estarán basados en los resultados del Inventario de Emisiones de Gases y Compuestos de Efecto Invernadero con año base 2020, en los resultados del Análisis de Vulnerabilidad y Riesgo de la ciudad de Mérida, y de los resultados de las mesas de trabajo que se harán con los representantes de los diversos sectores (energía,  residuos, transporte, forestal). De igual manera, es la primera vez que planteamos una meta de mitigación basada en ciencia. El nuevo PACMUN deberá estar listo a finales del presente año (2023).

El Plan Integral de Movilidad Urbana Sustentable de Mérida  (PIMUS-Mérida) es el resultado de un esfuerzo de colaboración local, nacional e internacional que, coordinado por el Instituto Municipal de Planeación de Mérida, presenta una visión de vanguardia, unos objetivos alcanzables y unas estrategias consensuadas para orientar los proyectos, las acciones y las políticas públicas en materia de movilidad, en nuestro Municipio. La movilidad urbana es uno de los temas más relevantes para los Meridanos ya que sus impactos van desde la vida cotidiana hasta la prosperidad y el posicionamiento de Mérida en el plano global. Así fue expresado en el Plan Municipal de Desarrollo 2018-2021 que, en tres de sus ejes estratégicos, aborda el tema; razón por la cual se compromete a atender esta prioridad ciudadana. Reconocemos que existen antecedentes locales en planeación de la movilidad urbana dignos de ser aprovechados, y que hay diferentes maneras válidas de elaborar planes; así que optamos por hacerlo nosotros mismos. Un Plan no resuelve los problemas, más bien, nos orienta para resolverlos. Así que presentar este PIMUS-Mérida sólo es un paso más en un camino a la solución de los problemas de la movilidad urbana. Así lo entendemos, por lo que el sector público municipal no se quedará rezagado en sus responsabilidades; más bien, las acepta y se compromete a hacer su parte. Además, dará seguimiento al logro de los objetivos planteados y evaluará los resultados; es decir, acompañará el proceso. Por principio de cuentas, se reconoce como actor clave y no como espectador. Conoce sus atribuciones y sus recursos y los pone al servicio de la ciudadanía.

plan_name: Plan Municipal de Infraestructura Verde Actualización Mérida 2021-2024
plan_comments: Las ciudades son territorios importantes para la implementación de medidas de mitigación al cambio climático, porque en ellas se concentra el gasto energético, las actividades humanas y la producción de gases de efecto invernadero debido a la conversión del hábitat natural como causa principal de la pérdida de la biodiversidad. Asimismo, es en las ciudades donde la gente experimenta efectos del cambio climático, temperaturas extremas, estrés y malestar que esto genera, escasez de agua o inundaciones, y donde se incrementan riesgos para la salud humana. Mitigar los efectos del cambio climático es de vital importancia. La infraestructura verde es un concepto que surge de la necesidad de valorar al medio ambiente de manera distinta. Este punto ha ido evolucionando en los últimos 12 años, siendo que hoy en día tiene un papel relevante en la planeación, la gestión, y operación urbana para hacer que una ciudad sea biodiversa y resiliente. La infraestructura verde es un indicador referente de salud y bienestar para la vida en la ciudad. Pensar en los recursos naturales como "infraestructura verde", nos ayuda a reconocer la importancia que tiene la naturaleza como soporte vital para una existencia saludable.En Mérida construimos una nueva visión de sustentabilidad, porque sabemos la importancia de reconocer que es la propia naturaleza quien resguarda a la ciudad. Con esta actualización del Tercer Plan Municipal de Infraestructura Verde seguimos progresando, alcanzando una mayor transversalidad temática y temporal, con un mayor acercamiento al territorio, planteando accionesaplicables a nivel de vivienda, vialidades, parques, barrios, ciudad y municipio.

plan_name: Clark County 2018 Multi-Jurisdictional Hazard Mitigation Plan
plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: The Hazard Mitigation Plan includes all Clark County Nevada jurisdictions, which are as follows
plan_comments: As defined in Title 44 of the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), Subpart M, Section 206.401, hazard  mitigation is “any action taken to reduce or eliminate the long-term risk to human life and property from natural hazards.” As such, hazard mitigation is any work to minimize the impacts of any type of hazard event before it occurs. Hazard mitigation aims to reduce losses from future disasters. It is a process in which hazards are identified and profiled, the people and facilities at risk are analyzed, and mitigation actions to reduce or eliminate hazard risk are developed. The implementation of the mitigation actions, which include short and long-term strategies that may involve planning, policy changes, programs, projects, and other activities, is the end result of this process.  To meet the requirements of the DMA 2000, Clark County (the County) has prepared a Multi-jurisdictional Hazard Mitigation Plan (HMP; hereinafter referred to as the 2018 HMP) to assess risks posed by natural and human-caused hazards and to develop mitigation action plans for reducing the risks in Clark County, Nevada. The 2018 HMP replaces the HMP that the County prepared in 2012. The following jurisdictions are included in the 2018 HMP: Unincorporated Clark County, city of Boulder City, city of Henderson, city of Las Vegas, city of Mesquite, city of North Las Vegas, Clark County School District (CCSD), and the Clark County Water Reclamation District (CCWRD). The 2018 HMP also includes tribal annexes for the Las Vegas Paiute Tribe and the Moapa Band of Paiutes. The 2018 HMP is organized to follow FEMA’s Local Mitigation Plan Review Tool (April 2017) which demonstrates how local HMPs meet the DMA 2000 regulations. As such, specific planning elements of this review tool are in their appropriate plan sections. Jurisdiction-specific information, including a vulnerability analysis and mitigation strategy, is located in Appendices F-M. A full list of chapter and appendix titles can be found in the table of contents. The tribal annexes are included as Annex A and Annex B. These annexes address the forthcoming (2018) Tribal Mitigation Plan Review Tool.

plan_name: Estratégia Municipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas -
plan_comments: Elaborada no âmbito do programa ClimAdaPT.Local, a Estratégia Municipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas de Évora foca-se na identificação de opções e ações de adaptação que visam promover a minimização dos efeitos das alterações climáticas. A partir da identificação e priorização das atuais vulnerabilidades e riscos climáticos e da sua projeção até ao final do século, o município de Évora procura promover um conjunto integrado de opções de adaptação para responder não apenas ao clima futuro e às consequências desse novo cenário, mas igualmente aos diferentes impactos climáticos já observados. https://www.cm-evora.pt/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/EMAAC_Evora_2017.pdf O município de Évora também dispõem de um Plano Intermunicipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas (2017) que visa dar a conhecer melhor o fenómeno das alterações climáticas ao nível local e sub-regional e, simultaneamente, identificar as ações necessárias para a adaptação das populações, entidades e serviços públicos aos cenários climáticos. Tem igualmente como objetivo promover a integração da adaptação às alterações climáticas no planeamento municipal e intermunicipal e, dessa forma, criar uma cultura de adaptação transversal aos vários setores e atores sub-regionais, reforçando a resiliência territorial.Atualmente está em elaboração o Plano Municipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas que pretende elevar a Estratégia Municipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas a Plano Municipal de Adaptação às Alterações Climáticas, nomeadamente aprofundando o conhecimento técnico e científico sobre o clima local atual e futuro, sobre a incidência espacial e sectorial das vulnerabilidades climáticas específicas do concelho, desenhando um plano de ação municipal facilmente operacionalizável na próxima década, contando novamente com o envolvimento da comunidade local e  incluindo a comunidade educativa.Atualmente está em elaboração o Plano de Ação Para a Sustentabilidade Urbana Évora 2030, enquadrado na iniciativa europeia Acordo Cidade Verde. Este plano pretende dar continuidade à estratégia do município ao nível da sustentabilidade ambiental, em concreto nos domínios do ar, água, natureza e biodiversidade, economia circular e resíduos, e ruído. Para tal, será estabelecido  um programa de ações que integram a componente de adaptação e mitigação, incluindo eficiência energética.

plan_comments: Peter Nierengarten, Sustainability DirectorLeif Olson, Long Range PlannerRachael Schaffner, Sustainability CoordinatorAnna Travis, Technical AssistantHadley Cook, Technical Assistant

This plan updates and expands upon Spokane’s 2009 Sustainability Action Plan. It identifies specific strategies and actions that will help meet our 100% renewable energy goals as adopted by the City in 2018, and to meet regulation set by the State of Washington to address greenhouse gas emissions.While climate change is a global issue, it requires action at every level, including local action. We believe that Spokane has much to gain by embracing the opportunities presented by this challenge.With this in mind, we are taking an approach to sustainability planning that balances the need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions with our commitment to create good jobs, a healthy environment and an equitable community.For example, in partnership with Avista Utilities, the Spokane Regional Transportation Council, and Urbanova, we have won a $2.5 million grantto install electric vehicle charging stations throughout Spokane County as part of a regionwide transportation electrification (TE) plan. The implementation of this regional TE plan will reduce emissions, cut transportation costs, create jobs, and bring more opportunities to historically underserved areas of our city.While this Sustainability and Climate Action Plan helps our community become environmentally responsible, it will also help us become more resilient by preparing our community for impacts caused by changing weather patterns. It will help us incentivize the development of a local clean energy economy and secure the economic benefits of becoming a resilient city. With smart mitigation efforts, we’ll be able to protect the economic and social benefits of our local tourism industry, beloved public events like Bloomsday, regional farming, the Spokane River, and our aquifer.We hope that this plan will contribute to what Spokane has been — a hub of prosperous innovation, collaboration, and creativity — built through the efforts of community members with the vision and courage to shape their own future

In 2020, Mayor Kincannon convened the Mayor’s Climate Council to identify and evaluate strategies to achieve the community emission goal established by Council in 2019. The cross-sector group of over 65 members included clean energy experts, conservation activists, advocates for equity, neighborhood representatives, developers, business leaders, utility representatives, and more. Council members and working groups (transportation, buildings/energy, waste, and equity) evaluated dozens of strategies for their impact to climate, economy, and community.  Strategies were ranked not only by emission reduction but also by impact on equity and other societal benefits (air quality, public health, mobility and access, housing, human services, and civic engagement).  The strategies ranked highest by the Council formed the basis for the City's 2021 Energy & Sustainability Work Plan, including:- Expand and improve bicycle and pedestrian facilities, connectivity, convenience, and/or safety to significantly increase trips taken by walking or biking- Make public transit investments that significantly enhance coverage, service quality, frequency, and/or speed- Significantly accelerate community adoption of electric vehicles- Partner with major local commercial fleet operators to transition to electric vehicles- Create voluntary large, commercial, and multi-family energy upgrade programs (eg. incentives, technical assistance) that achieve deep energy savings (~25%+) in ~20%+ of buildings- Amplify and opportunistically expand voluntary home energy upgrade programs that will achieve deep energy savings (~25%+) in 20% of homes- Develop or amplify opportunities to invest in renewables at scale, including community solar and in-valley investments- Research and develop strategies to promote opportunistic electrification (eg. replacement of fossil fuel-fired furnaces with electric heat pump technologies) in existing buildings over time- Pursue food waste reduction and collection programs that capture ~80%+ of organic waste from all high-volume locations (eg. restaurants)- Increase residential recycling through community engagement and education strategies- Promote consumption-reduction approaches such as sharing and re-use through education campaigns- Research pathways to offer organic food/yard waste collection for single-family and multi-family residential properties
It is a climate change mitigation plan. Some metrics are based on the national census, therefore complete community GHG emissions can only be reported every 5 years.

It is a climate change adaptation plan, some metrics are more difficult to quantify than mitigation because they might be dependent on many factors and may not indicate progress from our actions.  For example - we would like to reduce the risk of vector-borne diseases.  We can track the number of West Nile Virus mosquito ponds but if that number goes down, it  might be because of a dry year, not because of any mitigation efforts on our part.

Our Climate Future is the product of bringing together the climate action, renewable and energy efficiency, as well as zero waste goals into one comprehensive climate-, energy, and waste-focused, people-forward plan. The process to develop the plan included extensive community engagement from residents (including members from vulnerable populations) and business stakeholders, which resulted in the plan’s Big Moves (the visionary 2030 outcomes). The Next Moves outlined in the plan are the action steps required to meet the 13 broader goals. An annual report is produced and shared to show the progress on the Big and Next Moves, and approximately every 2 years, the City will create a new tactical plan to update the actions and priorities for the next two years. As assessment needs are discussed, the City is engaging community members and piloting opportunities to ensure vulnerable groups’ voices are part of the evaluation and assessment processes.

plan_boundary_desc: Smaller boundary: The MSAP only covers City operations; it does not cover community-related climate and sustainability goals
plan_comments: The Municipal Sustainability and Adaptation Plan (MSAP) outlines the City’s internal sustainability goals specifically for City buildings, services, and staff, to ensure City operations and employees are prepared to be resilient in the future. An overarching theme of the MSAP is “leading by example,” using the City’s goals and efforts to show the community that they are “walking the walk” when it comes to sustainability and climate goals and actions. The MSAP was developed with participation from staff across all Service Areas and departments to ensure that all needs and business processes were considered. The six goals of the MSAP are We are Resilient; Our Public Lands Thrive; We are Water Smart; We are Zero Waste; We are Carbon Neutral; and We are a World Class Workplace. Within each goal, there are objectives set and strategies outlined to successfully meet the objectives. The MSAP is only a 5-year plan and is currently being re-evaluated and updated. With this update, staff are seeking better alignment with the community-facing Our Climate Future plan so that the City can better connect the dots on how it's leading by example in sustainability, climate mitigation, and resilience.

plan_name: 2012 Albany Climate Action Plan (Appendix D of the Albany 2030 Plan)
plan_comments: The City of Albany identified the following actions to address greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from the community and to prepare for a changing climate. The Albany 2030 Comprehensive Plan includes an underlying theme of sustainability. Therefore, the plan development process, which included rigorous engagement of key stakeholders and the public at large, lent itself to the identification of climate protection strategies. The City reviewed all of the Albany 2030 strategies and highlighted those that directly impact the reduction of GHG emissions or the enhancement the City’s resiliency to the projected impacts of climate change. The determination was made based on whether the intent of each strategy, when implemented, would result in a reduction of GHG emissions or the enhancement of the City’s resiliency to a changing climate. Once the existing strategies were identified, the City worked with the Community Advisory Committee, consisting of members of the Albany community with diverse expertise in building energy, transportation, wastewater treatment, and natural resources, to identify and make recommendations to address any gaps in the existing strategies.

Palo Alto established its first Climate Protection Plan in 2007. In 2020, the City launched an update to the Sustainability and Climate Action Plan (S/CAP) to develop the strategies needed to meet our sustainability goals, including our goal of reducing GHG emissions 80 percent below 1990 levels by 2030 (the “80 x 30” goal) and most recent Carbon Neutral by 2030 goal. The S/CAP is a comprehensive document laying out the City's strategy to achieve these ambitious carbon reduction goals, while improving our natural environment, adapting to climate impacts, and increasing livability for Palo Alto residents. While reducing emissions is a priority for the City, Palo Alto believes in a comprehensive view of sustainability. The S/CAP addresses eight focus areas: Climate Action, Energy, Mobility, Electric Vehicles, Water, Climate Adaptation and Sea Level Rise, Natural Environment, and Zero Waste. Each focus area sets specific goals, lays out key actions to the City will take to achieve these goals, and establishes key performance indicators that will be monitored and reported on yearly to track the City's progress towards achieving our goals.

Note*City of Fremont is currently in the process of adopting the Climate Action Plan (CAP 2.0). The attached plan attached is a public-facing draft of the Climate Action Plan (CAP 2.0)-, entitled Climate Ready Fremont. Recognizing the need to update its GHG target, in 2019, the city adopted a Carbon Neutrality Resolution for Fremont to achieve a 55 percent GHG emission reduction from a 2005 baseline level by the year 2030 and to become a carbon neutral city no later than 2045. As part of the Carbon Neutrality Resolution, the City developed a Post-Carbon Framework to guide an update to the City’s first Climate Action Plan.The Post-Carbon Framework establishes the following guiding values that serve as the foundation of Climate Ready Fremont: • Equity & Access: Ensure that all people have the opportunity to benefit equally from climate solutions, while not taking on an unequal burden of climate impacts.• Efficiency & Innovation: Promote the efficient use of resources and the adoption of clean and climate-smart technologies and techniques.• Health & Wellness: Safeguard and enhance the ability of the community to live, work, play, connect, and thrive in a healthy social and physical environment.• Resiliency & Capacity-Building: Provide education and training on the opportunities offered by a more resilient future and encourage sustainable behaviors across all sectors of the community.In addition, the Post-Carbon Framework identifies seven key strategies that are incorporated throughout Climate Ready Fremont. The strategies are, Electrification & Fossil fuel Phase Out, Clean & Renewable Power, Climate adaptation & resilience, Resource Conservation & Elimination of Waste, Mobility & Connectivity, Carbon Sequestration, and Restorative Ecology & Green Infrastructure.

plan_name: "武汉市应对气候变化“十四五”规划
plan_comments: "《武汉市应对气候变化“十四五”规划》是武汉市生态环境局制定的一份指导我市在“十四五”时期应对气候变化的纲领性文件。《规划》以习近平生态文明思想为指导，贯彻新发展理念，落实党中央、国务院，省委、省政府关于应对气候变化工作部署，协同推进我市应对气候变化与生态环境保护相关工作，促进经济社会发展全面绿色转型。《规划》提出了指导思想、基本原则和总体目标，设置了11项指标，部署了28项重点任务，提出了保障措施。《规划》的总体目标是：到2025年，应对气候变化和生态环境保护协同增效局面基本形成。具体包括：严格控制温室气体排放。单位地区生产总值二氧化碳排放降低完成省下达目标，甲烷、氧化亚氮等非二氧化碳温室气体排放管控力度逐步增强。提升气候变化适应能力。海绵城市建设全域推进，城市防洪排涝能力显著提高，城市绿地覆盖率达到45%以上，林业碳汇能力显著增强。加强治理体系和治理能力现代化建设。完善应对气候变化工作领导机制，加大投入力度，严格考核评估，深化公众参与。持续推进和深化试点示范。深化国家低碳城市试点和国家气候适应型城市试点建设，推动碳市场建设和运行，开展国家气候投融资试点。深化应对气候变化合作交流。加强与国际机构、科研院所、友城等的交流合作，积极参与国际多边和双边合作机制。《规划》的实施成本主要包括以下几个方面：资金投入。《规划》提出了加大投入力度的要求，包括加大财政支持力度、鼓励社会资本参与、引导金融机构支持等。具体的资金需求量需要根据各项任务的实施方案进行测算。技术支撑。《规划》提出了推动低碳科技、低碳产业、碳交易、碳金融等业态快速发展的要求，需要依靠先进的技术手段和创新能力来实现。这方面需要加强科技研发和人才培养，提高技术水平和转化效率。制度保障。《规划》提出了完善应对气候变化工作领导机制、严格考核评估、深化公众参与等要求，需要建立健全相关的法律法规、政策措施、监督机制等。这方面需要加强立法工作和执法监管工作，形成有利于应对气候变化的制度环境。社会共识。《规划》提出了倡导绿色低碳生活、营造绿色低碳新时尚等要求，需要广泛宣传和普及应对气候变化的知识和意义，增强公众的参与意识和行动力。这方面需要加强教育培训和舆论引导工作，形成有利于应对气候变化的社会氛围。"

plan_name: "武汉市推动降碳及发展低碳产业工作方案
plan_comments: "《武汉市推动降碳及发展低碳产业工作方案》是武汉市为了贯彻落实党中央、国务院和省委、省人民政府关于碳达峰、碳中和目标和加快建立健全绿色低碳循环发展经济体系的重大决策部署，进一步深化全国低碳城市试点示范，加快打造“五个中心”、建设现代化大武汉而制定的一项工作方案。该方案提出了总体目标、工作任务、保障措施等内容，涵盖了能源结构调整、重点领域和行业低碳化、碳金融发展、低碳新风尚营造等多个方面，旨在为实现碳达峰、碳中和目标提供政策支持和行动指南。该方案的总体目标是以习近平新时代中国特色社会主义思想为指导，全面贯彻落实习近平生态文明思想，坚持以经济社会发展全面绿色转型为引领，以加快打造“五个中心”、建设现代化大武汉为目标导向，提升产业、能源、工业、建筑、交通等重点领域低碳化水平和生态系统碳汇水平，积极倡导和培育绿色生活方式，构建绿色金融多层次多元化支持体系，全面推进武汉市高质量发展，率先实现绿色崛起。该方案的工作任务包括四个方面：（一）大力调整优化能源结构。主要措施包括压减全市煤炭消费总量，大力发展可再生能源，重点推动氢能示范应用。（二）推动重点领域、重点行业率先实现“3060”目标。主要措施包括重点领域、重点行业尽快制订“3060”行动方案，强化重大工程项目碳评估，全力做好工业领域节能减排，不断提升交通领域低碳化，大力推广超低能耗建筑和可再生能源技术等绿色建筑应用，稳步提升森林碳汇能力。（三）努力打造全国碳金融中心。主要措施包括持续推进和深化碳市场建设，围绕全国碳排放权注册登记结算系统创新金融市场发展，打造以气候投融资为核心的绿色金融体系，推进设立绿色金融线上综合服务平台。（四）充分营造全社会低碳新风尚。主要措施包括创建“3060”先锋联盟及产学研联盟，开展低碳宣传教育活动，鼓励公众参与低碳行动，加强国际交流与合作。该方案的保障措施包括完善组织领导机制，强化政策法规制度建设，加大财政资金投入支持力度，加强科技创新和人才队伍建设，加强监测评估和考核奖惩，加强宣传引导和舆论监督。"

plan_name: "《武汉市低碳发展“十三五”规划》
plan_comments: "《武汉市低碳发展“十三五”规划》的实施有助于减缓未来气候升温的影响，主要体现在以下几个方面：通过调整产业结构，助力经济低碳转型，提高高新技术产业、服务业和战略性新兴产业的比重，降低高耗能行业的比重，减少二氧化碳排放强度。通过发展低碳能源，不断优化能源结构，压减煤炭消费总量，大力发展可再生能源和清洁能源，提高非化石能源消费比重，降低单位能源消费二氧化碳排放。通过发展绿色工业，推进工业节能降耗，加强工业企业节能管理和技术改造，推广应用先进节能技术和装备，提高工业用能效率和资源利用率。通过推广绿色建筑，实现建筑低碳发展，全面执行建筑节能标准和绿色建筑评价标准，推动超低能耗建筑和可再生能源建筑应用，加强既有公共建筑节能改造。通过发展绿色交通，构建低碳交通体系，提升公共交通分担率和服务水平，推广应用新能源汽车和清洁能源汽车，加快建设自行车道路网络和公共自行车系统。通过提升城市碳汇，打造生态低碳城市，加强山水林田湖草系统治理和自然保护区建设，增加森林覆盖率和蓄积量，提高城市绿化覆盖率和人均公园绿地面积。通过倡导低碳生活，努力构建低碳社会，开展低碳宣传教育活动，鼓励公众参与低碳行动，培育绿色消费理念和习惯。通过深化国际合作，搭建交流合作平台，积极参与国际气候治理进程，加强与国内外城市、机构、企业的合作交流，引进利用国际先进技术、资金、管理经验。以上措施有利于减少温室气体排放量和强度，增加碳汇能力和适应能力，缓解气候变化对武汉市的不利影响，促进武汉市绿色低碳高质量发展。"

"《武汉市海绵城市专项规划（2016-2030）》指出，武汉市是一个典型的内陆城市，受气候变化影响明显，近年来极端天气事件频发，如暴雨、内涝、干旱、高温等，给城市生态环境和社会经济发展带来了严重挑战。《规划》以“海绵城市建设”为主题，提出了“小雨不积水、大雨不内涝、水体不黑臭、热岛有缓解”的目标，通过建设一批海绵设施和项目，提高城市对降雨径流的收集、利用、排放和净化能力，增强城市水资源保障能力和水生态功能，改善城市微气候和人居环境。《规划》根据武汉市的自然条件、城市发展特点和海绵建设需求，将全市划分为171个海绵城市建设分区，并按照“全域统筹、分区分期”的原则，确定了近期（2016-2020年）和远期（2021-2030年）的海绵建设范围和目标。《规划》针对不同类型的海绵建设分区，提出了相应的海绵建设措施和技术方案，包括建筑小区、城市道路、公园绿地、港渠水系等四大类别，以及雨水花园、透水铺装、生态湿地、雨水花洒等多种形式。《规划》还从规划管控、技术标准、投资与运营等方面提出了海绵城市建设的保障措施，并结合国家和省级的相关政策法规，制定了海绵城市建设的评价标准和考核机制。"

plan_comments: The Local Climate Change Action Plan in Davao City covers a diverse range of sectors, addressing climate change impacts, promoting sustainable practices, and enhancing adaptive capacity accross all sectors. The plan addresses the specific challenges and opportunities within each sector, aiming to promote sustainable practices, enhance resilience to climate change impacts, and foster adaptive capacity. By considering these sectors, the Action Plan strives to address the diverse range of climate change challenges faced by Davao City and promote sustainable development across various industries and sectors.

plan_name: Davao City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan 2020-2025 /
plan_comments: Davao City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan 2020-2025 addresses a wide range of sectors, providing sector-specific strategies and actions to enhance disaster resilience. By considering the unique risks and challenges faced by each sector, the plan aims to build a more resilient and disaster-ready city.

The Davao City Climate Disaster Risk Assessment (CDRA) covers a wide range of sectors to evaluate the risks and vulnerabilities associated with climate-related disasters. The CDRA aims to identify potential impacts and vulnerabilities within each sector, helping to inform decision-making, prioritize adaptation strategies, and enhance resilience to climate-related risks in Davao City.

The Transport Roadmap provides the city with an overarching local plan and builds on the work of the Department of Transportation (DOTr), ADB, Japanese International Cooperation Agency (JICA), and others to support the development of sustainable and integrated network of land-based transportation in the city. The purpose of the Roadmap is to support the improvement of transportation so that it prioritizes modes according to their economic, environmental and community sustainability. The Roadmap sets the framework, over the next 20 years with a focus on the first 10 years, for transport networks and systems to be integrated and be more efficient.It also provides a mechanism to engage and partner with transport agencies to influence traffic and transport systems in the City of Davao.The Roadmap was developed based on best practice research and includes a human centered approach where walking, bicycle riding and public transport are considered a desirable basis of all trips, while supporting economic activity (including freight movements), urban and rural communities and tourism. It is also evidence-based and informed through consultations to balance and prioritize the needs of everyone in the city. The plan includes specific, targeted actions that can be measured against their delivery.

plan_name: Davao City Agriculture and Fishery Development Plan -
plan_comments: The development of the agriculture and fishery sector is a priority for governments worldwide. It has been recognized as a potential vehicle for attaining the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the New Urban Agenda (Habitat 3) of the United Nations. The sector can contribute to efforts in poverty alleviation, food security, and sustainability. At the local level, the sector can also contribute to the 10-point Agenda of the current administration.In Davao City, the importance of the sector is underscored by the employment it generates, its contribution to the economy, and its role as a source of food that can help sustain the health of the population. The preparation of the Davao City Agriculture and Fishery Development Plan for 2018-2022 (DACFDP) is an effort to guide the development of the sector and ensure that it will contribute to the agenda of the government. The DCAFDP is an update of the 2000-2004 Agricultural Development Plan (ADEP) and was conducted to address recent changes in the economy, society, and the environment. The key issues that need to be addressed are food security, poverty alleviation, health and nutrition, climate change, and sustainability.

Quezon City’s Enhanced Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) 2021-2050 is grounded by a far-reaching aspiration for carbon neutrality, climate resilience, and inclusivity, aligning with the City’s vision of a “Quality Community – a progressive and peaceful, clean and orderly place, conducive and hospitable to living, employment, and business.”Quezon City's Enhanced Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) 2021-2050 aligns with the National Climate Change Action Plan (NCCAP) of the Philippines. Consistent with the NCCAP, Quezon City's Enhanced Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) has identified the following wider opportunities and benefits as defined in its outcome statements:1. Increased availability, stability, and accessibility of safe and healthy food2. Increased availability, stability, and equitability of access to water resources3. Optimized urban water systems management contributing to flooding mitigation4. Reduced waste impact or pressure on the environment and ecosystems, aiming for 50% diversion rate by 20305. Reduced effect of urban heat and drought on Quezon City6. Reduced vulnerability of men and women and other vulnerable groups (children, elderly, and persons with disability, etc.)7. Increased accessibility of key public services to men and women and other vulnerable groups8. Increase in green, energy-efficient, and resilient buildings and establishments in Quezon City, targeting 80-100% of building permits granted being compliant to the updated Green Building Code by 20259. Availability of accessible, clean, and affordable renewable energy, targeting 100% solarization coverage for all city-owned buildings and facilities by 203010. Increased climate-proofed, rehabilitated, and improved ecologically-efficient technologies11. Increased uptake of active mobility, tripling cycling rate from 2.2% in 2016 to 6.6% of total modal share in 2030 and expanding bike lanes from 55 km in 2016 to 350 km by 203012. Increased uptake of mass public transport, with bus modal share increasing from 6.6% in 2016 to 8.5% in 2030 and railway modal share increasing from 4.2% in 2016 to 6.0% in 203013. Strengthened institutional and individual technical capacities of city departments, external stakeholders and community members to deliver the Enhanced Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) 2021-2050

Muntinlupa Enhanced Climate Change Action Plan
plan_comments: The Muntinlupa Climate Change Action Plan (MCCAP) covering the period 2017 – 2027, is in response to the urgency for action on climate change as mandated by Philippine Republic Act 9729, also known as the Climate Change Act of 2009. The MCCAP outlines the specific programs and strategies for adaptation and mitigation for 2017 to 2027 and provides key actions that enhance adaptive capacity and resilience of communities and natural ecosystems to climate change, adopts the total economic valuation of natural resources while ensuring biodiversity conservation, and recognizes its competitiveness.

plan_name: Makati City Local Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP)
plan_comments: Pursuant to Republic Act No. 9727 or the Philippine Climate Change Act of 2009, the City formulated the Local Climate Change Action Plan and adopted  through City Ordinance No. 2017-076. Currently, the LCCAP is in the process of updating this year. As mentioned, LCCAP monitoring is set annually and evaluated every three years. The annual monitoring sets the direction of what to prioritize and how much budget to allocate every year. Evaluation will focus on efficiency, effectiveness, and impact.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

plan_name: Makati City Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Management Framework Plan
plan_comments: The Framework Plan sets the city's GHG management agenda. It served as the blueprint to reduce future emissions through policy initiatives, projects, and program of activities. Presently, the city is in the process of updating its GHG Management Plan.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The Makati City Council passed City Ordinance No. 2018-080 to update city's Drainage Master Plan for the period 2017-2019. The plan provides framework to identify the existing deficiencies and develop drainage solutions to address such deficiencies, reduce flooding, resolve public safety concerns and improve maintenance opportunities throughout the City of Makati. The Makati Drainage Master Plan is subject to regular monitoring, evaluation, and coordination with other drainage master plans of the adjacent Local Government Units within the National Capital Region for a more effective and holistic approach and interventions that will greatly improve the flood receding time in the City of Makati and neighboring LGU's as well.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The City of Makati through the Task Force Manila Bay Clean Up, Preservation and Rehabilitation (otherwise known as the Task Force Makati Waterways) has been established to steer its compliance with the Supreme Court Mandamus/Ruling.  The Task Force formulated the Makati Waterways Master Plan to help  integrate all related measures, which would result in a more comprehensive waterways management program for the City's waterways. This will also serve as the baseline output for the preparation/updating of Makati’s Water Management Plan.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

plan_name: Makati City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan
plan_comments: Makati DRRM Plan integrates the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030) and ensures mainstreaming of Disaster Risk Reduction and Climate Change Adaptation in the different sectors of the city, inclusion of Gender and Development (GAD) sensitive/responsive measures, and alignment with other city development plans.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

plan_name: Makati Contingency Plan for Hydrometeorological Hazards
plan_comments: The Makati City Flood Contingency Plan seeks to prepare the City of Makati for the worst-case scenario of a 200 year return period flooding. By taking an inventory of existing resources, personnel and systems, the City is able to determine gaps and areas of weakness and therefore develop a contingency plan to address the needs of the affected population and the City in this scenario.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The Makati CDP serves as a guide for the City’s development which includes sectoral mainstreaming of climate change with priority programs and projects pertaining to climate change. The Republic Act (RA) 9279 or the Philippine Climate Change Act of 2009, Hyogo Framework for Action (HFA) and RA 10121 Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010 were also considered in crafting the City’s Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP). Makati has several areas that are considered vulnerable to geological and hydro meteorological hazards. Through the CDP, the City will formulate programs to improve the disaster resiliency of these identified vulnerable communities.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

Also highlighted is the presentation of the trends and conditions of each of these City’s development sectors, which could be used not just by policymakers in their decision-making but also by other concerned and interested parties. Finally, this atlas shall serve as more than just a mere reference but as a tool in analyzing the current state of the City that is necessary for its current and upcoming various planning activities.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

It presents a vision for the future, with long-range goals and objectives for all programs, projects, and activities that affect the local government. This includes guidance on how to make decisions on public and private land development proposals, expenditure of public funds, tax policies, among others.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the Ordinance are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The Makati ELA is also the framework of the City’s strategic directions and provides for the explicit expression of the present administration’s goals, objectives, strategic priorities, and programs that are consistent with the LGU’s major policy directives.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The Plan will be used as the basis and guide for the implementation of the future development projects, programs, and activities of each sector that will hopefully lead towards the formulation of more relevant and significant environmental-related plans.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. plan_name: Makati City 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan
plan_comments: The thrust of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan is sustainable development through active participation of all stakeholders. The approach to the formulation and implementation of the programs and projects identified in the plan is empowerment through public-private partnerships of all waste generators, collaboration or the establishment of strong active linkages between the waste generators and the different agencies of the city government and national government, and the continuous capacity building for the proper and sustainable implementation of the plan.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

The AIP of the Department of Environmental Services covers the delivery of environmental services to the residents of the city. This includes the solid waste management, urban greening, pollution control and regulation, environmental planning and technical assistance, operationalization of environmental special bodies, IEC, climate change program, among others.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

plan_name: Makati City El Niño - South Oscillation (ENSO) Contingency Plan -
plan_comments: The El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a recurring climate pattern involving changes in the temperature of waters in the central and eastern tropical Pacific. In the Philippines, this oscillating warming and cooling pattern, also known as the ENSO cycle, directly affects the rainfall conditions in some areas of the country.Monitoring and evaluation of the plan are conducted annually through the Program Review and Planning Workshop of the departments/offices concern. As for the updating process, the programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) indicated in the plan are reported in the Annual Report of the City Government and published in the My Makati Web Portal.

To accelerate action to achieve both carbon neutrality and climate resilience by 2050, Kuala Lumpur City Hall has developed the Kuala Lumpur Climate Action Plan 2050 to provide guideline for actions required to be done by the council and its stakeholders to achieve the objectives from the Paris Agreement.

Kuala Lumpur, as the Capital City and economic powerhouse of rapidly developing Malaysia, needs to lead the way to reducing GHG emissions of rapid economic growth, especially in contributing to the achievement of the national target of 45% reduction in GHG emission intensity of GDP by 2030 (compared to the 2005 level). To that end, the formulation and implementation of a holistic, scientifically grounded and people-centric city-level climate change mitigation plan – the Kuala Lumpur Low Carbon Society Blueprint 2030 – using the ‘Science to Action’ approach are highly essential.

plan_name: 臺南市氣候變遷調適計畫(2020修正版)
plan_comments: 我國為因應國際上之調適工作推動，陸續完成「國家氣候變遷調適政策綱領」，從國家整體發展的角度，訂定氣候變遷調適政策願景、目標及調適策略。同時，完成「地方氣候變遷調適作業指引」即宣告調適推動工作將由中央發展至地方政府層級之氣候變遷調適計畫，作為地方政府推動永續發展所必然需要擔負之任務，爰此，臺南市自民國 102 年(2013年)正式展開地方調適行動，以「國家氣候變遷調適政策綱領」為基礎，成立地方調適行動推動平台整合地方資源並執行臺南市面臨氣候變遷議題衝擊評估及調適政策研擬，期透過跨局處的協調合作，整合並監督各項任務的執行，才能有效降低臺南市遭受氣候變遷衝擊程度，避免更多的災害發生。
2018至2020年計畫實施總成本約為12,814,265,887元新台幣，詳如臺南市溫室氣體管制執行方案 (107-108 年版核定本)之附件一所示。
為了減緩全球暖化，並加速2050達到淨零碳排，臺南市積極推動再生能源，包含屋頂型與地面型太陽光電。考量光電案場類型與涉及之環境或生態議題，臺南市府積極與當地地主、里長、利害關係人或地方公民團體進行在地溝通，透過辦理在地溝通座談會，蒐集利害關係人對於環境與生態爭議案場區位之相關意見，作為後續政策擬訂與實施之參考。

plan_name: 臺南市節電夥伴節能治理與推廣計畫
plan_comments: 未來隨著科技進步，以及民眾生活型態與用電行為的改變，住宅與服務業需求復甦促使用電量攀升，因此，持續配合中央執行節電夥伴節能治理與推廣計畫，主要為加強推動地方服務業及住宅部門節電工作，研擬節電基礎作業及因地制宜等2面向，規劃「稽查輔導及節能診斷」、「節能志工培育與推動」、「節電教育宣導」、「建築降溫隔熱補助改造計畫」、「能源弱勢族群節能改善」等各項節電計畫，以住商部門作為整體節能政策推動之對象，較能廣泛性達成全市節電目標。協助媒合業者團體(如光電業者、光電公益團體等)推動能源弱勢族群關懷工作，透過業者團體之力量協助能源弱勢族群改善能源運用效率，研析能源弱勢族群用電狀況及設備汰換前後成效，分析用戶用電下降影響整體狀況，並透過深入了解能源數據，建立可估算與視覺化能源使用圖表。跨局處協力公益組織盤點能源弱勢用電消費情形，進一步鏈結在地企業、公協會提供社會支持，俾因應能源轉型提升用電效率。

plan_name: 重要濕地保育利用計畫(
plan_boundary_desc: Partial boundary: 含曾文溪口重要濕地及四草濕地2處國際級濕地，及八掌溪口濕地、北門濕地、七股鹽田濕地及鹽水溪口濕地4處國家濕地，面積達10,331公頃
plan_comments: 本市國家重要濕地面積為10,331公頃，期許到2030年可持續維持濕地面積10,331公頃。

plan_name: 前瞻基礎建設計畫 -水環境建設(
plan_comments: 藉由「水環境改善計畫」跨部會協調整合，集中資源以整體性及系統性方式，辦理河川、排水及海岸環境營造、污水截流、放流水補注、水源淨化、溼地營造、滯洪池休憩景觀、生態復育及污水處理等設置，營造自然豐富親水空間與生態棲地，打造一縣市一親水亮點，恢復水岸生命力及永續水環境。

plan_name: 經濟部水利署-縣、市管河川及區域排水整體改善計畫(
plan_comments: 案件評核原則：•淹水損害度 -淹水情形嚴重亟需改善者優先投入辦理•改善效益 -水患改善效益高，可大幅提升該水系治理率者•保護效益 - 人口密集區、重要工業區或農業區等保全對象，或可系統整體改善者•其他綜合考量 – 民眾支持度、復育生態棲地環境、水患改善後可產生亮點
