plan_name: Taoyuan City Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan(2st Stage)
plan_comments: The Environmental Protection Agency has approved the "Phase II – Action Plan for Controlling Greenhouse Gas in Taoyuan City" on May 8, 2023. In order to track the implementation progress of each plan item, results in Phase II are collected in this appendix.

plan_name: Taoyuan City Climate Change Adaptation Implementation Programs 2023 (draft)
plan_comments: In 2023, the latest draft of action plan for climate change adaptation is being formulated by Taoyuan City. Relevant bureaus and departments have proposed relevant adaptation plans. This appendix is a compilation of various adaptation plans.
The Taoyuan Green Energy City Plan aims to reduce energy poverty as one of its targets.

plan_name: Action Plan in response to Climate Change from 2021 to 2030 with a vision to 2050
plan_comments: The implementation plan is developed to:- Developing and implementing proposals and activities to reduce GHG emissions and ensure green growth based on local conditions in the industry, transportation, construction, and agriculture.- Preparing resources (human, technology, and finance) to meet the needs of implementing the Paris Agreement.- Developing and improving institutions and policies to improve the effectiveness of climate change response and green growth.

plan_name: Implementation plan of the Paris Agreement on climate change in Can Tho City.
plan_comments: The objectives of the implementation plan include the followings:- Developing and implementing proposals and activities to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions and promote sustainable green growth in industries, transportation, construction, agriculture, and rural development, in line with local conditions.- Carrying out programs and tasks to adapt to climate change.Mobilizing resources (human, technological and financial) to meet the requirements of the Paris Agreement.- Establishing and refining institutional frameworks and policies to enhance efficiency in coping with climate change and promoting green growth.

plan_name: Action Plan on Green Growth in 2021 to 2030 with a vision to 2050 in Can Tho City.
plan_comments: The main objectives them implementation plan is to identify key contents and tasks which contribute to the objective of restructuring the economy linked to a growth model and social equity in Can Tho City, towards a green economy, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions to help mitigate global temperature rise.

plan_url: https://www.lahti.fi/uploads/2023/06/f6892f41-lahden-ilmasto-ohjelma-2023.pdf - Climate Plan of the City of Lahti
plan_comments: The plan includes also the carbon sink and offsetting actions of the city which used to be in a separate programme.
The Climate Action Plan includes CO2-emission targets, and results from the Green Transition of the society in Aarhus.
The amount set aside for this purpose last until 2030, which means it includes the costs of the next climate adaptation plan as well.
All sectors addressed in emission inventories are covered

plan_name: Münster Climate Neutral 2030" concept study and Climate Protection Action Program 2030
plan_comments: The results of the concept study make it clear that it will take an enormous effort on the part of all those involved in urban society (private households, companies, administration, institutions, etc.) to come significantly closer to the goal of climate neutrality by 2030. The reduction paths presented in the individual fields of action make the challenge clear and show that fundamental social and political system changes are required in all areas, which would have to be brought about immediately.It should be noted that the illustrations are not to be understood in the sense of a feasibility study, but rather as a scenario analysis that shows, on the basis of assumptions to be made, what must happen in order to achieve the goal of climate neutrality by 2030. The illustrations thus represent the absolute best-case scenario and assume that the climate neutrality targets will also be brought forward to 2030 at EU and federal level and that corresponding measures will be taken within their scope of action. The concept study also makes it clear that, given the complexity of the challenge, there can be no concrete and purely municipal plan of action to ensure climate neutrality in 2030.It is imperative that the municipal scope for action be exhausted in order to achieve the goal in the event of changes in the external political framework conditions. For this reason, it must be a matter for the City of Münster Group to supplement and expand the ambitious Climate Protection 2030 action program with the measures proposed in the concept study as quickly as possible. These are not only motivating and encouraging measures, but also demanding or restrictive measures that must be initiated and permanently expanded and intensified. Climate change is real, the consequences are already severe, and measures to achieve climate neutrality cannot be delayed.

In summary, it can be stated that climate adaptation is a major challenge for the city of Münster, despite the good the city of Münster, despite the good preparatory work done in the past. with all stakeholders from business, politics, institutions, associations and, of course, the citizens. citizens.Only by joining forces can the catalog of measures be tackled and thus the longstanding, successful urban, green and open space development to maintain the good urban climatic conditions in in Münster can be continued and further developed.The citizens' expectations of their city are high, and many of them are willing to make an active contribution. is present in many, as shown by the sustained high level of interest in the subject area. Climate protection climate change are one of the most important challenges of our time. We can only meet them through decisive action.With a cost estimate (in the action plan in  2019) of approx. 425,000 euros by 2030, initial measures for climate adaptation could be continued. could be continued. The action plan was made by a political steering group, including the  mayor and heads of all political parties at City council. The action plans was revised 2019-2020 with a steering group of 3 council members.  Please note, when the Climate Action Plan 2021-2025 (mitigation and adaptation plan) was prepared it was decided to seek ideas for action from the grass roots and from the public.  Advertisements for ideas regarding actions in climate matters were published on the web, on radio and in newspapers during the period 15 November until 8 December 2019. A total of 200 ideas were  received through the portal in addition to proposals that were received by the steering group by e-mail and at meetings.  A meeting was held with external stakeholders from various fields of interest in Januar 2020 in order to seek ideas for new actions regarding climate affairs. A political steering group made a culling of the proposals that were received and prioritised. The points of emphasis that emerged through the action were divided into nine categories; six primary goals and three categories that contravene all the goals.  - Walkable city- Energy exchange   - Health-promoting modes of travel- Green structures- Circular thinking- Carbon sequestration- Adaptation to climate change- Awakening and innovation- Operations of the City of ReykjavikReykjavik is one of the 112 Climate City mission Cites of EU and is working on updating the goal of climate neutrality to 2030. In accordance with the nation climate actionplan , in 2018 the city of Nijmegen has established a  heat transitionvision to phase out the use of natural gas in domestic homes and Utility buildings towards collective distric heating and individuele heatpumps. The report can be downloaded under point 9 in the website https://ris2.ibabs.eu/Agenda/Details/Nijmegen/fa6166c4-c686-4788-a42d-3b57feb0dccd The road to a natural gas-free Nijmegen is neighbourhood by neighbourhood. For all of Nijmegen we looked at which place which alternative to natural gas is the most promising, whereby the affordability for the end user is central. On the base  on the lowest transition costs per neighbourhood, planning by us and our partners and existing initiatives in the city, a choice has been made for the neighborhoods and areas where we will be working on the heat transition. These are Dukenburg, Hengstdal, Bottendaal, Hatert and Heijendaal station area. We aim to be natural gas-free in these areas by 2035 at the latest. In addition, we will have a for the entire city developing an approach to prepare private homes for transition by installing insulation, ventilation and electric cooking stimulate.An estimated  of 25.000 homes will have an alternative heating devices  such as district heating or individual heatpump.  An  CO2 reduction of 55.000 t-CO2 is estimated. In addition  the isolation of resedential homes wil  attribute another approximatley 15.000 t-CO2 reduction.These 25.000 residential homes en Ulitiybuildings are planed to be natural gas free by the year 2035. Most of the other 50.000 residential home are made natural gas gree ready so that these homes can be an transisition between 2035 en 2045.As continued in 2019 a strategy has been made  how the the buildings of the city of Nijmegen can be connected to a city wide distric heating system.  The plan can be downloaded by https://online.ibabs.eu/ibabsapi/publicdownload.aspx?site=Nijmegen&id=88b39552-1f9e-4bc1-8af9-10a7b6471510 Updating the heat strategy from 2019 will start at the end of 2022. The new strategy will be adopted administratively in 2023.

The Council adopted the Sustainability Agenda in April 2011, as policy framework for the implementation of the municipal sustainability policy. The Sustainability Agenda is the successor to the Framework Memorandum on Climate, adopted in 2008, and gives substance to the sustainability ambitions of this Board as laid down in the coalition agreement “Working towards a sustainable future 2010-2014”. This Sustainability Agenda was announced is translated into a implementation program (UP).

The Arnhem Nijmegen region consists of 18 municipalities with a variety of sustainability ambitions. The updated roadmap for the energy transition region Arnhem Nijmegen serves as the basis for these local ambitions.The common regional ambition is to be energy neutral by 2050. In addition, work is underway to implement a regional agenda for energy and climate. This involves close collaboration with the province and other stakeholders such as the Gelders Energy Agreement and other relevant civil society organisations.

In April 2012, the municipality of Nijmegen started the co-creation process 'Power2Nijmegen'. The municipality, companies, knowledge institutions, social organizations and other experts have teamed up to find ways to to contribute to the ambition of the municipality of Nijmegen: a energy-neutral city in 2045. This ambition is part from the Sustainability Agenda (2011-2015), in which the Nijmegen sustainability policy has been established.

plan_name: Municipal sub-masterplan for energy and climate 2017-2030 (link:
plan_comments: The energy and climate plan has the legal status of a "sub-masterplan", which is an extension of the municipal master plan.The energy and climate plan provides targets and strategies for municipal climate policy 2017-2030.  The plan is reported on in the municipal annual report. The plan is being revised in 2021-2023 (see klimatrondheim.no).The measures were in the period 2017-2020 contained in the energy and climate plan's four year "action programme". This was replaced in 2021 by an expanded Climate Budget, which is updated yearly (see below).The climate adaptation chapter in the Sub-masterplan for Energy and Climate is supplemented by a dedicated Thematic Plan for Climate Adaptation, which includes an action plan (see below).

From 2021 the climate change mitigation and energy measures have been integrated directly into the yearly four-year municipal budget ("Climate Budget").  The Climate Budget is a dedicated climate chapter in the yearly municipal budget and action plan.  The climate budget is a cross-sectoral tool for the operationalisation of the Sub-Masterplan for Energy and Climate (Kommundelplan energi og klima) that allocates responsibility for implementation and/or coordination of measures, in addition to centralised mechanisms for the allocation of municipal funds and reporting. It comprises 1)  an emissions framework detailing business as usual compared to a emissions reduction pathway for the next four years 2) a list of action points and measures, with allocation of responsibility for implementation and/or reporting to the relevant municipal department 3) a description of funding for climate measures.  This replaced the previous Action Programme 2017-2020 as the main management tool for operationalising the sub-masterplan. The climate budget's chapter on measures /action points is divided up into four parts: actions 1-11 will reduce emissions in Trondheim and the emissions reduction has been estimated, measures 12-17 will reduce emissions but the reduction is not estimated due to data or methodological challenges, measures 18-28 will contribute to other energy and climate goals such as  reduction of carbon footprint, increase of energy efficiency and increase of innovation and green value creation, while the remaining measures 29-37 are measures to strengthen the knowledge base for future actions, such as analyses, plans, communications work etc.The implementation of the measures within the climate budget are reported together with the twice-yearly financial reports from the city administration to the city councilThe measures contained within the plan are city-wide and in include measures to be carried out by external actors,.  The financial aspect of the climate budget is restricted to the municipalities own funding streams (in combination with  national grants). The costs referred to above are the total investment and running costs allocated directly to the climate budget in the administrative budget, the city council also made some additional funding available when passing the budget.

plan_name: Green Strategy: Climate strategy for Bergen (the attached plan is a draft of the English version of the strategy)
plan_comments: The strategy aims for Bergen to be a low-emission society in 2030, and a 1.5-degree society in 2050. The strategy is made up of four sub-strategies to achieve the goals.1) Bergen must cut 85 per cent of direct greenhouse gas emissions by 2030, compared to 2009.2) Bergen takes care of the resources. In 2030, material consumption, indirect emissions and the amount of waste have been reduced. At least 65 percent of the waste becomes new resources.3) Bergen values and strengthens nature so that it can store carbonand deal with the effects of climate change. In 2030, we have solutions that benefit both nature and the climate.4) In 2030, Bergen is equipped to handle the effects of climate changeand exploits the opportunities in the transition to a low-emission society.
Realizado por el Centro Tecnológico de la Energía y el Medio Ambiente (CETENMA) de la Región de Murcia, de reconocido prestigio en estos temas.

A través de su Estrategia y Plan de Adaptación al Cambio Climático, el Ayuntamiento de Murcia quiere contribuir a dar una respuesta a la necesidad de preparar a la ciudadanía, los sistemas sectoriales y naturales del municipio ante un clima cambiante e incrementar su resiliencia.Para tener una mejor compresión de las implicaciones e impactos del cambio climático en el Municipio de Murcia, a lo largo del año 2016 se procedió con un análisis de riesgos climáticospara cada uno de los sectores seleccionados por su carácter estratégico: agua, medio natural, agricultura/huerta, salud, urbanismo. El diagnóstico de riesgos climáticos permitió identificar losprincipales peligros a los cuales se enfrentarían los sectores de la Sociedad Murciana a futuro, así como sus principales vulnerabilidades. Sentó las bases para identificar las medidas de adaptación más adecuadas y eficientes.El sector agua se consideraría como la prioridad desde la actualidad y hasta el periodo 2050-2064 debido a las sequías repetidas y la dependencia de los demás sectores. La agricultura tiene que asumir numerosas pérdidas por eventos climatológicos, por lo tanto, se consideraría también un sector prioritario en la actualidad. Desde 2020, la salud será cada vez más vulnerable debido a los daños colaterales a la ciudadanía, por lo tanto, pasaría a ocupar la cabeza de los sectores más prioritarios. El urbanismo, por asentar al conjunto de la sociedad, sus infraestructuras, asegurar el confort de la población, su acceso a los servicios, etc. se vería amenazado de forma creciente desde varias frentes y por numerosos eventos climáticos. Y a partir del tercer periodo temporal se constituiría el tercer sector más prioritario.A continuación, tras la elaboración de un primer listado de medidas potenciales, se inició el proceso de contraste participativo que se desarrollaría en tres fases a lo largo del primer semestre de 2017.Los mayores riesgos climáticos a los cuales se pretende responder con prioridad a través de la presente Estrategia de Adaptación corresponden a los peligros siguientes: disminución de las precipitaciones, sequía, olas de calor, incremento de las temperaturas.Finalmente, la Estrategia de Adaptación ha quedado estructurada en torno a 4 objetivos estratégicos que se materializaran a través de 11 metas y 61 medidas para responder a estos riesgos.

Renewable energy (sun and wind power) as well as recycled energy should be developed as far as possible.• Financial management: Facilitate a sustainable municipal finance market.• Carbon sinks

plan_comments: The Low Carbon Framework sets out the vision, purpose and directions of actions necessary to enable the city to deliver on Sunderland’s carbon neutrality goals.The Low Carbon Framework was adopted in December 2020 by the City partnership Board, on the recommendation of the 2030 Shadow Board, and subsequently endorsed by Sunderland City Council’s Cabinet in January 2021 and at the same time adopted its Low Carbon Action Plan (LCAP) which included a target for the Council to become carbon neutral by 2030. The partnership meets quarterly to ensure that best practice is shared, that duplication is avoided, and that resource efficiency, joint working and impact are maximised. Each partner is also developing their own Low Carbon Action Plan and carrying out initiatives to enable the city to reach its low carbon goals. The Low Carbon Framework is underpinned by these individual partner Action Plans

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: São José do Rio Preto contém parte da Estação Ecológica do Noroeste Paulista e Floresta Esatdual do Noroeste Paulista, que fazem divisa com outro Município. Tais áreas foram consideradas para as medidas de adaptação e resiliência do Plano.
plan_comments: Destacamos alguns desafios considerados duarnte a elaboarção do Plano de Adaptação e Resiliência à Mudança do Climas do Município.De clima tropical, com inverno seco e ameno, caracteriza-se por seis meses úmidos (outubro, novembro, dezembro, janeiro, fevereiro e março) e seis mais secos (abril, maio, junho, julho, agosto e setembro) e possui temperatura média anual de 24,04°C. Com poucos remanescentes florestais e impermeabilização do solo intensa em área urbana, o Município possui uma predisposição para que se intensifiquem os efeitos do aumento da temperatura. Destaca-se que, durante os seis meses de clima mais secos, o Município fica mais sujeito às queimadas e incêndios, devido ao clima associado a características da vegetação. Durante o período de estiagem, a população do entorno das áreas suscetíveis a queimadas sofrem por estarem muito próximas ao foco, além do Sistema de Saúde receber mais demandas nesse período.É importante destacar que o Município dispõe de parte da Floresta Estadual do Noroeste Paulista e da Estação Ecológica do Noroeste Paulista em seu território. A extensão da área dificulta o seu monitoramento além de um grande trecho estar contido em Município vizinho.O transporte coletivo no Município ainda é abastecido por diesel e São José do Rio Preto está em uma região de entroncamento de Rodovias, com alto fluxo de veículos movidos a diesel. Isso faz com que a quantidade de gases e materiais particulados emitidos em alguns períodos do ano deixe a qualidade do ar péssima, segundo classificação e índice de qualidade monitorados pela Cetesb. Com o aumento da população, a demanda por água também é maior, o que pode ocasionar racionamentos mais frequentes. Pensando também que o Município é sede da Região Metropolitana de São José do Rio Preto, a visibilidade aumenta e com isso as migrações. Hoje, 30% da população é atendida pelo abastecimento da captação de águas superficiais do Rio Preto e a captação subterrânea representa 70% por meio dos poços profundos do Aquifero Bauru e Guaranni.Pensando nos riscos de inundações, enchentes e alagamento, a área central da cidade, por possuir alta taxa de impermeabilização, alta densidade populacional e grande fluxo de pessoas.No inverno, as ondas de frio têm como tendência serem cada vez mais frequentes e intensas.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: Abrange todo o concelho de Guimarães
plan_comments: Guimarães preserves cultural and natural heritage, enhancing resilience, moving towards climate neutrality and aiming to be one-planet city with a climate-safe future.Under Governance Guimarães Ecosystem 2030, Guimarães has had a Climate Policy since 20152, which includes the Municipal Strategy for Climate Change Adaptation(MSCCA). Using scientific evidence, it comprises a systematic climate risks and vulnerabilities assessment, considering several scenarios: frequency and intensity of extreme events, geopolitical and socioeconomic conjunctures. It considers the different population groups and economic sectors in the territory.Guimarães is a national and European reference in Climate Adaptation, an inspiring example for othercities. Member of EU Mission: Adaptation to Climate Change and EU Cities Mission: 100 Climate-neutral and Smart Cities by 2030.Effects of global climate change are already locally evident with a need to adapt faster and comprehensively. Our climate team has acquired relevant skills and competencies to assess systematic climate risks and vulnerabilities (ADAM methodology). The last assessment was done in 2022.Climate action is dynamic and proactive.Through 2022 reassessment, drought and landslides were added to previous impacts. Alongside technical assessment, citizens were asked about climate events and impacts that most concerned them. Results were consistent with technical assessment.Climate action is systemic.All economic sectors were assessed. Through Climate City Contract, co-created with local  stakeholders and citizens, we foster an inclusive sense of belonging and responsibility. Economic projects include new business initiatives that, structurally, complement sustainable endogenous development and are supported by specific regulation. With the new Green Public Procurement criteria for waste, transport, energy, goods and services, climate action is more efficient and equitable.We will continue to provide a holistic and systemic approach to change, improving our adaptation capabilities, anticipating climate action, prioritizing non-technological innovation and co-creation, building science-based and smart adaptation, integrating lessons learned, capitalizing win-win solutions, and integrating data as it becomes available.

plan_comments: De momento, está a ser elaborado um novo plano de ação climática para o município de Guimarães, que substituirá o SECAP, estará em linha com a Missão 100 cidades e com a recente Lei de Bases do Clima. Com este novo plano, surgirá, evidentemente, uma atualização dos objetivos e das ações para alcançar os mesmos, tendo em vista novas considerações. Como por exemplo, a pobreza energética e transição justa.

Os setores acima são os mesmo do plano de ação. Para o setor Agrícola, as medidas incluem a manutenção de hortas comunitárias e a promoção do aproveitamento agrícola de terras em pousio. No setor Florestal, por exemplo, estão previstas medidas para o aproveitamento de resíduos de biomassa de florestas e para a restauração ecológica de áreas queimadas. No sector de Abastecimento de Água, o plano climático incluirá medidas de promoção de sistemas de reutilização de água, impermeabilização de solos e delimitação de novas zonas inundáveis. Para o setor de Informação e Comunicação, elaboração do plano de ação plano de comunicação e monitoramento dos principais impactos identificados. O setor Imobiliário diz respeito ao setor dos edifícios, pelo que o plano de ação inclui medidas relacionadas, mas não limitadas, ao desenvolvimento de incentivos à eficiência energética e estratégias de adaptação dos edifícios e à utilização de coberturas verdes. Finalmente, o setor de saúde humana e social preocupa-se com a conscientização sobre a prevenção de doenças e o desenvolvimento de sistemas de monitoramento da qualidade da água e doenças potenciais.NOTA: O plano de ação ainda estava em desenvolvimento no momento da redação do relatório CDP 2023, portanto a periodicidade do monitoramento, avaliação e atualização do plano de ação pode ser atualizada num futuro próximo.

plan_name: Plano de Adaptação para as Alterações Climáticas de Águeda
plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: As ações a implementar pelo PAAC de Águeda têm impacto sobre uma área que extravasa o espaço geográfico e administrativo da Câmara Municipal de águeda, com benefícios e impactos positivos a escala regional, nacional e global.
plan_comments: O plano surge nas ações decorrentes da implementação do Pacto de Autarcas a nível local, neste contexto Águeda tem aderido às iniciativas e desafios que a nível internacional são colocados aos municípios. A adaptação às alterações climáticas pressupõe a tomada atempada de decisões perante um cenário de alguma incerteza. Neste contexto, destacam-se quatro fatores que devem ser reconhecidos e orientar qualquer processo de adaptação:▪ É um processo contínuo;▪ É um processo específico;▪ É um processo que deve envolver múltiplos agentes, compreender perspetivas e contextos individuais;▪ É um processo que deve ser ajustado temporalmente.A metodologia de referência a utilizar na elaboração do plano para as alterações climáticas é uma metodologia baseada e adaptada à realidade portuguesa a partir do modelo UKCIP Adaptation Wizard. Este modelo foi desenvolvido e testado pelo UK Climate Impacts Programme (UKCIP)1, entidade integrada no Environmental Change Institute na Universidade de Oxford, por forma a providenciar um instrumento robusto para planeamento em adaptação, constituindo uma ferramenta de apoio à decisão.Esta metodologia pressupõe a utilização de princípios básicos de tomada de decisão e análise de risco e permite determinar quais os riscos climáticos que devem ser tidos em conta aquando de uma decisão, assim como que opções de adaptação serão necessárias implementar para fazer face a esses riscos.As principais etapas deste processo são:▪ Etapa 1. Preparação▪ Etapa 2. Identificação de vulnerabilidades climáticas atuais▪ Etapa 3. Vulnerabilidades climáticas futuras▪ Etapa 4. Opções de adaptação▪ Etapa 5. Monitorização- Avaliação (multicritério, participativa) das opções de adaptação em relação aos objetivos inicias;- Integração da adaptação na estratégia intermunicipal;- Definição do processo de implementação, monitorização e revisão. Quanto ao custo e dado o alargado horizonte temporal, assim como o elevado número de medidas incluídas no plano, não se prevê o custo global, já as medidas têm uma definição qualitativa quanto ao investimento que representa a sua implementação. O custo indicado é o de elaboração do plano.

plan_name: Plano de ação para a energia sustentável e clima
plan_comments: A adoção de uma estratégia de mitigação e adaptação às Alterações Climáticas é um elemento central no planeamento urbano, quer com o objetivo de promover um desenvolvimento mais sustentável, com menores impactes, quer com o objetivo de aumentar a resiliência local aos impactes das Alterações Climáticas, atuais e a médio-longo prazo. Com a elaboração e implementação do Plano de Ação para a Energia Sustentável e o Clima do Município de Águeda pretende-se identificar políticas e medidas de mitigação e de adaptação às Alterações Climáticas, de modo a alcançar uma redução de GEE, redução da pobreza energética e neutralidade climática. Como ações de mitigação referem-se a iniciativas de melhoria da eficiência energética, de aumento de produção renovável e outras com potencial de redução das emissões de GEE. Estas ações devem abranger os diversos setores consumidores de energia e com potencial de intervenção no território concelhio, tais como edifícios públicos, de serviços e residenciais, indústria, transportes e agricultura e pecuária, entres outros que se revelem relevantes. Ao nível das ações de adaptação, as mesmas são definidas de acordo com as especificidades e necessidades de cada território, tomando como referência os riscos e vulnerabilidades às Alterações Climáticas a que o município estará sujeito. Destacam-se, assim, como áreas prioritárias de intervenção, no âmbito da estratégia de adaptação, os setores da agricultura, florestas e pescas, a indústria, a proteção da biodiversidade, a gestão de infraestruturas de energia e de transportes, o turismo, o ordenamento do território, a gestão de recursos hídricos, serviços de saúde humana e segurança de pessoas e bens. Adicionalmente, e considerando que esta é uma problemática com impactes não só ambientais, mas também sociais e económicos, é essencial o envolvimento de toda a comunidade (empresas e serviços públicos, instituições financeiras, cidadãos, associações e cooperativas, instituições de educação e investigação, pólos de inovação e desenvolvimento, …) no desenvolvimento de um plano de ação e politicamente endossado e participado. O Município de Águeda pretende mobilizar atores locais e contribuir para a mitigação e adaptação às Alterações Climáticas do seu território e melhorar a sua resposta às vulnerabilidades atuais e futuras. Pelas medidas e horizonte temporal não se define o investimento.

Na estratégia de adaptação às alterações climáticas são abrangidos diversos setores para enfrentar os impactos das mudanças climáticas. Os setores cobertos incluem sobretudo agricultura, recursos hídricos, energia, infraestruturas, gestão de riscos de desastres e atividades em áreas urbanas. Cada setor requer avaliação de vulnerabilidades específicas e a implementação de medidas adaptativas adequadas. A estratégia visa fortalecer a resiliência das comunidades, ecossistemas e economias para enfrentar eventos climáticos extremos, escassez de recursos naturais e outros desafios decorrentes das mudanças climáticas. O objetivo é garantir uma abordagem holística e integrada para proteger os sistemas naturais e sociais contra os efeitos do clima em constante transformação.

O PAESC abrange diversos setores para reduzir as emissões de gases de efeito estufa e combater as causas do aquecimento global. Os setores cobertos incluem energia, transporte, indústria, agricultura, gestão de resíduos, uso do solo e florestas. Cada setor requer a implementação de medidas específicas para promover a eficiência energética, o uso de fontes de energia limpa e renovável, a redução do desperdício e a adoção de práticas sustentáveis. A estratégia visa alcançar uma economia de baixo carbono, diminuindo a pegada de carbono e contribuindo para a estabilização do clima global.

Estes setores incluem energia, transporte, indústria, agricultura, gestão de recursos naturais, conservação da biodiversidade, planeamento urbano, resiliência às alterações climáticas e sensibilização pública. The Risk Assessment and Management Plan (RAMP) was developed following successive years of repetitive, extreme inland flooding and significant, repetitive losses in a number of watersheds throughout Arlington County.  Rapid, dense legacy development in Arington 1940-1975 during a period of meager stormwater regulation and no codes for overland relief, acute changes in topography and elevation, and an under-sized, insufficient capacity infrastructure system that lacks tertiary and lateral assets. In addition, 2/3 of the County's 92 miles of streams were undergrounded and the opportunistic development above severely restricts or prevents access to the system for upgrades. These conditions are historic but their impact has been vastly amplified by climate volatility that produces frequent,  highly-intense storms and cloudbursts that rapidly overwhelm the stormwater infrastructure.  The pattern is escalating, with climate driving greater risk to property, infrastructure, critical government assets and services, and public health and safety. To create an effective, long-term climate adaptation and resilience plan for capital investment and strategic system diversification and upgrades, the RAMP was designed to:•update the County's climate projections from present day to multiple climate horizons (2040, 2070 and 2100)•use new climate projections to model future inundation and flooding across 9 watersheds•compare the present system's capacity against future capacity needs to produce a comprehensive gaps analysis•conduct vulnerability assessments•identify and model impacts to critical government assets•conduct risk assessments to measure the fiscal/financial "cost-of-inaction"; and•to develop a series of program, policy and capital projects to improve the stormwater infrastructure's adaptive capacity and resiliency potentialIn addition, the RAMP incorporates equity considerations and projects that will protect small businesses and residential structures and ensure public health and safety in disadvantaged communities in Arlington.

The Resilient Cambridge Plan is the City's roadmap to reduce the risks from climate change and prepare the community for impacts that cannot be avoided. The Plan focuses on the threats from increasing temperature, precipitation, and sea level rise.  Using projections to 2030 and 2070 based on the best available science, the City conducted a "climate stress test" -- the Climate Change Vulnerability Assessment (CCVA) -- to understand the potential impacts on the community if no action is taken. Starting with the CCVA as the technical foundation, two neighborhood plans for the Alewife area and The Port were developed to inform the citywide plan.  Public meetings, workshops, focus groups, and surveys and a range of technical studies were conducted to generate the content for the Plan.Resilient Cambridge is organized around four categories of strategies:  Closer Neighborhoods, Better Buildings, Stronger Infrastructure, and Greener City.  The strategies are meant to work together and complement each other.  Strategies are also recommended at the scales of sites and buildings, neighborhoods, city, and region.  And there are roles for all parts of the community -- residents, institutions, government, and businesses.  To be successful, everyone must do their part and collaborate.Please find more information about Resilient Cambridge here: https://www.cambridgema.gov/Departments/communitydevelopment/resilientcambridgeplan

The City of West Palm Beach's "Rethink Paradise: Sustainability Action Plan" was first developed in 2012 and has since been updated in 2017 and 2020. The 2020 update provides a detailed report of what the City has done since the Plan was last updated in 2017. For the 2020 update, the City organized the mitigation and adaption recommendations using the LEED for Cities and Communities as the central organizing theme for its sustainability program. For this Plan, the City and its team reviewed and analyzed over 400 adaptation and mitigation recommendations from five sources: (1) LEED for Cities guidance; (2) the Global Covenant of Mayors for Climate and Energy requirements; (3) the existing Rethink Paradise Recommendations last updated in 2017; (4) the Southeast Florida Regional Climate Change Compact "Climate Action Plan 2.0", and (5) the City of West Palm Beach's "Climate Resilience Vulnerability Assessment and Plan". The final list of recommendations was organized to identify overlaps across all the plans/recommendations evaluated and the City's overall goals to make one holistic and comprehensive guiding document that addresses climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies.

In this report, the promotion strategies of 30% greenhouse gas reduction in 2030 and zero climate net emissions in 2050 are planned in five major sectors, namely residential and commercial, industrial, transportation, agricultural and forestry, and waste. In addition, based on the four core supporting measures of "New Taipei City Climate Change Response Steering Groups", "regulatory system - New Taipei City Zero Carbon City Self-Government Regulations", "industrial development - Smart Industrial Area" and "financial support - green budget and climate fund", the foundation is laid for the construction and accelerated expansion of the capacity required for net zero. The climate vulnerable groups in the City are wanderers and the elderly living alone. In order to avoid the impact of extreme weather on them, the City starts the care mechanism during high and low temperatures.In addition, the "Bali Net Zero Carbon Demonstration Zone" has been planned to implement the 2030 net zero emission strategy in terms of industry, transportation, residential and commerce, and energy. In addition to carbon reduction measures, the City will accelerate climate change adaptation efforts, give priority to the elderly in the Bali sanatorium, low-income households and other groups vulnerable to climate change, carry out risk assessment and develop response measures to ensure that no one is left in the process of transformation.

In response to international climate change and global warming trends, New Taipei City has been promoting climate change adaptation and mitigation for a long time. In 2007, it established the "Low-Carbon Community Development Centre" under the Environmental Protection Agency which became the first local government in Taiwan to set up an independent unit to implement carbon reduction work. In 2016, the city government established the "Climate Change Mitigation and Adaptation Promotion Team" across agencies, with 17 agencies working together to address climate change and discuss greenhouse gas reduction measures.The City actively promotes climate change mitigation and adaptation. In 2020, a "New Taipei City Climate Action Plan" was specially compiled, which divides mitigation work into the sectors of renewable energy, industrial energy conservation, energy-efficient buildings, energy conservation, green transportation, and sustainable agriculture and forestry for implementation of the action plan. For the adaptation work, corresponding strategies for the impact of climate change are formulated according to various fields, and care visits for the homeless and the elderly living alone are carried out for the climate-vulnerable groups, cold weather protection materials are provided, and resettlement is encouraged and arranged when the temperature drops sharply, so as to protect the lives of homeless and elderly people living alone.

According to the "Climate Change Response Act" implemented in Taiwan in 2023, local governments are required to propose context-specific greenhouse gas reduction strategies and establish "Greenhouse Gas Reduction Implementation Plans" based on the action plan and sectoral action plans. These plans serve as directions for cities to implement carbon reduction policies. New Taipei City has already released the second phase (2021-2025) of the "Greenhouse Gas Control Implementation Plan." The city has also established the "New Taipei City Climate Change Response Steering Groups ," which promotes cross-departmental collaboration in areas such as "Residential and Commercial," "Environment and Agriculture," "Energy," "Industry," and "Transportation" to achieve carbon reduction goals. The city aims to reduce carbon emissions by 30% by 2030 (compared to 2005) and achieve net zero emissions by 2050. It also emphasizes a "Just Transition" approach, ensuring fairness across generations, genders, and vulnerable groups, and leaving no one behind in the pursuit of climate action goals.

屏東縣政府規劃第二期溫室氣體減量執行方案涵蓋能源、製造、運輸、住商、農業、環境等部門，並分別以「再生能源發展」、「能資源節約」、「運輸綠色化」、「永續農業及林業經營」、「資源循環利用」及「教育宣導與培訓」等面向規劃推動相關減量措施與行動計畫，目標於110-114年為本縣帶來40萬噸CO2e的減碳量及20萬噸的固碳量。<mitigation>Pingtung County Government enacts second-phase greenhouse gas reduction implementation plan, including energy, manufacturing, transportation, housing and commerce, agriculture, and the environment sector.From 2021 to 2025, the County government plans to reduce 400,000 tons of carbon emission and 200,000 tons of carbon sink capacity by developing renewable energy, energy saving, sustainable agriculture and forest management, circular economy and other relevant reduction measures and action plans.<adaptation>It goes without saying that people can’t live without water and electricity. Pingtung County has many precautions to prevent the influence of climate change, such as heavy rain, typhoons, and to ensure residents’ safety and livelihood. First, Pingtung County put much effort to ensure stable water supply by developing underflow water, river dredging and artificial lake. Agriculture as one of the main industries in Pingtung County, we need to ensure the amount of water resources to support farmers’ livelihood.The last but not the least, Pingtung County also put much effort to ensure stable electricity supply for our residents, we keep developing renewable energy to satisfy our daily use and prevent from power outage caused by natural disasters.<low-carbon transportation and social welfare>This project aims to promote public transportation by increasing  bus routes, transit stations, happy buses, and shuttle taxi.Pingtung County Government manages happy buses in 10 remote villages in the county to provide convenient public transportation based on local features. Bus routes covered locations, including hospitals, schools, and shopping malls, to satisfy residents’ livelihood, which can not only reduce students’ dropout rate, but also make elderly people to go to the doctor easier. Until 2021, there has been 306,000 passengers who take the happy buses. It is estimated that the number of passengers will reach 586,000 in 2025.

The strategy laid out in the Climate Action and Adaptation Plan is broken down into 4 topics: Buildings & Energy, Ecosystems & Natural Environment, Public Health, and Transportation. Each topic has a few guiding objectives, which involve the above sectors. Buildings & Energy:- Champion new buildings and redevelopment that make Medford a more affordable, resilient, and low-carbon city.- Retrofit existing buildings to be more efficient, resilient, and to have a smaller carbon footprint.- Build out resilient and renewable energy systems.Ecosystems & Natural Environment:- Protect, restore, and grow Medford’s natural systems for a more resilient city.- Mitigate flooding using nature-based solutions when possible.Public Health:- Create neighborhoods and infrastructure systems that support health- Invest in Medford’s people and businesses for strong and thriving communitiesTransportation:- Make it safer and easier to walk, bike, and take public transportation- Accelerate the transition to zero emission vehicles- Increase the resilience of transportation infrastructure to climate hazards

Somerville Climate Forward is a comprehensive climate change plan designed to lay out a set of 13 implementable action items to both reduce the city's contribution to climate change and prepare Somerville for the unavoidable impacts of climate change. This plan is the basis for the majority of the adaptation and mitigation goals/targets described throughout the CDP report for Somerville. It includes policies, programs, and strategies to reduce emissions, implement adaptation measures, and fairly distribute both the opportunities climate action creates and the current unequal burdens of climate change. Each year, a progress report is published for the public and used as an internal monitor for progress on various measures. Every five years, the plan as a whole is reviewed and a new version will be created and published. NOTE: End year of plan represents the end year of the current version of the Somerville Climate Forward plan.  Plan is subject to updates every five years. We are currently updating the plan.
The Net Zero Action Plan has five main objectives over five sectors - Electricity, Fuel, Waste/Water/Sewer, Governance, and Community Infrastructure. See Plan attachment for detailed objectives.

El Plan de Mitigación  Cantonal de La Unión constituye la herramienta de planificación cantonal para los próximos tres años con el fin de fortalecer el desarrollo bajo en emisiones a nivel territorial, mediante una serie de 13 acciones climáticas  distribuidas en tres ejes estratégicos: Transporte, Gestión de Residuos y Energía.  El plan se desarrolla como parte del Programa País Carbono Neutralidad, del cual forma parte la jurisdicción. Según los criterios del programa, el plan se evalúa y renueva cada 3 años, de la misma forma que el inventario. Para cada acción, según el impacto requerido, se estima una inversión necesaria, llegando a un total de 433 millones de colones entre las 13 acciones propuestas de forma estimada.El plan incluye acciones que impactarán a la totalidad de la población que habita y trabaja en el cantón, así como todas las actividades económicas que se realizan dentro de los límites de la jurisdicción.

plan_comments: Equipo municipal en conjunto con asesoría y apoyo de Asociación Adapt Chile. Lo que entregó una hoja de ruta para definir lineamientos asociados a estrategias de adaptación y mitigación ante el cambio climático.

El plan local de cambio climático se realiza bajo el marco del proyecto "Academias de cambio climático: Planes locales de cambio climático para gobiernos locales" que reúne a siete municipios en 2015, que declaran su interés y compromiso de la elaboración de estos planes. Esto a raíz de los impactos que ha tenido el cambio climático en diferentes partes del planeta y en nuestras ciudades. Este demuestra el compromiso del municipio de Providencia para generar acciones concretas en materia del clima y tiene como lineamientos principales la gestión integral de residuos, la movilidad sustentable, la educación ambiental, biodiversidad y gestión de áreas verdes, eficiencia energética e hídrica y agricultura urbana. Como información adicional, se puede indicar que actualmente estamos trabajando en una propuesta de actualización del Plan Local de Cambio Climático, de acuerdo a la Ley Marco de Cambio Climático publicada en junio de 2022 en Chile, la Estrategia Climática a Largo Plazo para Chile y la NDC a nivel nacional.

El informe está preparado para explicar el estado actual de la comuna en términos energéticos, a dónde se quiere y cómo se planea llegar. Se levanta un diagnóstico incluyendo el consumo energético de la comuna, considerando como fuentes de energía la electricidad de red, el gas natural y el gas licuado. Si bien no se consideró el consumo energético asociado al transporte, debido a la complejidad de calcular su demanda con exactitud, si se lo considera en el plan de acción con el objetivo de disminuir su impacto medioambiental. Adicionalmente, se calcularon los potenciales de eficiencia, energética, y generación a partir de las energías renovables solar y biomasa. Estos tres potenciales combinados, serían suficientes para reducir en un 62% del consumo eléctrico del 2014. Luego se definen tres metas para el año 2030, primero reducir en un 50% las emisiones de CO2 comunal por unidad de PIB. Segundo, incluir, en las decisiones energéticas la participación de los actores involucrados y por último contar con un 100% de ciudadanos sensibilizados en temáticas energéticas. Por último para alcanzar dichas metas se propone un plan de acción ordenado en los ejes de: 1. Energía limpia, 2. Gestión y cultura energética, 3. Ciudadanía y pobreza energética y 4.Educación e innovación.

The Climate Action Plan (CAP) was produced by the City of Hoboken in 2017, and was adopted in 2019.  An update to the Climate Action Plan is planned for 2024, with an updated GHG Inventory highlighting the differences between the years 2022 and the base year, 2017 emissions.

The City of Hoboken has been actively involved in sustainability efforts for nearly a decade, achieving Bronze certification from Sustainable Jersey in 2011 and Silver certification in 2017. The City aims to achieve Sustainable Gold Star in Energy by the end of 2022. Sustainability has been promoted through several recent and current efforts that address climate adaptation, flood mitigation, public and environmental health, Green Building, and non-vehicular transportation. Community stakeholders were engaged during the feasibility/environmental review (planning) phase, in which NJDEP sought community input at public meetings, community advisory group meetings, and executive steering committee meetings, as well as meetings with individual stakeholders and property-owners. Information was provided to the community via an extensive project website and an electronic mailing list maintained by NJDEP, bolstered by the City’s efforts to continue engagement via the City website, social media, and email alerts.

The City created an addendum to the 2015 Resilient Buildings Design Guidelines (https://drive.google.com/file/d/0B9lTDiYbNgQrenhaYWhHT1ZPRTg/view?resourcekey=0-U4I6viIkN9aopq2tkW9-Dw), which currently includes strategies to mitigate coastal flooding on private property, to also include preparedness solutions for homeowners during intense/severe rainfall events. This non-regulatory guidance serves as a technical resource and starting point for property owners to reference when engaging with a licensed design professional.
The Plan is focused on actions the City will take - both in its own operations and within the community - to decrease GHG emissions and adapt to the impacts of climate change.

plan_name: City of Mountain View Climate Protection Roadmap and Sustainability Action Plan 4 -
plan_comments: The Climate Protection Roadmap identifies strategies and mechanisms to reduce community-wide greenhouse gas emissions for building energy, transportation, and solid waste. SAP-4 actions are organized around 27 goals within the following categories: Transportation, buildings and energy, land use, zero waste, water, parks and ecosystems, and core sustainability programs and governance.

The Greenhouse Gas Reduction Program includes strategies for into the following sectors: energy, transportation, solid waste, water, and carbon sequestration. Energy: The Energy Strategy recommends ways to increase energy efficiency in existing buildings, enhance energy performance for new construction, and increase use of renewable energy. The Waste Strategy increases waste diversion and recycling, reducing consumption of materials that otherwise end up in landfills. The Water Strategy promotes the efficient use and conservation of water in buildings and landscapes. The Transportation Strategy encourages transit, carpooling, walking, and bicycling as viable transportation modes to decrease the need to drive. The Carbon Sequestration Strategy uses street trees and urban forestry to capture and store carbon emitted from other sources. The GHG reduction measures impact various audiences, including the City of Mountain View, residents and community members, businesses and organizations, and industry.

plan_name: Shoreline Regional Park Community Sea Level Rise Capital Improvement Program
plan_boundary_desc: Smaller boundary: Covers the Shoreline Regional Park Community, located in the northern part of the City, bounded by Highway 101 to the south, City of Palo Alto boundary at San Antonio Road to the west, Stevens Creek to the east, and Salt Ponds A1 and A2W to the north
plan_comments: The City of Mountain View (City) developed a Sea Level Rise Capital Improvement Program (SLR CIP) for the Shoreline Community in 2012, documented in the Shoreline Regional Park Community Sea Leve Rise Study Feasibility Report and Capital Improvement Program report (2012 Study). The SLR CIP presented a program to address long-term flood inundation impacts to the City due to a combination of the projected sea level rise, coastal flooding from South San Francisco Bay, and fluvial flooding from Permanente Creek and Stevens Creek. The program is designed to provide a coastal barrier to reduce the risk of Bay tide flood of the City shoreline, and minimize flooding due to overtopping and backwater from Permanente Creek and Stevens Creek. Based on the SLR CIP developed in 2012, the City has been working on projects in the SLR CIP. In addition, the City has been coordinating closely with members of other agencies working on regional efforts that affect the City’s sea level rise adaptation efforts.The Shoreline Regional Park Community Sea Level Rise Capital Improvement Program Update (2022) is an incremental update of the 2012 Study. It updates the sea level rise projection and vulnerability of the City, and the projects in the SLR CIP, planning level cost estimates and implementation plan.  With the latest projections, the planning year was updated from 2067 to 2070, which equates to an approximately 50-year planning horizon. The CIP includes fourteen projects to be initiated within 5-10 years that will help the City balance flood risk management needs of the City and the region as well as restore and preserve tidal marsh and wildlife habitats. The SLR CIP and sea level rise assessment will be monitored and updated every 5 years. More information can be found in the two attachments.

plan_comments: The Mountain View City Council has also signed onto the BayAdapt Joint Platform for sea level rise planning, an initiative of the San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission.  The Bay Adapt Joint Platform is a consensus-based strategy comprised of 9 actions and 21 tasks that will protect people and the natural and built environment from rising sea levels. Rather than specifying individual projects, the Bay Adapt Joint Platform lays out regional strategies that focus on overcoming barriers and identifying factors for successful adaptation outcomes we want throughout the region.

The Climate Action and Resilience Plan calls for carbon neutrality by 2050, 100% clean and renewable electricity by 2030, zero waste by 2050. CARP aims to reduce emissions across every sector and create a more livable community for all. It plans to increase the amount of public trees, protect private trees, maintain tree longevity across both sectors, diversify tree planting selection (to take into account anticipated future climate zone shifts due to a warming climate), and increase planting of native species, natural habitats, and green infrastructure, especially in areas where environmental justice issues have been identified. CARP aims to efficiently utilize public land to increase stormwater infiltration and detention while enhancing the primary use of the property. Per CARP, the City of Evanston is tracking and addressing energy and water usage through its Benchmarking Ordinance, and aims to increase water efficiency, decrease water usage, increase building efficiency, and decrease GHG emission in its Green Building Standard and passage of Building Performance Standards legislation.  Water quality will be improved  through the CARP goals to reduce emission and waste contaminants.The plan has aided in creating programs to begin to address residential and commercial building retrofits that encourage building electrification and other means of emission reductions, such as more energy efficient HVAC and appliances, better insulation, and other methods. It also supports updating the Green Building Ordinance to increase building performance standard and thus reduce stationary energy usage. CARP advises the transition to a circular economy, greatly reducing the amount of materials going to landfills and creating methane emissions. This comes in the form of bolstering recycling and compost capacity for the Evanston community. This includes diverting and recycling materials from the construction sector. Green space and biodiversity preservation are detailed in the CARP, in order to reduce urban head effects, provide access to natural areas for community members, and maintain a healthy ecosystem for all species. CARP aims to reduce vehicle miles traveled, while increasing trips made by walking, bicycling and transit. It also calls to increase use of electric vehicles, which decreases carbon emissions from vehicles and equipment.

plan_boundary_desc: Smaller boundary: The Zamboanga City Local Climate Change Action Plan covers only 98 barangays of the city.
plan_comments: “Addressing the changing climate” is a challenge in local governance being in the frontline community services as it has to balance ecological, human well-being and good governance. The hazards of climate extremes such as the current and projected temperature rise and unpredicted higher or lower than normal rainfallare associated with potentially damaging bio-physical manifestations. The dry spell condition termed as drought in progress being experienced in Zamboanga City, may intensify from moderate to strong “El Niňo” and may last until early 2016 (El Niňo Advisory No. 6). The challenge is the ability of the local government system and the community to build-up its “resilience to resist, accommodate, and recover from the effects of climate change hazards”. This formulated action plan is not mainly a compliant of the legal framework; but in itself is an expression of our preparedness as individual and concerned group which developed plan, allocated resources and established adaptation and mitigation measures to response on the climate change impact or its consequences. While there are competing priorities, planning for climate change and similarly with disaster risk may expedite the release fund support under existing policy (RA 9729); hence, demand equal attention at the local level. However, in ensuring a wide ranging support of its implementation, this plan adopts the guiding principles of sectoral coordination; information dissemination and instill public consciousness; good practices; flexibility amidst uncertainties; and priortizing adaptation actions that secure vulnerable communities and population. As equally important feature of the plan, it has semblance to the National Climate Change Action Plan (NCCAP) in terms of work priorities and target outcomes as reflected in the proposed PPAs, in the aspects of food security; water sufficiency; environmental and ecological stability; human security; climate-friendly industries and services; sustainable energy; and knowledge and capacity development.

The Ten Point Plan lays out a framework for how the Council will work with the city to address the shared challenge of the climate crisis.  It is not a fully costed delivery plan which details the exact route for the city to net zero.  More detail on the specific pathways the Council and city will need to follow to reach net zero will be outlined in a forthcoming set of Routemaps.

The Our Council chapter includes decarbonisation actions for its housing estate, operational estate (including; offices, schools, depots, libraries, community centres), fleet and streetlighting along with Land Use, Land Use Change and Forestry of its own land, governance arrangements and how it will engage with and educate its employees.

31 of our actions came from the recommendations of our Climate Assembly.  A Climate Assembly was held because we wanted to make sure that the local community were able to  have a real say in decisions and were involved in shaping climate change projects that were right for them. FutureGov ran the assembly for us in Blackpool. This ensured the process was independent. Around forty Blackpool residents took part in the climate assembly, which was run entirely digitally due to the pandemic. An “open access” survey was created to gather intelligence from the population generally, giving the opportunity from those with various protected characteristics to give their view, with the intelligence gathered being used to develop the plan. An equality analysis ensured that consideration was given to those with vulnerabilities as part of the initial development phase.A separate youth climate group has been set up to bring together young people across Blackpool. A youth assembly was held by the group and this fed into the climate assembly sessions to make sure young people's voices are heard in the fight against climate change.A link to further information on Blackpool's Climate Assembly can be found here: https://www.blackpool.gov.uk/Residents/Planning-environment-and-community/Climate-emergency/Climate-assembly.aspx

plan_name: Dundee Local Heat and Energy Efficiency Strategy
plan_comments: The Local Heat and Energy Efficiency Strategies (Scotland) Order 2022 (https://www.legislation.gov.uk/sdsi/2022/9780111053935) puts an obligation on the Council to prepare and publish its first Local Heat and Energy Efficiency Strategy (LHEES) and its first LHEES delivery plan on or before 31 December 2023; and publish an updated LHEES and LHEES delivery plan at intervals of no more than 5 years after the date of publication of the previous strategy. The LHEES is the long-term plan for decarbonization of heat and tackling fuel poverty. The analysis includes identification of heat network opportunity area and  heat network zones; identification of the most suitable low and zero carbon technologies for heat decarbonization of on-gas properties; identification of measures to improve fabric efficiency of buildings and removing poor fabric energy efficiency as the cause of fuel poverty. In Dundee the LHEES is integrated with Local Area Energy Plan.

plan_name: Local Area Energy Plan. Plan is linked here:
plan_comments: Dundee City Council is collaborating with partners through Regional Energy Systems Optimization Planning (RESOP) project (https://www.ssen.co.uk/news-views/2020/2020-ssen-partners-with-dundee-city-council-resop-project/) to develop a Local Area Energy Plan. The plan is Dundee's whole-system approach to decarbonizing all components of local energy system in line with the city's ambition of becoming Net Zero by 2045 or sooner. The aim of the LAEP work is to carry out analysis of technical potential for renewable energy generation; waste and low carbon heat sources; future buildings (domestic and non-domestic) heat and electricity demand mapping and forecasting; analysing and forecasting transport (public, private and heavy goods vehicle) electrification, uptake and charging infrastructure roll-out; and carry out Heat Network analysis. Dundee's LAEP is unique in a sense that it is developed in parallel to statutory LHEES and is being visualized in WebGIS tool called LAEP+. The integration of with LHEES means the LAEP is imbedded within  statutory plan, has stringent data, has a transparent public engagement and a robust monitoring plan.

Transport accounts for around 70% of the emissions in Arendal. Penetration of electric cars is to a large extent dependent on the supportive national policies for electric cars such as, exemption from toll, rebate in taxes etc. If the national authorities decide to change these incentives, this might affect the share of electric cars.  There are also other national policies such as ban on fossil fuels for heating which affects, or may affect, the electricity use and emissions in the long run. The forecasts for population growth in Arendal show over 50,000 inhabitants in 2030 and over 54,000 in 2040.   It is crucial to maintain the goal of zero growth in traffic even if the number of inhabitants increases.  State and regional support is crucial to facilitate more public transport, cycling and walking.

plan_comments: El Plan Nacional de Adaptación es uno de los principales insumos de la Estrategia Nacional ante el Cambio Climático al 2050, que viene siendo actualizada y que establecerá una agenda en materia de adaptación con una mirada a largo plazo.Para ello, este Plan cuenta con trece acciones estratégicas que asegurarán su puesta en marcha: desarrollar capacidades de predicción del clima a futuro; aprovechar de manera sostenible los recursos forestales con capacitaciones a los pueblos indígenas, afroperuano y campesinos; implementar buenas prácticas de manejo, mejoramiento y conservación en los sistemas productivos agropecuarios; el aprovechamiento sostenible de los recursos hidrobiológicos para el fortalecimiento de la pesca y actividad acuícola; e implementar medidas de gestión y conservación de ecosistemas forestales.En esa perspectiva, se fortalecerá la articulación interinstitucional y la optimización del sistema de abastecimiento de agua para uso multisectorial, también se implementarán buenas prácticas de manejo de suelos agrarios, y se desarrollarán infraestructuras y procesos para la planificación energética y la gestión del agua, entre otras acciones.
This Plan has already been prepared but will be approved by the Municipal Council in the coming months.

Tema abordado en el Punto 9.3.1 del Plan.Liderazgo y alianzas estratégicas:Es fundamentalmente importante establecer una organización que lidere la realización de los proyectos concretos. La implementación del concepto energético tiene que ser un proceso participativo e inclusivo para que los actores claves se identifiquen con la visión Vitacura 30 / 30. El proceso participativo garantiza el acceso a la información, aumenta la aceptación de las medidas y genera una identificación con el concepto energético. Un enfoque participativo requiere mucho más tiempo, pero a medio y largo plazo es más beneficioso en la aplicación de las medidas.La organización debería estar liderada por el Municipio (con el alcalde como persona icónica). A nivel operativo, a medio plazo, se debería contratar una persona que esté a cargo de la implementación del plan energético. La conciencia y el cambio de comportamiento en la población local son clave. Por tanto, es esencial la rápida aplicación de proyectos concretos. Tienen que ser tangibles, visuales y generar una buena imagen en el Municipio para genera la confianza y la credibilidad necesaria de cumplir la Visión 30 / 30. Acciones comunicativas son importantes, pero por sí solas no son suficientes. Un punto importante a medio y largo plazo es la involucración de empresas privadas en la organización. En un primer paso, las empresas privadas deberían estar conectadas por la plataforma CleanTech.

The Municipality SECAP (Energy and climate strategy) shows how Västervik Municipality will contribute to the transition to a fossil-free society. By 2030, the municipality and the municipal companies together will be net climate positive, according to the internationally accepted model Greenhouse Gas Protocol (GHG) scope 1 - 3, expanded with avoided indirect emissions. The energy and climate strategy are divided into 5 target areas:• Reduced greenhouse gas emissions• Increased energy efficiency• Safe and secure energy supply• Increased local production of renewable energy• Adaptation to a changing climateThe action plan linked to the SECAP outlines 22 actions and activities to achieve the goals. • Energy and climate advice.• Tools for sustainable procurement.• Climate platform Västervik.• Fossil-free business travel and transport.• Fossil-free transport by sea and on railway.• Spatial planning to reduce emissions of greenhouse gas emissions.• Improved conditions for bicycle traffic and when changing between different modes of transport.• Improved infrastructure for charging electric vehicles.• Energy-efficient and climate-neutral construction.• Energy efficiency in association premises and meeting rooms.• Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions fromagriculture, through the production of biogas from manure.• Reduced greenhouse gas emissions from soiland forestry through the production and use of biochar.• Feasibility study on production and storage of hydrogen from solar electricity. • Transition to energy-efficient LED light sources in the public lighting.• Security in the energy system.• Development of district heating, combined heat and power (CHP) and Cooling.• Increased production of renewable electricity, for example solar electricity.spatial planning for adaptation to a changing climate;• Water retention measures in the landscapeThe analyses show that focus of the work to reduce emissions must be on transport, heating, and construction. Transport is a major source of greenhouse gas emissions. In concrete terms, it is about providing conditions to replace fossil petrol and diesel with other less environmentally harmful fuels such as renewable electricity, hydrogen, biogas, or biodiesel, as well as choosing vehicles that use as little energy/fuel as possible in relation to its function.

plan_name: San Rafael Climate Change Action Plan (
plan_comments: From the CCAP report page 33 - Community Engagement:The Climate Action Plan contains actions that the City can undertake to reduce its own emissions by about 1,500 MTCO2e, bringing the emissions from municipal operations down to 56% below 2005 levels. However, since emissions from governmental operations make up less than 1% of community-wide emissions, that is just a drop in the bucket. The fact is that our residents, businesses, workers, and visitors will haveto do their part to ensure we meet our reduction targets. The City can compel some of these actions by adopting ordinances and buildingregulations, but much of the success of our plan will depend on informing our community members and encouraging them to takeaction on their own.The City has been partnering with Resilient Neighborhoods (https://www.resilientneighborhoods.org/ ) since 2009 to educate San Rafael residents on ways they can reduce their carbon footprint. The program organizes Climate Action Teams of up to 12 households that meet five times over two months to learn about strategies and resources to improve home energy efficiency, shift to renewable energy, use low-carbon transportation, conserve water, reduce waste, and adapt to a changing climate. To start, participants calculate their household carbon footprint and then take actions to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions by at least 5,000 pounds or 25%. Pages 33-35 of the CCAP provide details of several community engagement measures to reduce community emissions.

plan_name: Catbalogan City Local Climate Change Adaptation Plan

plan_comments: According to a study conducted by Engr. Ronald Orale, and the abstract states that Catbalogan Sky City Mega Project (CSCMP) is a climate-change (CC) adaptation strategy proposed after Typhoon Haiyan devastated the Philippines in November 2013. It is currently being built on top of a hill about 120m from sea level to avoid the impact of storm surges, sea-level rise, and flooding. With the city’s continued expansion, water demand further worsens the supply gap. This study focused on determining the carbon footprint of the proposed water supply scarcity solution. This solution includes the construction of a reservoir to receive runoff water from the watershed where the CSCMP is located. Results of the study show that the reservoir can supply the water requirement for the entire city. However, the carbon footprint of the recommended solution is between 123% and 557% due to water treatment of heavily contaminated runoff water and the power consumption in distributing water to higher elevations. There is a need for the city to design a harvesting system that will reduce the need for more intense water treatment (i.e., reducing exposure of runoff water to contaminants) and the use of renewable energy in powering pumps and other treatment activities.

The presence of extreme weather events such as strong typhoons, storm surge, abnormal changes of weather are potent factors on the destruction of marine ecosystem.  In effect, fishermen are directly affected to these extreme weather events.

Currently, the City LGU has no waste water treatment facility, but there is already a project proposal for implementation. Good water waste management system will create a great impact on Health and sanitation condition of the city residents which also have a direct effect on the financial status of the people and the  economy of the city as a whole.

Shelter, especially those who were displaced due to government projects is among the priority projects of the City LGU. There is a proposed tenement, namely: Step North Payao, Pier 1 and Step South Lagundi.  The  Step North Payao and  Step South Lagundi Housing Project will be funded through the National Housing Authority, and  the Pier 1  tenement Building will be funded through Pag-ibig Fund, but there  is still no final project cost.
Agriculture is one of the major economic activity in  Catbalogan City which experiences drought as well as flooding.  Utilizing resilient variety of seeds will minimize losses.
