The Legazpi Climate Change Action Plan (LCCAP) 2019-2028 will be a ten-year plan that will correspond to the city Comprehensive Land Use Plan (CLUP) and Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP). The LCCAP will anchor on all the national mandates and frameworks mentioned earlier, as well as the regional and provincial frameworks. The plan also follows a set of globally applicable guiding principles for city climate action planning such as that developed by the UN Habitat. The LCCAP will provide sufficient analysis and detailed options for climate change adaptation and mitigation. As a derivative plan of the Comprehensive Development and Comprehensive Land Use Plans, a local climate action plan is necessary in responding to the city development goals, based on technical findings of climate impacts on the different ecosystems.The Plan includes the climate change information and situational analysis of the  different hazards such as seasonal temperature, seasonal rainfall, floods, storm surge, sea level rise, rain-induced landslide, lahar and strong winds .

The Forest Land Use Plan was built on the consensus to develop recommendations on the best uses of forestlands, including the important steps of identifying open access areas under appropriate management schemes, and provides the basis for allocating Forest and Forestlands under appropriate management and tenure arrangements, generating stewards of nature with established accountabilities and allocate FFL, as natural resource assets for appropriate management, tenure arrangement and putting these assets according to best use in order to harmonize the use of forestlands thus, attain balance of production activities within the context of forest protection, rehabilitation of watersheds and biodiversity conservation.

The CLUP 2019-2028 represents an amalgam of all the planning program elements. It shall serve as a general and long-range policy guide to the city’s future physical development and policy  directions. The plan depicts in textual and map form the land use patterns and general location of  development and conservation areas, the direction of urban expansion, the transportation arteries  and linkages, and growth nodes. Legazpi City has re-adopted the Land Use Plan for the past planning period, with some revisions, to accommodate the densification of urban activities through infilling development, and reduce the risks of disaster and the effects of climate change. The Land Use Plan Maps show the general distribution and extent of the land uses. It is instrumental in the development of the Zoning Map. The land use patterns of the land use plan map are also reflected in the zoning map to be optimally effective, and enable the desired land use patterns to occur.

The Legazpi City Zoning Ordinance was approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod through SP Ordinance No. 14-001-2019 and declared valid and operative by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of Albay through Provincial Resolution No. 00233-2019. Zoning is concerned primarily with the use of the land and regulation of development  through imposition of building heights, bulk, open space, and density provisions in a given area.  Zoning is the division of a city/municipality into zones or sub-zones (e.g. commercial, residential, industrial, institutional, agricultural, forest, etc.) according to present and potential uses of land to maximize, regulate and direct their use and development in accordance with the Comprehensive Land Use Plan. It takes the form of a locally enacted ordinance which provides, among others, regulations affecting uses allowed or disallowed in each zone or sub-zone, conditions for allowing them, and procedures on evaluating deviations. (HLURB Guidebook Volume 3)

Responding to the global call for ambitious and transformative climate actions, Legazpi City aims to move towards setting minimum energy performance for all energy-consuming devices, equipment and facilities, a cleaner and more efficient fuels and technologies for power generation and later on for transport, and a more effective treatment and disposal of its wastewater and solid waste generation as the interventions called as “activity” to curb emissions. In addition, the City also geared towards a more sustainable agriculture and forest management in order to enhance the “sinks” as the carbon sequestration initiatives which in turn improve the ecological system, the city believes that providing intervention to both “activity” and “sinks” is a robust action in achieving the goal towards low emission development

RA 9003 mandates that the responsibility of Solid Waste (SW) collection is under the jurisdiction of the 70 Barangays and Residual management is the responsibility of the City Government.In the implementation of the Legazpi City Ecological Solid Waste Management (ESWM) Program, the said responsibility are being practiced and enforced. In support to the new roles of the Barangay concerning segregated collection, the 49 identified Barangays being part of the ESWM Project are provided with SW collection equipments/tools and upgraded their competencies in terms of capability building not only to sustain its management and operations, continuous Information and Education Campaign (IEC) but most importantly to achieve waste diversion up to 30% to 50% of waste streams in the household level, to prolong the life of the Landfill which is designed to be filled-up in two years time if the City does not segregate.The advocacy of the City Government as regards to “No Segregation, No Collection” is strictly implemented. This intervention forms part of the policy of the City Government to achieve its goal as mandated by RA 9003.

Conservation has been given low priority. Investments have been insufficient, and at the same time, regulations need to be updated for effective enforcement. Water use zone classifications are often disregarded because people ignore or violate the rules which are loosely implemented. Weak enforcement and lack of effective registration and licensing of fishers and their paraphernalia result in uncontrolled utilization of the marine environment through IUU fishing activities. The fisheries and aquatic resources are abused and destroyed through inappropriate fishing practices, coastal developments and environmental stresses. Thus, the resources that people hope will sustain future generations as they have for many years are in danger of being exhausted. There is an urgent need to initiate measures that will ensure the maintenance and preservation of these resources to meet the needs of the present without compromising the environmental integrity.This plan applies to the management, utilization, conservation, protection, development and disposition of all coastal and fishery resources within the city waters of Legazpi as defined in Republic Act 7160 and other pertinent laws. The management of marine resources traditionally has been organized around particular uses such as fisheries or mineral exploitation, resulting in separate regimes governing its use. Over time it has become apparent that such a sectoral approach is inadequate for sustaining the goods and services that flow from ecosystems and often aggravates conflicts among users. As a result, a holistic ecosystem approach has emerged as the preferable basis for managing natural resources and the environment.A central element is the city water use zoning plan that identifies the specific uses of its zone to be designed to adopt each management area, prevent and resolve conflicting use of specific zones, for example, through the issuance of exclusive fishery privileges based on zoning. This must be supported by policy development, appropriate, updated legislation and regulation, strengthened law enforcement and institutional arrangements. The plan also emphasizes outreach and education programs with partners to raise public awareness.

The City’s Comprehensive Development Plan is anchored on the national long-term vision, or AmBisyon Natin 2040, which represents the collective vision and aspirations of Filipinos for themselves and for the country. The CDP also takes off from the National Government’s Socio- Economic Agenda and is informed by inputs from the cross-section of stakeholders and the general public. The technical analysis of the major development concerns was also guided by the Regional vision of making Bicol the most livable region in the Philippines.

Legazpi City is considered as the gateway to Bicol because of its relative proximity to the provinces of the region and its geographical location.  Being the region’s administrative seat and service center, The City is accessible to all modes of transportation: sea, air and land.  Sea and air transportation is manageable and stable, while land transportation was critically needs important attention.  Land transportation issues and problems contribute negative impact to social, economic and environmental situation of the City. This, then, threatens the Legazpeño’s and visitor quality of life and comfortable, fast and safe mobility. Majority of its people with low average income, especially in the rural areas, do not have access to private motorized transport, making them dependent on other modes.The City shall make an effort to provide adequate, effective, and efficient transportation facilities that would provide access and mobility for the people to pursue socio-economic activities, as reflected in City Comprehensive Land Use Plan, and in a better position to identify local public transport service requirements being in-charge of formulating the Comprehensive Land Use Plan and Transport and Traffic Management Plans.  The City public transport services should be provided in a timely, sufficient, cost-effective, and reliable manner. This is why the City Government of Legazpi through the City Council or Sangguniang Panlungsod (SP) wanted to pass a Local Public Transport Route Plan (LPTRP) to address problems, issues and concerns in relation to the looming worst situation of the local transport scenario in the city.

The Legazpi City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Plan 2020-2025 is a six-year plan to implement practical and doable measures to attain the objectives of the above-mentioned frameworks and that of the national, regional and provincial government. The highlights of this plan include the implementation of strong policies that address disaster resilience and the exacerbating effects of climate change to hazards, preserving our protective ecosystems, and a strong public-private partnership. The plan is still in drafting phase.

The Legazpi City's City Plan of Action on Marine Litter (CPOA-ML) was realized in support and localization of the Philippines' National Plan of Action on Marine Litter's (NPOA-ML). This CPOA-ML, in particular, covers policies and measures aimed at preventing, reducing, and managing marine litter. It encompasses not only the city government's efforts, but also those of all key stakeholders who play important roles in decreasing waste leakage from land and maritime sources into rivers, local waters, and, ultimately, the world's oceans.Aside from reducing the impact on land and marine life, tackling marine litter aids in resource and waste management difficulties, creating buy-in among stakeholders, facilitating partnerships, and preparing for opportunities for green socioeconomic recovery.The City of Legazpi conducted a series of multi-stakeholder consultations with support from the Healthy Oceans and Clean Cities Initiative (HOCCI), which is being implemented by the United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat) and funded by the Government of Japan, to analyze global and local marine litter issues, make an inventory of existing marine litter reduction and management initiatives, identify gaps and challenges, and develop the city's local plan.The City of Legazpi targets to focus on social enterprise development that aims to promote the reduce, reuse, recycle (3R) advocacy, marine litter baselining and capacity building, marine solid waste collection and transportation optimization through technology deployment, and establishment of centralized materials recovery facility.

The city government set the tone in sustainable practices with the energy efficient design of the city hall which does not utilize an airconditioning system. This alone, translated in an annual savings of approximately P1.2 million in electricity costs. Among the flagship programs of the San Carlos Sustainable City Project is the establishment of the first bioethanol plant in the country, which could generate forty million liters of ethanol annually or one-tenth of the national annual requirement. This substantially reduces the country’s reliance on imported fossil fuel.The city government also constructed the Translink Highway that connects San Carlos to the Capital city of Bacolod resulting in a 50% reduction in travel time. This highway also opened up access to remote mountain areas of the city resulting in increased economic activity in the rural areas. The Water Port was also expanded with the reclamation of four hectares of the sea. This extended the port to 242.5 meters long which made it more accessible to Roll-On, Roll-Off vessels. To ensure food self-sufficiency and security, the city government established Rural Growth Centers (RGCs) in three strategic mountain barangays, which serve as nodes of development in the rural areas. The two Agricultural Productivity Training Centers (APTCs) operating in two of the three RGCs have provided trainings in organic crop production as well as orientations on unsustainable economic activities to more than a thousand farming households. Agricultural productivity is evident with 36 tons of different vegetables and crops produced annually.In terms of environmental protection, the solid waste management program of the city is so effective that it has managed to divert 70% of the waste. The city has an Eco-Center which is an integrated facility for handling waste. It has a landfill as well as a composting and materials recovery facility. In addition, the program for upland eco-system management has resulted in the reforestation initiative covering hundreds of hectares. The coastal and marine resources are amply protected with the declaration of these areas as marine reserves.

The Environmental Sustainability Plan and Climate Action Plan (climate chapter of sustainability plan) were updated and published in 2023. The plan is intended to articulate Boulder County’s vision to create a more environmentally sustainable and just community and to provide a blueprint for achieving our collective climate action and resilience goals. The new plan includes updated science-based GHG emissions reduction targets, an updated and extended climate action plan chapter, updated data and includes new sections, such as nature-based solutions and carbon drawdown. This plan uses a “systems-wide” approach, taking careful account of the numerous and diverse components that provide the foundations for the quality of our environmental, economic, and social condition. The vast list of topics discussed throughout includes climate risk, climate accountability, agricultural viability, human health, forest health, energy production and use, resource consumption and preservation, transportation, air and water quality, and meaningful public participation.

This plan is aimed at improving the resilience of the county and its municipalities in the face of climate change.  As this was written in 2012, it does not use the term 'Climate Adaptation" but that essentially is what the plan's purpose is. It identifies the potential impacts of climate change, explores the implications of these changes in the context of resource management institutions, and outlines opportunities for adaptation planning efforts. This plan focuses on four key sectors: water supply, emergency management (EM), public health, and agriculture and natural resources.  It is intended to serve as a resource for county and municipal planners as they integrate climate change, in addition to other concerns, into their ongoing planning efforts.

This plan was created by Boulder County Parks and Open Space (POS). POS manages natural, cultural, agricultural, and recreational resources that are already experiencing impacts related to climate change. These impacts may increase in the future. In our 2020 Strategic Vision, the development and implementation of a policy for climate change adaptation and mitigation is an objective under Strategic Goal G: Adapt to Climate Change. This Climate Adaptation and Mitigation Policy (CAMP) outlines goals, objectives, and strategies for mitigating climate change impacts and promoting the adaptation of the resources most vulnerable to projected climate change. This plan targets the next several years of action. While the CAMP is specific to POS, it aligns with several county-wide efforts to prepare for climate change and improve resilience, including the Board of County Commissioners (BOCC) Strategic Priorities for Land and Water Stewardship, the 2018 Environmental Sustainability Plan, the Boulder County Climate Change Preparedness Plan (2012), the Boulder County Comprehensive Plan, and ongoing work at the Boulder County Office of Sustainability, Climate, and Resiliency.

Including:-Promoting EVs and reducing VMTs (transportation)- Increasing building and energy efficiency, increasing electrification to take advantage of the more renewable grid, installing more renewable solar-Promoting reuse, recycling and compost-reduce water use-Increase quality and amount of natural areas, to promote more carbon uptake and offset emissions. Attachments F (Scoping Report for Ecosystem Services) and G (Recommendations for Nature-Based Solutions) looked into trade-offs, co-benefits, and synergies.

There were city staff as well as community members involved in the creation of the plan.  also attached is our ongoing Climate Adaptation Plan.   While our Climate Action Plan addresses resilience, the planning process for this has been ongoing.

This report sets out the targets determined with the participation of the stakeholders of Antalya province and aimed at reducing emissions from energy consumption and greenhouse gases in different sectors. The most important bases of this report, the starting point of which is the Antalya province-scale greenhouse gas inventory, are the reports prepared or commissioned by Antalya Metropolitan Municipality and different institutions regarding the future of Antalya province, and the visions of the city stakeholders for the future of the city. In the preparation of the baseline Emissions Inventory, the activity-based approach most commonly used by cities was used. In this approach, all CO2e (or GHG) emissions from direct (through fuel combustion) or indirect (through electricity consumption) energy consumption in Antalya are included. Most GHG emissions are CO2 emissions, while CH4 and N2O emissions are of secondary importance for combustion processes in the residential and transportation sectors. All CO2, CH4 and CO2 emissions are calculated for all fuel types along with their global warming potential (GWP) using IPCC emission factors from the Fifth Assessment Report (AR5). One of the reasons for including emissions other than CO2 is that Antalya calculates emissions from waste (CH4), wastewater (CH4, N2O), enteric fermentation of livestock (CH4) and chemical fertilizers used in agriculture (N2O).

Plan de Acción para la Adaptación al Cambio Climático del Cantón Buenos Aires 2023-2030
plan_comments: Plan de Acción para la Adaptación al Cambio Climático del Cantón de Buenos Aires 2023-2030 el cual se fundamenta en:1. el uso de la metodología establecida en la Guía para la planificación de la adaptación ante el cambio climático desde el ámbito cantonal (DCC MINAE, 2021);2. la integración de tres enfoques transversales: i) integración vertical, ii) participación ciudadana y iii) equidad de género.3. un perfil local desarrollado a partir de la información disponible en la Municipalidad y alineado a lo indicado en el diagnóstico territorial del Plan Regulador;4. un análisis de riesgo asociado al clima utilizando una metodología cuantitativa con información oficial y disponible, que se complementa con ejercicios participativos para identificar los peligros e impactos asociados al clima dentro del cantón;5. las principales necesidades y oportunidades cantonales para reducir sus factores de vulnerabilidad y exposición, así como evitar y disminuir pérdidas y daños;6. la integración de los enfoques de adaptación integrada: i) adaptación basada en ecosistemas, ii) adaptación basada en comunidades y iii) gestión del riesgo;7. la identificación de una serie de medidas de adaptación mediante un proceso participativo, las cuales se alinean con la visión de adaptación y con las prioridades de la Política Nacional de Adaptación al Cambio Climático 2018-2030 (PNACC) y la planificación local, así como enfoque directo o indirecto con los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible (ODS) y el Marco de Sendai;8. el diseño de medidas que incluyen: a) soluciones basadas en naturaleza, adaptación basada en comunidades y que procurarán un manejo adaptativo de los recursos naturales y de la biodiversidad, y b) de la gestión del riesgo, con énfasis en medidas preventivas, incluidas las llamadas medidas de obras grises (estructurales);9. la integración de mecanismos para su implementación, monitoreo y evaluación, basados en las capacidades locales.

El Programa Municipal para la Acción ante el Cambio Climático (PMACC) de Zapopan, surgió de una adhesión voluntaria en el año 2019 al Pacto Global de Alcaldes por el Clima y la Energía, para la elaboración del mismo se contó con financiamiento por parte del Programa Internacional de Cooperación Urbana (IUC) y la Unión Europea.Previo proceso de consulta pública y en Sesión Ordinario celebrada el 28 de abril de 2022, se aprobó el Dictamen y Propuesta de Comisiones bajo el expediente 45/2022, por medio del cual se aprobó el Programa Municipal para la Acción ante el Cambio Climático del Municipio de Zapopan, mismo que fue publicado en la Gaceta Municipal bajo el volumen XXIX, no. 75, Segunda Época, el 12 de mayo de año 2022. El Programa contempla la realización de 15 medidas de mitigación (2 en materia de energía,  8 en materia de transporte y 5 en materia de residuos) y 8 medidas de adaptación (4 en materia de riesgos, 3 acciones en materia de manejo sustentable de los recursos naturales y conservación de la biodiversidad y 1 consistente en la habilitación del Fondo Municipal para el Cambio Climático y Resiliencia).Para hacer frente a las obligaciones y acciones previstas en el PMACC, se constituirá el Fondo Municipal para el Cambio Climático y Resiliencia, a que se refieren los artículo 19 y 20 del Reglamento de Cambio Climático y Resiliencia del Municipio de Zapopan, Jalisco.En congruencia con los acuerdos internacionales suscritos por el Gobierno de México, la política nacional y estatal de cambio climático, y demás normativa aplicable, el Programa Municipal tiene objetivos encaminados a contar con una mejor sociedad, mejor territorio y mejor ciudad de acuerdo al Plan Municipal de Desarrollo y Gobernanza (PMDG), el objetivo general es integrar, coordinar e impulsar acciones públicas en el Municipio para disminuir la huella de carbono y los riesgos ambientales, sociales y económicos derivados del cambio climático.Estos objetivos fortalecen y consolidan el camino para el cumplimiento de la visión institucional que tiene Zapopan descrita en su PMDG, misma que se adopta como visión del Programa: 1.- Reducir (o limitar cuando relevantes) las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero. 2.- prepararse para los impactos del cambio climático. 3.- Aumentar el acceso a la energía sostenible y asequible dentro de nuestra competencia jurídica y jurisdiccional).

plan_name: Plano de Adaptação e Resiliencia Á Mudança do Clima de Francisco Morato
plan_comments: Pensar em Políticas Públicas que trará qualidade de vida à população moratense, acesso a espaços públicos seguros, inclusivos, acessíveis e verdes, como preconiza a Agenda 2030 e os ODS, especialmente o ODS 11. Cidades e Comunidades Sustentáveisque propõe tornar as cidades e os assentamentos humanos inclusivos, seguros, resilientes e sustentáveis, em especial para as mulheres e crianças, pessoas idosas e pessoas com deficiência, é pauta imprescindível.O Plano de Adaptação e Resiliência à Mudança Climática de Francisco Morato reúne em suas páginas uma história de muitos esforços em proporcionar à população uma vida digna, de direito, que “não deixa ninguém e nenhum lugar para trás”.  Esta história, que hoje se materializa neste importante instrumento de política pública, se iniciou em 2017, com a reestruturação da Defesa Civil de Francisco Morato, que passou a atualizar o mapeamento das áreas de riscos, para que pudéssemos criarestratégias mais amplas de prevenção. Em vez da Defesa Civil de FRANCISCO MORATO atuar meramente nas respostas pós desastre, assertivamente fomos priorizando a prevenção, a capacitação das comunidades para transformar condições perigosas (riscos) e reduzir as vulnerabilidades locais.Como atores comprometidos no alcance da resiliência local, não mediremos esforços para uma pauta tão necessária, até porque,falar sobre mudanças climáticas é falar das Dimensões Humanas, é falar das pessoas. E alinhar as expectativas da nossa comunidade, com foco na Agenda 2030, é a maior  ferramenta para garantia de promoção de política pública. Falaremos do acesso à moradia segura, do saneamento básico, do desenvolvimento de emprego e renda, da saúde, da educação e meio ambiente, enfim, desse imprescindível processo de transformação social atravessado pelas questões econômicas e ambientais.Nosso propósito com esse Plano de Adaptação e Resiliência à Mudança Climática é orientar e qualificar ainda mais as práticas de redução de desastres; é mais um passo nessa caminhada.Espero, com esse projeto, como disse Paulo Freire, “que ninguém caminhe sem aprender a caminhar, sem aprender a fazer o caminho caminhando, refazendo e retocando o sonho pelo qual se pôs a caminhar”!

plan_comments: As medidas constantes deste Plano têm por base o diagnóstico de utilização de energia e emissões no Município e as vertentes de maior ação direta das Autoridades Locais, centrando-se no Edificado, Transportes e Iluminação Pública. Este Plano torna claro que o grande contributo para a redução de emissões relaciona-se menos com as infraestruturas sob gestão direta das Autoridades Locais, mas com a ação dos diversos atores que desenvolvem a sua atividade no Município.Dado o caráter evolutivo de vários fatores influentes para este Plano, como sejam as soluções tecnológicas, as condições económicas e possibilidades de financiamento, bem como riscos associados a fenómenos como a recente COVID-19, está prevista a contínua monitorização e revisão. Este processo permitirá manter o Plano dinâmico, sempre alinhando as medidas com melhor prossecução dos objetivos definidos. Os impactos de médio-longo prazo, nomeadamente em cenários de evolução sócio-económica, são de momento ainda desconhecidos ou especulativos, pelo que não considerados neste exercício.

El inventario cubre las emisiones generadas a partir de consumo de energía en unidades estacionarias, transporte, residuos, procesos industriales y uso de productos, y agricultura, silvicultura y otros usos del suelo (AFOLU). Las mismas se dividen en tres alcances (alcance 1: todas las emisiones directas de GEI; alcance 2: emisiones indirectas de GEI asociadas al consumo de electricidad, calor o vapor adquiridos, y alcance 3: otras emisiones indirectas, como las asociadas a la extracción y producción de materiales y combustibles comprados, actividades relacionadas con el transporte en vehículos que no son propiedad o controlados por la entidad analizada y actividades relacionadas con la electricidad no cubiertas en el alcance 2 (ej., pérdidas en la transmisión y distribución), actividades subcontratadas, disposición de desechos, etc.).El estudio de riesgo climatico se ha basado en los lineamientos dados por el Quinto Informe de Evaluación del Grupo Intergubernamental de Expertos sobre el Cambio Climático (IPCC) en torno a las variables de amenaza climática, exposición, sensibilidad y capacidad de adaptación.Dentro de los escenarios futuros de cambio climático proyectados por el IPCC en su AR5 y los usados por los estudios de regionalización para la Tercera Comunicación Nacional del Ecuador, se ha trabajado con el RCP 4,5 y el RCP 8,5. Se utilizaron estos dos escenarios, puesto que el primero (RCP 4,5) es el que más se ajusta a Ecuador si las condiciones y tendencias climáticas actuales se mantuviesen iguales en el presente siglo ( MAE; PNUD, 2016). Por otro lado, el escenario RCP 8,5 es el que muestra las peores condiciones futuras proyectadas y, por lo tanto, sería el máspesimista al que se podría enfrentar la ciudad

During 2020 we worked with industry experts The Carbon Trust and consulted with a wide range of stakeholders in the production of our 'Climate Emergency Action Plan'.The Carbon Trust action plan set out emissions scenario analyses to help the Borough understand how it may be able to meet its net-zero target.  Four scenarios were produced: ‘Business as usual’, ‘Energy efficiency and alternative fuels’, ‘High electrification’ and ‘Radical response’. The scenarios achieve a 26%, 86%, 85% and 94% reduction in emissions respectively from the 2017 baseline to the target year in 2050, with a variety of interim targets / pathways. The Council published a new ambitious policy commitment in 'Our North Tyneside Plan Council Plan 2021- 25', committing the authority to the publishing of an action plan of the steps we will take and the national investment we will seek to make North Tyneside carbon net zero by 2030 - see the action plan attached. This target for net-zero carbon emissions across the borough is now well ahead of the UK government's target of achieving carbon net zero by 2050. Thee plan also covers adaptation, reflecting the short, medium- and long-term climate change risks to residents, business, and infrastructure as a result of hotter and drier summers, more intense rainfall, stronger winds and more storminess, and warmer winters. The council undertook to deliver a risk-based revision of climate adaptation to its service provision using the BEIS sponsored Climate Adaptation toolkit and its five-step process. This work builds upon previous risk assessments undertaken in line with the government’s former National Indicator 188. The climate adaptation toolkit can be found here:  https://my.northtyneside.gov.uk/sites/default/files/web-page-related-files/Climate%20Adaptation%20Toolkit%20Booklet%20Feb%2023.pdf

The City of Richmond uses a Dike Master Planning program to guide future dike upgrading projects, and to ensure that land development adjacent to the dike is compatible with flood protection objectives. The program includes 4 phases for the 49 km of the Lulu Island perimeter dike in Richmond, and an additional 5th phase for Sea Island, Mitchell Island, and Richmond Island. The goal is to raise the dikes to 4.7 m CGVD28 to allow for 1 m of sea level rise and 0.2 m of land subsidence, while allowing for further upgrading in the future. The longterm vision is to provide the City with a world-class level of flood protection to keep pace with the rapidly growing population and assets within the dikes.

On December 9, 2019, City of Richmond Council adopted the City of Richmond Public Tree Management Strategy 2045, A Plan for Managing Richmond’s Public Urban Forest. This Strategy demonstrates the City’s continued commitment to maintaining a healthy urban forest and the prudent management of this valuable natural asset. The document outlines the goals and objectives for the sustainable stewardship of all City-owned trees for the next 25 years.

The strategy provides a framework for managing and guiding decisions regarding the city-wide system of natural areas in Richmond and the ecosystem services they provide on City, public and private lands. This strategy, intended to be opportunistic and collaborative, will set out priority areas, initiatives and projects for the on-going and long-term implementation of the Ecological Network (EN). There are many City actions, initiatives and projects currently underway that are supported by a range of regional and City policies, regulations and plans. The Strategy will seek to complement, align and inform the current planning and regulatory context in order to strengthen and enhance Richmond’s natural spaces.

Tagum City Local Climate Change Action Plan
plan_comments: The Tagum City Local Climate Change Action Plan covers the following sectors:a. Supporting sustainable farming practices, promoting local and resilient food systems, and reducing emissions from agricultural activities. b. Promoting energy-efficient practices in buildings, encouraging the use of solar panels or wind turbines, and supporting initiatives for sustainable transportation. c. Strategies for reducing waste generation, promoting recycling and composting, and implementing waste-to-energy projects. d. Involve expanding bike lanes, introducing electric or hybrid buses, implementing carpooling programs, and promoting walkability.e. Building green infrastructure to manage stormwater, promoting water-efficient practices, and safeguarding water sources.f. Involve zoning changes to encourage mixed land use, promoting green spaces, and designing climate-resilient infrastructure.g.  Initiatives to enhance healthcare infrastructure and improve community resilience to climate-related health risks.h. Supporting green businesses, promoting sustainable tourism, and fostering innovation in clean technologies.i. Community workshops, educational campaigns, and integrating climate education into school curricula.j. Providing equitable access to resources, social safety nets, and disaster preparedness plans.

plan_name: Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan
plan_comments: The Tagum City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan covers the following sectors:a. Identifying potential hazards that the city might face, such as earthquakes, floods, typhoons, landslides, and others. It also assesses the vulnerability of the city's infrastructure, population, and environment to these hazards.b. Establishment and improvement of systems that can provide advance notice of impending disasters, allowing authorities to take appropriate actions.c. Identifying evacuation routes, establishing temporary shelters, and ensuring that vulnerable populations are taken care of.d. Ensuring the resilience of critical infrastructure and lifelines, such as roads, bridges, communication networks, and utilities, to withstand the impact of disasters and recover quickly.e. Plans for emergency medical response, the establishment of field hospitals, and ensuring that health facilities remain operational during and after disasters.f. Training emergency response teams, acquiring necessary equipment, and coordinating with relevant agencies.g. Community awareness campaigns, training programs, and the development of disaster-ready communities.h. Initiatives for reforestation, soil conservation, and preserving wetlands.i. Setting up disaster insurance schemes, contingency funds, and financial arrangements for recovery.j. Establishing coordination mechanisms, clarifying roles, and ensuring efficient governance during disasters.k. Strategies for rebuilding infrastructure, restoring livelihoods, and providing psychosocial support to affected individuals.

The Tagum City Promise covers:a. strategies to increase energy efficiency, promote renewable energy sources (such as solar, wind, hydro), and transition away from fossil fuels.b.  Improving public transit, promoting electric and hybrid vehicles, enhancing cycling and pedestrian infrastructure, and optimizing urban planning to reduce the need for private vehicle use.c. Adopting green building standards, retrofitting existing buildings for energy efficiency, and promoting the use of energy-efficient appliances.d. Waste reduction, recycling, and waste-to-energy initiatives. This sector aims to minimize emissions from waste while promoting sustainable waste management practices.e. Sustainable land use practices, such as reforestation, afforestation, and promoting agroforestry, can help absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Address reducing emissions from agricultural activities and promoting sustainable farming practices.f. Sustainable water management practices, including reducing water waste and improving water quality.g. Supporting the development and adoption of technologies that help reduce emissions across various sectors.h. Establishing policies, regulations, and incentives that encourage low-carbon practices is essential.i. Raise public awareness about climate change and the benefits of low emission development.j. How to secure funding for sustainable projects and attract investment in low-carbon initiatives.

plan_name: Tagum City Energy Efficiency and Conservation Plan
plan_comments: The LEECP covers the following sectors:-Transportation-This sector encompasses strategies to improve fuel efficiency and promote sustainable transportation options like electric vehicles, public transit, and biking.-Agriculture- involve energy-intensive practices such as irrigation and machinery use. Energy efficiency plans might suggest more efficient irrigation systems or renewable energy sources for farms.-Public Buildings-Government buildings and public facilities are included in energy efficiency this plan, focusing on lighting, heating, cooling, and overall energy management.-Renewable Energy Integration-This also address the integration of renewable energy sources like solar, wind, and hydropower into the energy mix.-Education and Awareness-Promoting energy-saving behaviors and educating the public about energy-efficient practices.

plan_name: Tagum Water Supply and Sanitation Plan
plan_comments: a. The plan would focus on ensuring reliable and safe drinking water for residents. It involves improving water treatment facilities, expanding distribution networks, and reducing water losses through leak detection and maintenance.b. The plan addresses proper disposal and treatment of wastewater. Strategies include upgrading sewage treatment plants, implementing efficient sewer systems, and promoting proper sanitation practices.c. The plan addresses monitoring and maintaining the quality of water sources, ensuring compliance with water quality standards, and responding to water quality issues promptly.d. An important aspect is educating the public about water conservation, sanitation practices, and the importance of responsible water usage.e. Given the potential impacts of climate change, the plan considers consider measures to ensure the water supply system's resilience to extreme weather events and natural disasters.f. The plan focus on developing financial schemes to support the maintenance and expansion of water supply and sanitation infrastructure over the long term.g. The plan includes recommendations for updating or establishing policies and regulations related to water supply, sanitation, and environmental protection.

plan_name: Tagum City Integrated Coastal Management Plan
plan_comments: 1. The plan focuses on the preservation, restoration, and sustainable use of coastal ecosystems, such as mangroves, coral reefs, dunes, and estuaries. 2. The plan also addresses the development and regulation of human activities along the coast, including urban expansion, infrastructure development, and zoning regulations to prevent unsustainable land use.3. Given the vulnerability of coastal areas to climate change impacts, the plan involves strategies to adapt to rising sea levels, increased storm frequency, and other climate-related challenges.4. The plan covers sustainable fishing practices, responsible aquaculture, and the protection of marine biodiversity.5. The plan also addresses pollution from coastal development, shipping, and agriculture is crucial. 6. This plan involves raising awareness among local communities and stakeholders about the importance of coastal management, sustainable practices, and climate change impacts.7. This plans includes the protection of marine and coastal biodiversity, including endangered species and critical habitats.8. The plan encompasses strategies to support local economies, enhance livelihoods, and promote sustainable economic activities that don't compromise coastal ecosystems.

plan_name: Tagum City Smar ICT Master Plan
plan_comments: Smart Governance- focuses on using technology to enhance government services, improve transparency, and engage citizens in decision-making processes.Smart Infrastructure-involve utilizing ICT to manage and optimize urban infrastructure, including transportation systems, utilities, and waste management.Smart Mobility- implementation of intelligent transportation systems, traffic management, public transit enhancements, and solutions to reduce congestion and promote sustainable mobility.Smart Energy-using ICT to monitor and manage energy consumption, promote energy efficiency, and integrate renewable energy sources.Smart Environment-using technology to monitor air and water quality, manage waste more effectively, and implement measures to mitigate environmental impacts.Smart Education-Utilizing technology to enhance education and provide e-learning opportunities is a key focus, especially given the potential of ICT to bridge educational gaps.Smart Safety and Security- implementing advanced security and emergency response systems, such as surveillance, disaster management, and public safety apps.Smart Connectivity-Ensuring robust and reliable digital connectivity throughout the city is a critical foundation for various smart initiatives.Smart Citizen Engagement- involve citizens in the planning and implementation of smart initiatives, using digital platforms for communication and feedback.Smart Health-Implementing digital health solutions for remote healthcare services, telemedicine, and health data management could be included.Smart Agriculture-involve using technology to support local farmers, promote sustainable agricultural practices, and enhance food security.Smart Water Management-Using ICT to monitor and manage water resources efficiently is crucial, especially in regions prone to water scarcity.Smart Waste Management-This sector involves optimizing waste collection and disposal using sensors, data analytics, and efficient routing.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: El PACMetro  (Plan de Acción Climática Metropolitano) con el que se trabaja en conjunto con los Municipios de la Zona Metropolitana para implementar las diferentes líneas de acción que atañen a todos y cada uno de los Municipios.
plan_comments: Para la creación del Plan de Acción Climática Metropolitano (PACMetro), en el que se implementan diferentes líneas de acción para todos los Municipios involucrados, pero no siendo limitantes estas líneas para el Municipio, si no que nuestro Municipio, adhiere más líneas de acción al PACMUN. En razón de lo expuesto este Gobierno Municipal, es líder en siete líneas de acción del PACMetro, siendo estas las que se enlistan a continuación:  1.Gobierno con buen papel: Implementación del programa transversal ¨Gobierno con buen papel¨ para la recolección, reutilización, valorización y reciclaje del papel desechado por las dependencias del municipio de Tlaquepaque. 2.Renovar y modernizar el alumbrado público tradicional por tecnología LED en la red vial primaria de Tlaquepaque. 3.Promover la certificación del desempeño energético en edificación e instalaciones a través de incentivos fiscales para su renovación. 4.Crear plan de reforestación continua intermunicipal en zonas de alta fragilidad para la conservación y mejoramiento de áreas forestales a su vez generar una reducción en cuanto a la generación de olas de calor presencia en agentes contaminantes del aire. 5.Modificación del reglamento para la creación de incentivos fiscales que promuevan la Naturación Urbana municipal. 6.Creación del Plan de Reforestación continua para sitios prioritarios. 7.Crear e implementar sistemas de vigilancia y atención a afectaciones a la salud relacionadas con riesgos climáticos para la población en el AMG.

Climate 2050 is Metro Vancouver's climate strategy for achieving a carbon neutral, resilient region. Climate 2050 is organized around ten Issue Areas, intended to provide logical groupings of climate goals, strategies, and actions. They reflect the functions and responsibilities under Metro Vancouver’s mandate and the range of climate-related challenges and initiatives affecting the region. Each Issue Area may consider climate adaptation and greenhouse gas reductions and it is intended that climate strategies and actions may meet goals in more than one Issue Area. The ten issue areas are: Nature & Ecosystems; Industry & Business; Water & Wastewater Infrastructure; Energy; Land-Use and Urban Form; Buildings; Agriculture; Transportation; Waste; Human Health & Well-being

The Clean Air Plan focuses on actions that Metro Vancouver can implement under our delegated authority. The Plan also identifies where other governments and partner agencies need to take action to help achieve our regional vision and 2030 targets. The Clean Air Plan is organized around six issue areas: Transportation; Buildings; Industry; Agriculture; Health; and Measure, Monitor, and Regulate.

plan_comments: El diseño y el proceso metodológico que se utilizó para formular el Plan de AcciónClimática Municipal -PACMUN-, se basa en el método cualitativo y cuantitativo.Cualitativo, porque la información generada en el tema de adaptación, describe un cruce deinformación entre el trabajo que ha realizado el programa de Gestión de Riesgo deDesastres del ICC, para analizar la vulnerabilidad de la zona ante eventos climáticos en losúltimos años y el trabajo que se realizó actualmente, donde de forma participativa, seanalizó la vulnerabilidad de los medios de vida ante amenazas climáticas, con la finalidadde que los actores locales compartieran la realidad municipal y analizarán las condicionesclimáticas que están afrontando y así determinar cuáles serán los ejes a trabajar para alcázarel desarrollo y la adaptación al cambio climático. En los últimos años la red vial de algunas zonas en el distrito de Ate se ha extendido progresivamente, de acuerdo con la ocupación urbana que se ha venido dando a lo largo de las avenidas principales en forma desordenada e irregular,  sumado a ello la congestión vehicular y la falta de áreas verdes en distrito. lo que conlleva a una percibir una mayor sensación térmica en el distrito en épocas de verano para ello se propone ampliar y mejorar las áreas verdes en el distrito para hacerle frente a los efectos del cambio climático.Asimismo la falta agua es uno de los problemas que aquejan al distrito de ate, para ello se propone dar mayor a las aguas provenientes de los canales de irrigación y se proponen 5 proyectos de riego tecnificado así como el establecimiento de plantas nativas y resistentes a la sequia.Finalmente para reducir el impacto por riesgo de huaycos, se propone mejorar la línea de comunicación ente las diversas instituciones siendo Centro de Operaciones de Emergencia Nacional, Defensa Civil , Instituto Nacional de Defensa Civil y Servicio Nacional de Metereología e Hidrología del Perú.

plan_name: (1) Durham Community Climate Adaptation Plan (DCCAP) and, (2) the 2019 Durham Community Energy Plan (DCEP).
plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: The DCEP and DCCAP plans articulate how Durham Region, and its local area municipal partners can collaborate to advance the transition to a low carbon, climate resilient future with community GHG reduction targets for the Region.
plan_comments: Implementation of the Low Carbon Pathway from the Durham Community Energy Plan (DCEP) would  increase investment by $1 billion per year to cover the cost of making those investments more energy efficient and renewable. However, this $31 billion incremental investment over 30 years would pay back $40 billion in energy savings and other revenues meaning the investment would pay for itself.  Meanwhile, the Durham Community Climate Adaptation Plan (DCCAP) assumes a proposed Ontario Climate Resilience Fund designed to assist municipalities to make critical investments in the infrastructure and programs necessary to protect their communities against the extreme weather associated with the changing climate in Ontario.  The Fund has been assumed to have an initial provincial contribution of $500 million. The provincial contribution has been assumed to be ramped up each year by about $200 million. By 2020 the Fund would meet its designed maximum provincial contribution of $1 billion/year until 2025. DCEP also proposes rationale, operations, effectiveness and size of the fund to be thoroughly evaluated in 2024 with possible revision and renewal in 2025 and 5-year increments thereafter.

plan_comments: As per the Climate Emergency Response Plan: Phase 1: Resilience, Annualized damages from flood and heat in Whitby are estimated to increase sevenfold by 2070 to more than $700 million per year in the absence of action using a Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 8.5 model. Collectively the adaptation actions have the potential to reduce the annualized damages by 2070 to $65.8 million, representing a 27% decrease from 2020 levels and a 90% decrease over 2070 levels compared to a business-as-usual scenario.

plan_name: Blackburn with Darwen Climate Emergency Action Plan.  Please note, this document is being formatted for publication.  It will shortly be uploaded to the Council's web site.  The previous plan can be found here:
plan_comments: The Council declared a Climate Emergency in 2019 and pledged to work towards becoming a carbon neutral Borough by 2030. The Declaration was supported by a Climate Emergency Action Plan (CEAP) comprising activities ranging from quick wins to longer-term aspirations, grouped under five objectives:1)Make sound climate-related decisions  - to use resources sustainably so as not to add to the burden of climate change emissions in Blackburn with Darwen2)Create a resilient & attractive Borough - to align policy to climate change mitigation and adaptation objectives to create sustainable places where people want to live, work and visit and capture the benefits to health and the economy from the move to a climate-friendly borough;3)Be lean and clean with resources and energy - to use energy more efficiently and generate more locally from renewable sources, cut waste and improve recycling4)Travel lightly - to make and facilitate the transition to cleaner, greener fuels and more active travel5)Capture more carbon - to store carbon naturally by increasing tree cover, protecting soils and enhancing natural habitatsPriorities for 2023/24 include:- Working with the People's Jury on the Climate Change Crisis to implement their specific recommendations;- Techno-economic feasibility of heat networks in our town centres;- Implementing a programme of energy efficiency measures in low income homes, funded by the Home Upgrade Grant ;- Planting trees;- Introducing EVs to the fleet;- Establishing a network with other stakeholders to progress collaborative working on climate action- Progressing Lancashire-wide action to secure shared zero carbon outcomes- Working with the Blackburn with Darwen Food Alliance to bring good, sustainably produced food to our communityWe have not yet fully quantified the cost of our proposals.

plan_boundary_desc: Wider boundary: The Greater Lafayette Climate Action Plan includes the jurisdictions of West Lafayette, Lafayette, and Tippecanoe County.
plan_comments: The Greater Lafayette Climate Action Plan (GLCAP) addresses the importance of acting to mitigate and adapt to climate change within the Greater Lafayette, which includes West Lafayette, Lafayette, and Tippecanoe County. The plan includes technical studies, such as a Greenhouse Gas Inventory, Hoosier Resilience Index Assessment, and Tree Canopy Assessment that, along with studies of global emissions, aim to inform the science-based reduction targets of 58% of greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 and 80% of greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.  Strategies for implementation are divided into four focus areas: Energy Use in the Built Environment, Transportation, Agriculture and Forestry, and Water, Wastewater, and Solid Waste. Listed within each of the focus areas are goals, with one goal from each focus area being denoted by the public as a Public Priority Goal, and listed within each goal are numerous actions along with a general model for implementation costs, co-benefits, and implementation timelines. The mission of the GLCAP is "to build a resilient Greater Lafayette by designing policies and implementing initiatives to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and protect equitable quality of life for current and future generations."

The consultant was Sustainability Solutions Group. Total cost of implementing plan was not provided although an assessment of investment opportunities (marginal abatement cost curve) was completed for emissions reduction versus cost/savings (page 40 of the Climate Action Plan).

plan_comments: The Wandsworth Environment and Sustainability Strategy (WESS) and action plan was developed by climate change focused officers in conjunction with central policy unit, with significant engagement and involvement from all service areas of the organisation.The WESS sets out our vision for the borough, what we have achieved so far in Wandsworth, the challenges we face, and the actions we will take to address these challenges over the short, medium and long term. By implementing the Strategy, we will improve the overall sustainability of our Borough, improve the health and wellbeing of residents, as well as reducing future costs.The key climate issues mentioned in the report are below. For each of these, the council’s objectives, actions, and targets are discussed.Sustainable Transport- Including a new cycling strategy, banning diesel cars from Council office estate and making all fleet vehicles non-fossil fuel poweredAir Quality- Including dirtiest buses to be replaced with cleaner, less polluting models, expansion of the number of school streets and increased communication around air qualityEnergy Management- Including continued monitoring of energy consumption, investment in energy efficiency measures and work on schools decarbonisationUrban Greening and Open Spaces- Including promotion of Wandsworth as a Green borough, piloting an ‘adopt a tree’ campaign and expansion and connection of core biodiversity areasWaste Management- Including reduction in singe use plastics from council operations, increase in % of waste recycled and reduction in total waste collected from municipal and residential buildingsSustainable Procurement- Including revieing all contracts to ensure they encompass Social Value elementWater Management and Flooding Resilience- Including development of green and blue roofs on residential and commercial building and maintenance of flooding hotspot registerPlanning and Sustainable Development- Including zero carbon target by 2050, development of decarbonisation strategy and increase district heating network

The Wandsworth Environment and Sustainability Strategy (WESS) Action Plan supports the WESS. The Action Plan outlines specific actions that will be delivered on over specific timescales. These actions are covered in the followed themes: carbon neutral by 2030; enabling sustainable and low carbon transport; improving air quality; decarbonising buildings across the borough; improving biodiversity and green spaces; reducing waste and increasing the circular economy; climate adaptation and resilience; and communication, engagement and partnership. The Action Plan is reported on annually, to monitor progress. Further, the Action Pan is updated annually, to ensure the actions are still keeping us on track to meet our overarching targets and also represent the latest knowledge of climate risks. This update includes outlines or specific measures/targets that will be taken to meet the actions.

plan_comments: The Richmond Climate Emergency Strategy (RCES) outlines the risks and impacts of climate change in Richmond and how the council will become carbon neutral as an organisation by 2030. It also outlines borough-wide initiatives on mitigation and energy efficiency; waste, plastics, and the circular economy; air quality; green infrastructure and biodiversity; and water abatement and flood management. The RCES also details how it is linked with other relevant council strategies (for example, the Local Flood Risk Management Strategy), to ensure that climate actions are imbedded into and across different council departments.In addition to the consultation highlighted above, officers leading on climate change have either called or met officer leads from local authorities that have been highlighted nationally as leading the way in terms of their approach to climate change, including Bristol, Stroud, Manchester, and Edinburgh. Officers have also met with key local partners, including Achieving for Children and South West London Environmental Network. In order to get a London perspective, officers have also met or spoken to officers in other London Boroughs, including Camden, Ealing, Hackney, Hounslow, Kingston, and Redbridge. Officers have also attended London-wide officer network events, including the London Environment Directors Network (LEDNet) Climate Change Group, the LEDNET policy group, and the London Policy and Strategy Network. On a sub-regional basis, the South London Partnership has also started working on climate change as a key issue and officers are involved in work here. In order to understand the work of leading climate change charities and organisations and identify how the best practice they have identified can be applied in Richmond, meetings and calls have been held with UK100, Climate Outreach, Possible, and the Centre for Sustainable Energy. Officers have also attended conferences and events organised by groups such as Friends of the Earth, Nesta, Carbon Literacy Trust, Royal Geographical Society, and Localis.

plan_name: Richmond Climate Emergency Strategy Action Plan 2023:
plan_comments: The Richmond Climate Emergency Strategy Action Plan supports the Richmond Climate Emergency Strategy (RCES). The Action Plan outlines specific actions that will be delivered on over specific timescales. These actions are covered in the followed themes: our council (becoming carbon neutral as an organisation by 2030); our legacy (reducing direct carbon emissions and improving energy efficiency); our waste (waste, plastics and the circular economy); our air (improving local air quality); our nature (green infrastructure and biodiversity); and our resilience (climate resilience and flooding). The Action Plan is reported on annually, to monitor progress. Further, the Action Pan is updated annually, to ensure the actions are still keeping us on track to meet our overarching targets and also represent the latest knowledge of climate risks. This update includes outlines or specific measures/targets that will be taken to meet the actions.

The plan mostly focuses on GHG reduction.

This annual integrated action plan is monitored throughout each year and evaluated in December each year being scrutinised at our Cabinet and Full Council Meetings. We are currently delivering our Year 4 Climate Emergency Action Plan and in our end of year action plan we assign a Red/Green/Amber rating system to the progress made on each project and we also publicly report our progress on area wide emissions, renewable energy, council direct emissions and we are just introducing nature recovery goals to this monitoring process. This report is prepared by council officers in the Climate and Nature Emergency team, with input from other specialist officers and also external stakeholders.

Within our Net Zero Action plan, actions for carbon reduction are identified in six areas:- Transport - To deliver low-carbon, affordable transport choices for all - Building and energy - To reduce demand for energy, support the switch to low carbon energy and heat, and increase renewable energy generation - Resources and waste - To support the transition to a circular economy including the sustainable extraction, use and end of life management of natural resources - Business and economy - To help businesses to “go green”, grow the County’s low carbon economy and increase demand for low carbon goods and services - Community - To inform, engage and involve residents and communities in identifying and delivering local solutions to achieve net zero carbon - Nature and land use - To develop a growing and resilient network of land and water that is richer in plants and wildlife, optimises carbon storage and supports climate resilience

Within the plan we have identified the following groups of emission sources:▪ Estates – Carbon emissions from council buildings including, energy consumption (e.g. electricity and gas use), refrigerant gases, water use and waste generation, alongside the opportunities to generate local renewable energy and sequester carbon across the estate.▪ Fleet – Carbon emissions from fuel used (e.g. diesel and petrol) in vehicles operated by the council.▪ Business travel – Carbon emissions associated with staff journeys undertaken for work purposes in staff vehicles, also known as ‘grey fleet’ (not including council operated fleet vehicles and employee commuting).▪ Streetlighting & Traffic Signals – Focussed on the emissions from electricity use to power streetlighting and traffic signals operated by the council.

plan_name: Estrategia de mitigación y adaptación al cambio climático
plan_comments: Las fuentes de financiación e instrumentos financieros para financiar el plan son potenciales, aún se está trabajando en la definición de ellas.

El año 2018-2019 se trabaja en los diagnosticos locales para los riesgos y amenzas del clima en el territorio de Renca, con muy pocos datos duros disponibles a nivel local. Al año 2024, se debiera de contar con un nuevo instrumento que integre los datos que se han ido levantando desde el año 2019 a la fecha e involucrar los objetivos de la ley marco de cambio climático recienemente públicado en el diario oficial. Asi mismo, se ha adquirido desde el municipio el compromiso por la carbonoeutralidad y la elaboración de uan estrategia que guía la implementaación de una com,una cero carbono.

La elaboración de la estrategia energética local, ha tenido 2 hitos, el primero consistió en un proceso el año 2017Objetivos específicos:1) Realizar el diagnóstico energético de la comuna, identificando la cobertura del suministro, el consumo actual y sus fuentes, la demanda no cubierta, la proyección de la demanda y las brechas de cobertura, acceso y calidad de la energía.2) Implementar durante la elaboración de la Estrategia Energética Local un proceso inclusivo y participativo con los actores claves de la comuna de Renca.3) Estimar el potencial de generación de energía renovable y de eficiencia energética en la comuna, con el objetivo que ésta sea energéticamente más independiente.4) Definir una visión, objetivos y metas que permita al municipio trazar un plan de acción para el desarrollo energético, de manera participativa y con incidencia.5) Definir las acciones en cuanto a la implementación de programas y proyectos concretos para impulsar un desarrollo energético local equitativo y sostenible y alcanzar los objetivos y metas definidos en el punto anterior.El año 2021, se evalúa nuevamente las acciones propuestas en metodología participativa, según los lineamientos del Ministerio de Energía y se actualizan las lineas de acción el resultado de ésto se denomina "Fichas plan de acción ENERGIA - Renca", "Informe Sello Comuna Energética Renca" e "20220531_Inventario emisiones Renca FINAL"  adjuntos, lamentablemente aún no esta actualizada la Estrategia Energética Local (EEL) con los nuevos criterios y fichas para contar con un documento unificado.

EJES ESTRATÉGICOS1. RENCA ORGULLOSA: Fortalecer la identidad renquina a partir de aquello que verdaderamente nos representa. Una comuna orgullosa tiene también gran capacidad de diálogo y cooperación, para avanzar hacia el futuro considerando nuestras distintas visiones y aportes.2. RENCA CRECE: Serán presentadas iniciativas que promuevan una mayor prosperidad compartida para todos, por medio de un modelo de crecimiento y gestión que haga un mejoramiento concreto de la calidad de vida comunal.3. RENCA PARTICIPA: Medidas que apuntan a recuperar la importancia de los renquinos y renquinas en el municipio, a través de su inclusión real y efectiva en instancias de discusión,  consulta y participación colectiva.Principios rectores del PLADECO de Renca:  El Plan de Acción del PLADECO de Renca 2020-2024 se funda en principio que guían e inspiran la gestión municipal. Estos principios, detallados a continuación, son transversales a todas las áreas de desarrollo del Plan de Acción, como las nociones fundamentales que guían el espíritu de cada una de las iniciativas formuladas mas adelante:1- Enfoque humano2- Renca equitativa3- Enfoque de género4- Renca sustentable5- Comuna participativaEl Plan de Acción para el desarrollo de Renca se a organizado en torno a cinco áreas de desarrollo:- Desarrollo Social: Niñez y adolescencia, violencia intrafamiliar, pueblos originarios, Adulto mayor, narcotráfico e inseguridad, déficit habitacional, servicios de salud.- Desarrollo Urbano: Vialidad local y urbanización, permeabilidad intercomunal, movilidad y transporte público, desarrollo urbano y participación, alumbrado público, infraestructura pública.- Desarrollo Medioambiental: Cambio climático, Cerros de Renca, Sistema de infraestructura verde, gestión sustentable de recursos, Educación ambiental.- Desarrollo institucional:  Modernización de los procedimientos institucionales, atención al vecino de calidad, gobernanza, transparencia y participación.- Desarrollo económico: Oportunidades laborales, micro y pequeño emprendimiento, innovación empresarial.

plan_name: Estrategia Comunal de Movilidad de Renca 2019-2024
plan_comments: El objetivo de la Estrategia de Movilidad tiene como foco principal abordar los desplazamientos no motorizados, priorizados de acuerdo a la pirámide invertida de preferencias de movilidad utilizada por distintas organizaciones y que pone a peatones primero. Es necesario hacer más caminable la comuna, ya que permite fomentar aquello que naturalmente ya ocurre y ponerlo en valor como un medio de transporte eficiente y sustentable, además de mejorar la accesibilidad de los espacios públicos para todos, incluyendo grupos específicos que muchas veces son olvidados al momento de planificar, como niñas y niños, adultos mayores, personas con discapacidad, entre otros. Esto porque al hacer la ciudad más accesible para estas personas es más accesible para todas, democratizando el acceso a espacios público y elevar el estándar de calidad de los mismos.  Por otra parte, esta estrategia enmarca en los tres ejes de esta administración municipal: Renca Crece, Renca Orgullosa y Renca Participa, que buscan entregar un sello a los proyectos realizados. Por ello es que se fomentan proyectos donde la comunidad sea parte del proceso, se involucre en los cambios y se sienta orgullosa de la construcción conjunta de una mejor calidad de vida en la comuna. Este documento se refiere a un aspecto fundamental para la calidad de vida, en tanto la movilidad es una actividad cotidiana, que recoge aquellos desplazamientos realizados con fines laborales o de estudios, como instancias de recreación, deporte, familiares, entre otros. Una comuna más amigable para movilizarse, es una comuna con mejores espacios públicos, mejores condiciones de salud, más sostenible y por ende, mejor calidad de vida.A partir de esto, se han definido los siguientes objetivos específicos:﹣ Fomentar el uso de modos de transporte no motorizados o colectivos. ﹣ Reconocer las necesidades de movilidad de las distintas personas. ﹣ Generar un sistema de movilidad seguro para todas y todos

Por su parte, el Criterio de Desarrollo Sustentable fue definido sobre la base del PLADECO vigente y en coherencia con los objetivos ambientales:Desarrollo urbano sostenible centrado en la calidad de vida de las personas, que potencia el rol residencial de la comuna, atrayendo servicios y considerando la escala de los barrios y el valor de los cerros de Renca, mientras que resguarda la mixtura de usos de suelo residenciales y productivos con una intensidad de uso acorde a su capacidad de carga.Los Factores Críticos de Decisión (FCD) fueron definidos tras un proceso de identificación de temas prioritarios para el cual se analizaron diversas variables y las relaciones entre ellas, los cuales combinados con los objetivos ambientales dieron lugar a cuatro FCD:FCD1: Mixtura de ocupación de suelos. Incorpora el análisis de la diversidad de usos, la gestión del patrimonio cultural construido, la exposición a amenazas naturales e incendios, y el suelo sin desarrollo inmobiliario (donde se aborda la problemática de los microbasurales).FCD2: Disposición de infraestructura verde. Aborda la distribución, cobertura y accesibilidad a áreas verdes, así como la consolidación de los Cerros de Renca como área verde.FCD3: Calidad ambiental. Involucra la contaminación por fuentes fijas (industrial), contaminación de fuentes móviles, y el impacto de la termoeléctrica.FCD4: Impactos de los grandes sistemas de movilidad. Incluye el impacto de las autopistas, del Metro de Santiago, del proyecto Tren Santiago-Batuco, y del proyecto Túnel Lo Ruiz.

El Plan Regulador Comunal de Renca es del año 1984 (publicado D.O. feb 1985), desde entonces, han cambiado muchas cosas en la comuna y también en el resto de la ciudad, por lo que es necesario hacer una nueva propuesta que se adapte a los tiempos actuales.Todo el sector poniente de la comuna, desde Avenida Condell hasta la Autopista Vespucio Norte (el límite de Renca con la comuna de Pudahuel), solo está regulado por el Plan Regulador Metropolitano de Santiago. Necesitamos un Plan Regulador Comunal que abarque la comuna completa.Además, el Plan Regulador Comunal se hizo antes de que hubiera autopistas urbanas concesionadas en Santiago, varias de las cuales pasan por los bordes de la comuna, antes de que existiera Transantiago (actual RED o RMM) y antes de que volviera la demanda por vivir en comunas céntricas de la ciudad. A esto se suma que llegarán varios proyectos a la comuna como la Línea 7 del Metro o el Tren Santiago - Batuco. Todas estas iniciativas requieren que se realice una revisión del desarrollo urbano de Renca, para planificar la comuna que queremos y definir cómo lidiar con la mayor demanda por vivir en la comuna, por poder desplazarse dentro de ella y hacia el resto de la ciudad de manera adecuada y contar con espacios públicos y servicios de calidad.

plan_name: Actualización del Plan de Desarrollo y Ordenamiento Territorial del cantón Lago Agrio
plan_comments: El cantón es reconocido nacional e internacionalmente por su abundante riqueza natural,  donde aún prevalece el bosque nativo como la principal cobertura del suelo. Estos bosques primarios poseen flora y especies faunísticas silvestres, en algunos casos en condición de endemismo, que requieren un cuidado primordial para que no se vean afectadas por la industria petrolera, una de las mayores causas de contaminación ambiental en el cantón; por la tala y el comercio ilegal de especies, operada por una cruel cadena de tráfico, comercio, transportación y venta; y por las actividades productivas que cada vez ganan terreno con el intenso avance de la frontera agrícola, amenazando gravemente algunas especies maderables y animales. La deforestación es de los problemas ambientales más preocupantes del cantón, con estadísticas que bordean las 95.000 hectáreas de bosques talados en los últimos 28 años con  promedio de pérdida de 3.300 ha/año. Las causas de la deforestación son varias, pero se destaca la ausencia de una adecuada planificación y zonificación del territorio, donde se establezcan las formas de uso y ocupación del suelo; el avance de la frontera agrícola unido al desconocimiento de buenas prácticas agrícolas, la presencia de un modelo económico basado en la dependencia hacia las actividades petroleras, las mismas que se realizan con un alto costo ambiental y la expansión no organizada de las zonas urbanas. En este aspecto, hay que resaltar la importancia de la declaratoria de áreas protegidas en el cantón, tanto de aquellas que son gestionadas por la autoridad ambiental competente, como por las iniciativas privadas que buscan conservar los boques nativos a cambio de una retribución económica que ayuda al sustento familiar. Sin duda, estas áreas son parte de las potencialidades que ofrece el cantón; sin embargo, requieren de una adecuada administración para evitar la deforestación y la pérdida de biodiversidad,  en su interior, como en las zonas de amortiguamiento. En el Programa Ciudades Intermedias Sostenibles, del cual el GAD Municipal de Lago Agrio es parte, dentro de estos estudios, los riesgos relacionados con el cambio climático son las altas temperaturas, que en un escenario referencial al 2040 presenta una anomalía estimada de 0,5 a 0,6°C, la misma que cubre el 69,10% de superficie del cantón, afectando en gran medida a sus parroquias.

plan_comments: Climate Village Program (Proklim or Program Kampung Iklim) is a nationally mandate program which facilitates a community scale initiative on joint adaptation and mitigation action. Proklim is conducted by exploring community’s strengths and characteristics which can be maximized to tacke climate change issues within the community. InTambrauw, Proklim has been used to facilitate local initiative to conserve the environment (forest and mangrove) as well as in preserving the habitat of Penyu Belimbing (Dermochelys coriacea). Through the Proklim initiative, Tambrauw’s Environmental Agency gives capacity building to the communities and providing them with tools that can help them to implement their local inititive in preserving the environment

Indigenous Peoples in Tambrauw regency play important role in preserving the environment as their traditional ecological knowledge has been proven since time immemorial in sustainably managing the environment. They can fulliff their subsistence needs without causing too much stress to the environment. Tambrauw regency has made a progressive step in recognizing indigenous peoples by enacting the Local Regulation No.  6/2018. This policy gives legal basis for indigenous peoples to manage the environment per their customary laws and institutional arrangements and this plan is intended to support that goal. Moreover, the empowerment of indigenous peoples is also important to achive the vision as Conservation Regency (Kabupaten Konservasi) as laid out on the Local Regulation No. 5/2018. Indigenous peoples are the original stewards of the land, thus, they have more knowledge that more suitable with the biophysics characteristic of Tambrauw in sustainably managing the environment. The plan has both adaptation and mitigation aspect. The adaptation aspcet in incresing the resiliency of Indigenous Peoples in facing threats posed by climate change while the mitigation aspect can be achived by preserving the forest intact as the result from sustainable forest management per Indigenous poeples’ customary law

Irá produzir biogás e biofertilizante através do aproveitamento de esterco e matéria orgânica oriunda da bovinocultura e pecuária dos beneficiários e contribuir com a redução de emissão de gases do efeito estufa.. Os dois biodigestores já construído tem a capacidade de oferecer o equivalente à um e meio botijão de gás para o consumo em média de uma família com 5 pessoas. Além de produzir aproximadamente 600 litros/ciclo de biofertilizante para aplicação nas lavouras e unidades produtivas dos beneficiários. Busca-se construir mais 50 biodigestores.

Esta é uma ferramenta de combate à fome e garantia de segurança alimentar e nutricional de famílias em vulnerabilidade social, através de práticas sustentáveis, aproveitamento de alimentos, subprodutos e promoção da alimentação saudável.  Trará Fortalecimento da agricultura familiar para produção de alimentos com técnicas agroecológicas sustentáveis, aproveitamento de alimentos que seriam descartados,  higienização, processamento, distribuição e mitigação da fome de famílias vulneráveis e garantia da segurança alimentar e nutricional dos beneficiários. Acesso das famílias agricultores as a compras institucionais através do PAA e PNAE.Trabalhar a transversalidade através do banco com as secretarias de Assistência social, educação e saúde.O banco de alimentos conta com hortas agroecológicas para retro-alimentação das cestas distribuídas. Após a seleção e processamento dos alimentos recebidos, o resíduo orgânico é compostado e utilizado para fertilização das hortas. A unidade trabalha com lixo zero, fazendo aproveitamento integral do alimento recebido e devolvendo ao sistema alimentos saudáveis, livres de fertilizantes sintéticos e agrotóxicos.O banco através da horta trabalhará a educação Ambiental e saúde

Tem como objetivo proteger a diversidade biológica, disciplinar o processo de ocupação através de zoneamento e assegurar a sustentabilidade do uso dos recursos naturais no encontro do Rio Carinhanha como Rio São Francisco.A área está no encontro do Rio Carinhanha com Rio São Francisco. E o crescimento Urbano desordenado no local promoverá a degradação dos recursos naturais, quanto da fauna e flora. Outro ponto importante a destacar é o fato do local ser passível de inundações pelo Rio São Francisco, o que pode trazer consequências danosas ao município.

The province has formulated adaptive and mitigative measures that are both robust and tailored to its unique geographical and socio-economic characteristics. These measures include infrastructure improvements, land-use planning adjustments, sustainable agriculture practices, enhanced disaster preparedness, and the promotion of renewable energy sources.
